/pəˈsɛnt/, /pɚˈsɛnt/ Suffix ( speedrunning ) Suffix forming speedrunning categories requiring a given percentage to qualify. ( speedrunning ) Suffix forming speedrunning categories related to the suffixed element, or subject to constraints based on it. Back-formation from any% or low% , in which the “%” was a literal indicator of the percentage completion the player would achieve in order to qualify for the given leaderboard category. The suffix was then broadened to form category names ...

Conjunction Abbreviation of and . ( dated ) Abbreviation of Latin et . Usage Note The ampersand is often used if the words to the left and right together form one inseparable unit, for example plug & play. Occurrences of & in proper names are considered part of the name itself, and are almost always reproduced even in contexts where ampersands are not normally used. In formal use, not all ands are replaceable with an ampersand. For example and linking two main clauses or appearing before the las...

/ænd lɪt/ Noun (crosswording, sometimes attributive) A cryptic crossword clue that can be interpreted in its entirety as either a definition or wordplay; for example, "God incarnate, essentially" for "Odin", a Norse god whose name is hidden in "G od in carnate". From & + clipping of literal or literally , referring to the fact that the clue both forms a traditional cryptic clue and can be read literally as a straight clue. /d/ Verb Contraction of had , as an auxiliary verb. Contraction of had , as a main verb, but not a phrasal verb. Contraction of would or should . Synonym: 'ld ( colloquial ) Contraction of did . Usage Note Usually a representation of informal speech, either quoted dialogue or as used in informal writing. Most frequently used with pronouns ( I'd , he'd , she'd etc.), but potentially with almost any everyday noun, as well as with personal names. Compare -'d . Contraction of would or had .

/əm/ Pronoun ( now colloquial ) Them (now only in unstressed position). From earlier hem , from Middle English hem , from Old English heom (“them”, dative) of hie , originally a dative plural form but in Middle English coming to serve as an accusative plural as well. From -s (suffix forming regular plurals of nouns) and ( ) (encloses optional variants or variant elements). /æk/ Suffix One affected with. Of, belonging to. Used to form the names of nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) that are derivatives of acetic acid. From French -acque , from New Latin -acus , from Ancient Greek -ακός (-akós, “-ic”). Related to -ic and -y . From acetic acid, which is used in the synthesis of many of these compounds.

/ək.l̩/ Suffix Used to form an adjective from a noun. Usage Note Most adjectives ending in -acal are formed by adding -al onto an existing word ending in -ac rather than using this suffix. From -ac +‎ -al , probably on the model of -ical beside -ic .

Suffix A unit or set (especially of a specified number). (medicine, anatomy) toward From Ancient Greek -άς (-ás) (genitive -άδος (-ádos)): a suffix forming feminine nouns. Derived from Latin ad (“at”), from Proto-Italic *ad . Coined by British anatomist John Barclay.

Suffix Used to form nouns denoting action, or a person performing said action. Indicating a drink made from a given fruit. Used to form collectives; see -ad . Borrowed from Spanish -ado , from Latin -āta , feminine form of -ātus , suffix used to create adjectives and nouns.
Suffix ( slang ) A suffix used to create adjectives imparting a specific form of coolness, verve, or trippiness. From psychedelic .

/æ(ː)/, /ɑ(ː)/ Suffix Used to intensify an adjective, especially in a mocking or taunting manner. Usage Note Used after an adjective or a phrase (typically in quotation marks) that acts as one. Either hyphenated or spaced. Respelling of -ass representing a pronunciation with elision of the final consonant, which can occur in African-American Vernacular English. Compare bih and shi or shii . Suffix ( sometimes hyperbolic ) One whose wealth exceeds a specific number of units in the local currency. Rebracketing of millionaire , which was, in turn, borrowed from French millionnaire .

/æl/, /əl/ Suffix Of or pertaining to. Adjectival suffix appended to various words, often nouns, to produce an adjective form. Often added to words of Latin origin, but used with other words also. Suffix Obsolete spelling of -al .

/-əns/ Suffix Added to an adjective or verb to form a noun indicating a state or condition, such as result or capacity, associated with the verb. From Middle English -aunce , -ance , from Anglo-Norman -aunce and continental Old French -ance , from Latin -ant- , -ent- + -ia , from stem of Latin -āns , -ēns .

/ənsi/ Suffix Added to verbs and adjectives to form nouns conveying a condition or quality associated with the verb. From Latin -antia , -entia , with influence from French -ance , French -ancie , Spanish -ancia , etc.

/-ˈeɪ.nɪ.əs/ Suffix Found in adjectives from Latin. From Latin -āneus , with influence from -ous (from Latin -ōsus ) although the two suffixes are not etymologically related.

Suffix forms medical terms for the onset of certain changes in puberty From Ancient Greek ἀρχή (arkhḗ, “beginning”). First used in menarche .
/ɑɹ.ki/, /ɑː.ki/, /ə.ki/, /əɹ.ki/ Suffix form of government or rule From Latin -archia , from Ancient Greek -αρχίᾱ (-arkhíā), from ἀρχή (arkhḗ, “rule, government”).

Suffix Someone who is in a specified condition (“pejorative agent suffix”). From Middle English -ard , from Old French -ard (suffix), from Frankish *-hard (“hardy, bold”), from Proto-Germanic *harduz (“hard”). Cognate with Middle High German -hart . More at hard.
Suffix Used to form an adjective meaning "of or pertaining to" from nouns ending in -arium . By surface analysis, -ari(um) +‎ -al .

Suffix A believer in something. An advocate of something. ( uncommon ) A native or inhabitant of somewhere. Used to form an adjective meaning "of or pertaining to" from nouns ending in -arium . Usage Note Most English words ending in -arian are not derived in English using this suffix. Most often -an or -ian is added to words formed in Latin using -arius or to a stem ending in "ar". For examples, see barbarian , nonagenarian , Rastafarian , Hungarian , and Trinitarian . Back-formation from vario...
Suffix Adjective suffix denoting "of or pertaining to" that correspond to -ary words. From -ary + -ic
Suffix Used to form an adjective meaning "action, process, result, or ideology" from adjectives and nouns ending in -ary . By surface analysis, -ar(y) +‎ -ism .
Suffix Agent suffix denoting "one who does or believes" that correspond to -ary words. From -ary + -ist

/-ˈa(ː)(ɾ).iː.um/, /-ˈeə̯ɹ.iː.əm/, /-ˈeɹ.i.əm/, /-ˈeɾ.i.əm/, /-ˈeɾ.i.ʌm/, /-ˈeːɹ.iː.əm/, /-ˈeːɾ.iː.um/, /-ˈɛə̯ɹ.iː.əm/, /-ˈɛɹ.i.əm/, /-ˈɛːɹ.iː.əm/ Suffix A place associated with a specified thing. Synonym: -orium A device associated with a specified function. Usage Note Care should be taken to distinguish occurrences of -arium from occurrences of -ium . For instance: element names (such as barium and samarium ) have the -ium suffix. Learned borrowing from Latin -ārium . Doublet of -ary .

Suffix Used to form verbs from nouns or adjectives ending in -ary . Etymology treeProto-Italic *-āzios Latin -ārius der.Middle English -arie English -ar(y) Proto-Indo-European *-id- Proto-Indo-European *-yéti Proto-Indo-European *-idyéti Proto-Hellenic *-íďďō Ancient Greek -ῐ́ζω (-ĭ́zō)bor.Late Latin -izō der.Middle French -iser bor.Middle English -isen English -ize English -arize From -ar(y) + -ize .
/ə.ɹɒl/, /əˌɹɔl/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of dicoumarol derivatives used as anticoagulants. From dicoumarol .
/ə.ɹəʊn/, /əˌɹoʊn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of antiarrhythmics.
Suffix Embellished form of -aroo.
/ˈa.ɹə.tiːn/, /ˈɛɹ.əˌtiːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of arotinoid derivatives. From carotene .
Suffix Forms names of speech impairments, especially relating to the ordering of words. From Ancient Greek ἄρθρον (árthron, “joint, articulation”) + -ia .

/ə.ɹi/, /ɛɹ.i/, /ɹi/ Suffix Of or pertaining to. Adjectival suffix appended to various words, often nouns, to produce an adjective form. Often added to words of Latin origin, but used with other words also. Synonyms: -al, -ar, -ese, -ic, -id, -ish, -like, -oid, -ory, -ous, -y ( nonproductive ) Ending of some substantives borrowed or inherited from Latin and French. ( mathematics ) Having the specified arity. Synonym: -adic Inherited from Middle English -arie , a back-formation from Latin and Fre...
/-ˌes/, /-ˌez/, /-ˌeɪs/, /-ˌeɪz/, /-ˌeːs/, /-ˌeːz/, /-ˌæɪs/, /-ˌæɪz/ Suffix Used to form the names of enzymes. Derived from ending of diastase ; a libfix.
Suffix (biochemistry, pharmacology) Used to form names of generic corticosteroid drugs.

Suffix Used to intensify an adjective. Used to convert an adjective into a noun for a person who has that trait. Resembling. Partially from ass , and partially from a corruption or simplification of assed .
Suffix someone associated with something From Latin -asta , from Ancient Greek -αστής (-astḗs), from -άζω (-ázō, verbal suffix) +‎ -τής (-tḗs, agent-noun suffix).
Suffix Used to form diminutive and pejorative nouns, labeling someone pretending to be what they are not. Learned borrowing from Latin -aster (“little, petty, partial, incomplete”).
/æstɪk/ Suffix Used to form adjectives from nouns, often with the meaning meaning "of, or relating to". Equivalent to -ast +‎ -ic
/əs.vɪə/, /əsˌvɪɹ/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of hepatitis C virus NS5A inhibitors used as antivirals. From [Term?] +‎ -vir (“antiviral”).
Suffix Used to form formal plurals of certain Greek-derived words ending '-a', such as thema/themata, stigma/stigmata, schema/schemata.

/eɪt/, /ət/ Suffix forms adjectives with meaning "having the specified thing" Synonyms: -ed, -ous forms adjectives with meaning "characterized by the specified thing" forms adjectives with meaning "resembling the specified thing" ending of Latin- (ending in -ātus ) and French-borrowed (ending in -é ) participial adjectives ( obsolete ) up until Early Modern English, formed regular past participles of verbs ending in -ate . Rare afterwards except in archaizing poetry, religious writing and Scotla...
Suffix Contest Etymology treeProto-Indo-European *h₂weh₁- der.Proto-Indo-European *-trom der.Ancient Greek ἆθλον (âthlon)bor.English -athlon Borrowed from Ancient Greek ἆθλον (âthlon, “contest”).

Suffix An event, usually collaborative, characterized by intense activity, effort, and time spent in connection to a specific theme or goal. A such event focused on attracting fundraising for charity. Rebracketing of marathon .
/-eɪtɪm/ Suffix Changes a component into an adverb meaning "by that component." Borrowed from Latin -ātim .
/ˈe.ʃən/, /ˈeɪ̯.ʃən/, /ˈeː.ʃon/, /ˈæɪ̯.ʃən/, /ˈɛjʃən/ Suffix An action or process. The result of an action or process. A state or quality. By surface analysis, -ate +‎ -ion .
Suffix of, related to, or associated with the thing specified
Suffix Used to form agent nouns, usually from verbs that have the ending -ate. Borrowed from Latin -ātor or -ate +‎ -or .
Suffix forms adjectives of, relating to, or connected with the thing specified forms nouns that represent things or places where a specified activity takes place From -ate + -ory .
Infix ( pharmacology ) a hybrid monoclonal antibody, derived from both rat and murine sources From -a- (“rat”) +‎ x (“cross”) +‎ -o- (“mouse”).
Suffix Used in the game of Pig Latin.
Suffix (Toronto, Southeastern-Ontario) Used to mark or derive certain nouns from verbs, adjectives, and other nouns.
/ə.zæm/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of diazepam derivatives. Contraction of -azepam or -azolam .
/ˈæz.ə.nɪl/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of benzodiazepine derivatives used as benzodiazepine receptor antagonists and agonists. From (benzodi)aze(pi)n(e) +‎ -il ( of unknown origin ).
/ˈaz.ɪ.pam/, /ˈeɪ.zɪ.pam/, /ˈæz.əˌpæm/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of diazepam derivatives.
/ˈaz.ɪ.peɪt/, /ˈeɪ.zɪ.peɪt/, /ˈæz.əˌpeɪt/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of diazepam derivatives. Blend of -azepam +‎ carboxylate .
/ˈaz.ə(ʊ).siːn/, /ˈæz.əˌsin/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of narcotic antagonists/agonists related to 6,7-benzomorphan. From benzazocine .
Suffix (organic chemistry, pharmacology) Used to form names of generic antidepressant drugs.
/ˈeɪ.zə(ʊ).læm/, /ˈæz.əˌlæm/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of diazepam derivatives. Blend of azole +‎ -azepam .
/ˈeɪ.zəs.ɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of prazosin derivatives used as antihypertensive substances. From prazosin .
/ˈbæ/ Infix ( pharmacology ) A shortened allomorph of the monoclonal antibody affix -bac-, used before the affixes -xi- and -zu- for ease of pronunciation.
/ˈbæk/, /ˈbæs/ Infix ( pharmacology ) a monoclonal antibody that targets a bacterium From bacteria .
/ˈbæk.tæm/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of β-lactamase inhibitors. Contraction of beta-lactamase .
Suffix ( Internet slang ) Forms nouns which refer to acts of manipulation which seek to elicit a particular response. From bait .

Suffix (chiefly UK, soccer) Refers to styles of football. (slang, usually derogatory, somewhat humorous) forms nouns with the meaning "one who is characterized by". (slang, usually derogatory, somewhat humorous) forms adjectives with the meaning "characterized by". Back-formation from football . Journalist Jamie Braidwood suggests it may have been inspired by Michael Lewis' 2003 book Moneyball and its 2011 film adaptation. Likely from figurative use of bal...
/bə.meɪt/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of propanediol and pentanediol derivatives used as tranquilizers. Clipping of carbamate .

Suffix Added to a stem to form a patronymic or matronymic surname. ( usually humorous ) Forms nouns defining large agglomerations of a particular substance or quality. Borrowed from German -berg . Back-formation from iceberg .
/ˈbeɪ.təˌsɔl/, /ˈbiː.tə.sɒl/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of prednisone and prednisolone derivatives. Probably from beta(metha)s(one) +‎ -ol .
Suffix Combined with a descriptor conveying a characteristic (such as skill, experience, or social position) to form words for people. From newbie .

/ˈbaɪ.ɑnt/, /ˈbaɪ.ɒnt/ Suffix ( biology ) Forms nouns for a discrete living organism that has a specified mode of living. Probably from German, modification of Ancient Greek βῐούντ- (bĭoúnt-), βιῶν (biôn, “living”), present participle of βῐοῦν (bĭoûn, “to live”), from βῐ́ος (bĭ́os, “mode of life”).
Suffix Related to bionts. biont +‎ -ic
Suffix ( biology ) A specified way of living. By back-formation from the earliest relevant terms such as symbiosis (which see).

Suffix Used to form adjectives describing a method of living. Used to form nouns describing organisms having a specified method of living. From Ancient Greek βιωτικός (biōtikós, “of life”), from βίος (bíos, “life”).
Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form generic names of barbiturate drugs.
Suffix an immature cell or tissue

Suffix Having a specified number or type of elements that go on to form other bodies. Of or relating to an immature cell or tissue. Etymology treeAncient Greek βλᾰστός (blăstós)bor.English -blast Proto-Italic *-ikos Latin -icus lbor.Old French -ique bor.Middle English -ik English -ic English -blastic From -blast + -ic . Etymology treeAncient Greek βλᾰστᾰ́νω (blăstắnō)Proto-Indo-European *-tós Proto-Hellenic *-tós Ancient Greek -τός (-tós)Ancient Greek βλᾰστός (blăstós)bor.English -bl...
Infix (UK, Australia, slang) An intensifier.
/-blə/, /-bləɹ/ Suffix ( Internet slang ) Combined with a noun or adjective for a specific community of users or a genre of posts on the social media platform Tumblr. Clipping of Tumblr .
Suffix carried or spread by From borne , past participle of bear .
Suffix Synonym of -borough . From boro , from Old English burh (“fortified place”).

/-b(ə)ɹoʊ/, /-bɘɹɘ/, /-bəɹə/, /-bɹɘ/, /-bɹə/ Suffix Placename suffix, indicated site of a fortified place. Placename suffix, indicating a borough. From borough , from Old English burh (“fortified place”).
Suffix automatic system, computer program or machine From bot , a clipping of robot .
Suffix Moving or travelling towards. Confined or restricted to a certain place; unable to move in certain conditions; -ridden From bound (“obliged to, certain to”).
/bɹə.diːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of bradycardic agents. From brad(ycardic) +‎ -ine .
Suffix Synonym of -burgh . From Old English burg (“fortified place”).

Suffix Used to make the names of hamburgers served in buns with the addition of the specified foodstuff, or with that foodstuff used in place of the usual meat. Used to make the names of patties made from or containing the specified foodstuff. (US, sports) Used to emphasise a high number of points scored by a player or team. Rebracketing of hamburger , as if it were ham + burger .
Suffix Place name suffix, indicating the site of a fortified place. From Old English burh (“fortified place”). Doublet of -borough and -bury .

/-bɘɹi/, /-bəɹɪ/, /-bɹi/ Suffix A placename suffix indicating a fortified place. From Old English byriġ , the dative case of burg (“fortified place”) (whence borough); doublet of -borough and -burg . Due to the collapse of the case system between Old and Middle English, many placenames retain a fossilized dative form, as places would most commonly have been invoked in the dative (after a preposition in , at , to etc.).
/ˈbju.təˌɹɔl/, /ˈbjuː.tə.ɹɒl/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of phenethylamine derivatives used as bronchodilators. From (tert-)but(yl) +‎ -terol .
/bjəˌvɪɹ/, /bjʊ.vɪə/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of RNA polymerase (NS5B) inhibitors used as antivirals. From [Term?] +‎ -vir (“antiviral”).
Suffix ( in place names ) Habitation. Ultimately from Old Norse býr (“settlement”).
Suffix procession Back-formed from cavalcade , inferred to be a suffix

Suffix (organic chemistry, pharmacology) A synthetic alkaloid used as a local anesthetic. From German -kain , extracted from the word Kokain (“cocaine”), because cocaine was formerly used as a local anaesthetic.
Suffix Forming nouns relating to a bending or curvature. From Ancient Greek κάμψις (kámpsis).
/kəp.əʊn/, /kəˌpoʊn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of catechol- O -methyltransferase inhibitors. From tolcapone .

Suffix ( cardiology ) Forms nouns relating to a heart condition. Learned borrowing from Ancient Greek κᾰρδῐ́ᾱ (kărdĭ́ā, “heart”) +‎ -ῐ́ᾱ (-ĭ́ā, suffix forming abstract nouns), equivalent to cardia +‎ -ia .
Suffix Used to name a plan that provides health care services.
/ˈkɑɹ.nɪl/, /ˈkɑː.nɪl/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of carboline derivatives used as benzodiazepine receptor antagonists and agonists. From car(boli)n(e) +‎ -il ( of unknown origin ).
Suffix Part of a fruit or fruiting body. From Ancient Greek καρπός (karpós, “fruit, grain”).
Suffix Having a specified number or type of fruit or fruiting bodies or part thereof. From -carp +‎ -ous . From Ancient Greek καρπός (karpós, “fruit, grain”).
Suffix Forming words for audiovisual content distributed to an audience, usually in a manner denoted by the word stem. Back-formation from broadcast .
/ˈkeɪʃən/ Suffix (informal, sometimes humorous) Forms a more specific type of vacation, and sometimes ad-hoc nonce words. [from late 2000s] Back-formation from vacation ; a libfix.
/kə.vɪə/, /kəˌvɪɹ/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of carbocyclic nucleosides used as antivirals. From ca(rbocyclic) +‎ -vir (“antiviral”).

/k/ Suffix (Internet slang, humorous) Used to replace "ck" and some similar endings at the end of a word or syllable. Does not alter the meaning (apart from making words jocular), nor the pronunciation. The exact origin of this suffix is unknown. Currently, it is used to bypass profanity filters, by the Crips street gang in the United States to avoid writing "ck" (as "ck" could stand for "Crip Killer"), emphasize the "k" sound, and sexualize certain words (e.g. thicc , the most popular word ...
Suffix Denoting a cappuccino-like beverage with another specified ingredient (a cherry-ccino with cherries) or purpose ( puppuccino for puppies). From cappuccino and various blends with cappuccino .
Suffix (after 1 , 2 , or 3 ) Times: used to form a multiplicative numeral from a cardinal numeral.

Suffix ( incel slang ) Denoting a type of incel (often through a stereotype). (Internet slang, loosely, ironic) Used tongue-in-cheek for a wide range of effects unrelated to romance. A clipping derived from incel . A highly-productive suffix; compare the more offensive -fag , which may have partly inspired it. First attested in c. late 2010s.

Suffix ( pathology ) A tumour. Synonym: -oma ( pathology ) A hernia. Derived from Ancient Greek κήλη (kḗlē, “tumour, hernia”). Etymology treeProto-Indo-European *kh₂ewl- der.Proto-Indo-European *kh₂u-l-eh₂ Ancient Greek κήλη (kḗlē)der.English -cele Derived from Ancient Greek κήλη (kḗlē, “tumour, hernia”). See -coel .
Suffix ( geology ) Related to the geologic era called the Cenozoic, the current one. From Cenozoic . From Ancient Greek καινός (kainós, “new”).
Suffix ( medicine ) Puncture and aspiration of From Ancient Greek κέντησις (kéntēsis, “the act of pricking”), from κεντέω (kentéō, “I prick”).

/ˈsɛntrɪk/ Suffix Having a specified number of centres. Having a specified object at the centre, or as the focus of attention. Representing a combining form of Ancient Greek κεντρικός (kentrikós, “central”). Cognate with Bengali -কেন্দ্রিক (-kendrik)
/ˈsɛntrɪzm/ Suffix A focus on, or belief in the superiority of, one culture, people, place, or other thing. Back-formation from -centric .
Suffix (type or number of) head
Suffix ( anatomy ) head From Ancient Greek κεφαλή (kephalḗ).
Suffix (medicine, pathology) head From French -céphalie , German -zephalie , or Latin -cephalia , from Ancient Greek κεφαλή (kephalḗ, “head”) and a suffix: French -ie , German -ie , or Latin -ia .

Suffix Neural reception. ( slang ) Combined with a noun to indicate a layering, nesting, or recursion of the referent. Back-formed from words such as nociception and proprioception . Rebracketing of inception coined with reference to the 2010 science fiction film of the same title, the plot of which involves a team of people infiltrating someone's subconscious mind, proceeding through several layers of dreams in the process.
Suffix Forms names of specific neurological receptors. Abstracted from nociceptor and proprioceptor .
Suffix Pertaining to a tail-like structure. From New Latin cercus , from Ancient Greek κέρκος (kérkos, “tail”).
/siːz/ Suffix Used to form the irregular plural form of Latin-derived nouns ending in -x: Derived from the pluralization pattern of Latin third declension nouns.

/-sest/, /-sɛ(ː)sʈ/, /-sɛst/ Suffix A suffix combined with a noun or surname to create a word for incest or an inappropriate relationship involving the referent(s). (dysphemistic, humorous) A suffix for relationships between participants in the same (usually close-knit) activity (see castcest, classcest, clubcest, coursecest, friendcest, teamcest, workcest) or residents of the same complex (see dormcest, floorcest, hallcest, housecest). Rebracketing of incest .
Suffix Forms placenames, originally those settled by the Roman Empire in Great Britain. From Old English ċeaster , from Latin castrum

/tʃ(j)æ/, /tʃ(j)ə/ Usage Note Sometimes written as a separate word ( cha ). From an unstressed you (/jə/) after a word ending in /t/; the unstressed sequence /tj/ coalesces into /tʃ/ ⟨ch⟩ in many accents. Compare di dja .

Suffix ( anime and manga fandom ) Appended to a person's name (usually a female, child, a close friend, or an intimate) to add politeness. It is sometimes used to denote cuteness or familiarity. ( Internet slang ) Used in the names of imageboards, usually ones that try to emulate 4chan. From the Japanese familiar suffix ちゃん (-chan). In reference to 4chan, in turn from Futaba Channel (cf. its URL, www.2chan.net , or its alternate names ふたばちゃん and 双葉ちゃん found on its homepage); hence, derived from ...
/ˈkiːzɪə/ Suffix (literally and figuratively, forms nouns) defecation Representing the Ancient Greek χέζω (khézō, “I defecate”) + -ῐ́ᾱ (-ĭ́ā, “-ia”, suffix forming abstract nouns).
Suffix Used to form names of sciences, especially those concerning the mind, behavior, or humanity, by replacing the initial psy- with another word or prefix. Back-formation from psychology .

/koɹ/, /kɔː/ Suffix An organism, especially a plant or plant pathogen, that spreads by a specified agent, or in a specified manner. A group of habitats with a similar environment and a similar biota. From Ancient Greek χωρέω (khōréō, “I move, spread”). Backformation from biochore, from bio-+Ancient Greek χώρα (khṓra, “country”).
Suffix ( geometry ) Used to form the names of 4-dimensional solids bounded by a certain number of cells/polyhedra ( polychora ). Synonym: -hedroid From Ancient Greek χῶρον (khôron, “room”).
Suffix (biology, botany) Spread, or (especially) having seeds which are dispersed, by (some specified animal, etc)
/ˌkɔɹiː/ Suffix Method of plant or seed dispersal. From -chore + -y .
Suffix Producing or changing color in a certain way. ( rare ) Having a certain complexion. From Ancient Greek χρώς (khrṓs, “color, complexion”) + -ic .
Suffix having a specified colour various pigments
Suffix Relating to time. Ultimately from Ancient Greek χρόνος (khrónos) + -ous .
/ˈsɪ/ Infix ( pharmacology ) a shortened allomorph of the monoclonal antibody affix -cir-, used before the affixes -xi- and -zu- for ease of pronunciation
/ˈsaɪ.kloʊˌvɪɹ/, /ˈsʌɪ.klə(ʊ)ˌvɪə/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of bicyclic heterocycle compounds used as antivirals. From aciclovir .
Suffix Used to make adjectives corresponding to nouns ending in -cide .
/-saɪd/ Suffix killing killer From Middle French -cide , from Latin -cīdium (“killing”). From Middle French -cide , from Latin -cīda (“cutter, killer”), from caedō (“cut, kill”).
/ˈsɪl.ɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of generic penicillin antibiotic drugs.

Suffix bacteriocin Antibiotic substance. From English colicin , from French colicine , from coli- +‎ -cine , with ending formed by analogy from antibiotics like auréomycine, streptomycine, with second element from myc- +‎ -ine .
/ˈsɪɹ/ Infix ( pharmacology ) a monoclonal antibody that targets the cardiovascular system From circulatory.

Suffix Added to names of primarily Greek letters to indicate a phonetic phenomenon relating to the corresponding phoneme: Excessive usage of the phoneme. A sound change towards the phoneme. The retention of a phoneme when related varieties lose it. A speech disorder marked by either excessive or defective use of the phoneme. Back-formation from iotacism .
/ˈsaɪ.tə.biːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of cytarabine or azacytidine derivatives used as nucleoside antiviral or antineoplastic agents. Contraction of cytarabine .
Suffix ( mineralogy ) fracture (having a fracture of such a form) From Ancient Greek κλᾰ́σῐς (klắsĭs, “breaking, fracture”) (via French -clase ), from κλάω (kláō, “I break”).
Suffix Fragmentation, breaking Ultimately from Ancient Greek κλᾰστός (klăstós, “broken”). Doublet of -clase .
Suffix Something that breaks or destroys. From Ancient Greek κλαστός (klastós, “broken”), verbal adjective of κλάω (kláō, “to break”).
Suffix Relating to a something that breaks or destroys. -clast +‎ -ic
Suffix Closure, blockage, shutting. From Ancient Greek κλεῖσις (kleîsis).
/klɪ.diːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of muscarinic receptor agonists and antagonists. From quinuclidine or (cy)cl(o)- +‎ -idine .
/klɪˈdɪn.i.əm/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of muscarinic receptor agonists or antagonists. From -clidine +‎ -ium (“quaternary ammonium compound”).
Suffix having a specified slope From Ancient Greek κλίνω (klínō, “to lean”). Cognate with Latin clīnō .
Suffix A specified form of slope or gradation. Back-formation from -clinal .
Suffix having a specified slope ( of a crystal etc ) having a specified number of oblique axial intersections
/ˈkloʊ.fəˌnæk/, /ˈkləʊ.fən.æk/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of ibufenac derivatives used as anti-inflammatory agents. From chlo(ro)- +‎ -fenac .
Suffix shinned From New Latin cnēmis , from Ancient Greek κνήμη (knḗmē, “shin”) + -ic .
Suffix Used to construct company names that borrow meaning from the root word. Clipping of company .
Infix ( pharmacology ) a shortened allomorph of the monoclonal antibody affix -col-, used before the affixes -xi- and -zu- for ease of pronunciation
/-ˈkɑkəs/, /-ˈkɒkəs/ Suffix any spherical microorganism From New Latin coccus , from Latin coccum , from Ancient Greek κόκκος (kókkos, “grain, seed”).
Suffix A cavity. Via Latin, from Ancient Greek κοῖλος (koîlos, “hollow”). See -cele .
Suffix hollowed out, relating to concavity From Ancient Greek κοῖλος (koîlos, “hollow”).
Infix ( pharmacology ) a monoclonal antibody that targets colon tumors From colon.
Suffix Forms nouns (and occasionally adjectives) relating to organisms that inhabit a particular habitat. Borrowed from French -cole , from Latin -cola (“cultivator”).
Suffix Having a specified habitat. From Latin -col(a) (“inhabitor”) + -ous . By surface analysis, -col(e) +‎ -ous .

Suffix ( science fiction fandom slang ) Used to form the names of conventions. Noun ( chiefly anime and manga ) Forms terms for abnormal sexual attractions. Clipping of convention . Back-formation from lolicon , ultimately a clipping of complex (“abnormal mental condition”).
/ˈkɑn.əˌzoʊl/, /ˈkɒn.ə.zəʊl/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of miconazole derivatives used as systemic antifungal agents. From miconazole .

Suffix referring to a vehicle that works with one or more vertical rotors similar to that of a helicopter The clipped form copter predates it, which raises the question of compounding versus affixing, but regardless, it is now widely reanalyzed as a suffix derived from helicopter .

Suffix ( music ) Denoting a genre of music, especially one influenced by hardcore music. ( by extension ) Denoting a genre, movement, subculture, or group. ( chiefly Internet slang ) Denoting an aesthetic or vibe.
Suffix horn(s) From Latin cornū (“horn”).
/koɹt/, /kɔːt/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of corticosteroids except prednisolone derivatives. From corticosteroid .
Suffix World.
/ˈkɑk.sɪb/, /ˈkɒk.sɪb/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of selective cyclo-oxygenase inhibitors. Contraction of COX inhibitor .
/-kɹəsi/ Suffix rule (in the sense of governing). Usage Note Contrast -cracy with the far rarer, homophonous -crasy . From Ancient Greek -κρατία (-kratía), from κράτος (krátos, “power”).

Suffix A skill of a particular kind. Synonym: -work A vehicle of a particular type. A Minecraft mod or server with a particular theme. From Middle English -craft , -creft , from Old English -cræft (“art or skill in”), from cræft (“art, skill”). More at craft . Back-formation from Minecraft ; ultimately from etymology 1 above.

/-kɹəsi/ Suffix temperament, constituent pattern. Usage Note Because -crasy and -cracy are homophones, and the former is comparatively much rarer, the two suffixes are often mistakenly conflated in the latter spelling, thus giving rise to such common misspellings as idiosyncracy . Ultimately from Ancient Greek κρᾶσις (krâsis, “mixing, tempering”), often via Latin -crāsia and, additionally, Old French -crasie .

/-kɹæt/ Suffix A participant in a specified form of government. An advocate of a specified form of government. From Ancient Greek κράτος (krátos, “power, might”), modelled after words of Ancient Greek origin such as democrat and aristocrat . From Proto-Indo-European *kret- (English hard ).

/ˈkɹætɪk/ Suffix used to form adjectives, of or befitting -cracy used to form adjectives, of or befitting -crat From Middle French -cratique , and its source, Latin -craticus , from Ancient Greek -κρατικός (-kratikós), from -κρατία (-kratía, “-cracy”), from κράτος (krátos, “strength, might, rule, authority”). Equivalent to -crat +‎ -ic or -cracy +‎ -ic .
Suffix indicating a material functionally similar to concrete indicating having to do with concrete From concrete .
/kɹiːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of acridine derivatives. Contraction of acridine . Ancient Greek κρίνω (krínō, “to separate”).

Suffix ( sports ) Forms the names of sports that involve racing across an artificial cross-country course of bumps, jumps, drop-offs, troughs, curves, and such. Synonym: ( abbreviation ) -X Abbreviation of cross country .
Suffix Cryptography. Hidden. From Ancient Greek κρυπτός (kruptós, literally “hidden, concealed, private, secret”).
Suffix crystal.
Suffix Denoting things associated with, or compared to, Dracula: Count Chocula, Bunnicula etc.
Suffix ( diminutive ) Used to form diminutive nouns. Synonyms: ( suffixes ) -ee, -el, -et, -ette, -icle, -ie, -kin, -le, -let, -ling, -ole, -ule, -y, ( prefixes ) mini-, micro-, nano-

/ˈkjʊə.ɹi.əm/, /ˈkjʊɹ.i.əm/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of curare-like substances, neuromuscular blocking agents with rigid structure. From cur(are) +‎ -ium (“quaternary ammonium compound”).
/kjəˈɹoʊ.ni.əm/, /kjʊˈɹəʊ.ni.əm/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of neuromuscular blocking agents with rigid structure. From cur(are) +‎ -onium (“quaternary ammonium compound”).

/-si/, /-sɪj/ Suffix ( no longer productive ) Used to form nouns of state, condition or quality. ( no longer productive ) Used to form nouns of rank or office. From Anglo-Norman -cie , ultimately from Latin -cia , -tia , Ancient Greek -κια (-kia), -τια (-tia), originally variants of Latin -ia and Ancient Greek -ια (-ia), -ία (-ía) or -εια (-eia). Original loan words like pharmacy and papacy could have formed the model.
/ˈsaɪ.kliːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of generic tetracycline antibiotic drugs. From tetracycline .
/-ˌsajʈ/, /-ˌsaɪt/, /-ˌsɑɪt/ Suffix Used to form cell names and classifications for mature cells. From Latin cyta , from Ancient Greek κύτος (kútos, “vessel, jar”). Compare cyte .
Suffix Related to (a particular kind) of cells. -cyte +‎ -ic
Suffix Pertaining to a cytoma. From cytoma +‎ -ous or -cyte +‎ -oma +‎ -ous . By surface analysis, cyt- +‎ -omatous .

Suffix An empty suffix, perhaps derived from the past-tense suffix above, added in some dialects to the present tense forms of some words which then add an additional -ed in the past tense. Marks ordinals written in digits when the final term of the spelled number is "second" or "third"
Suffix ( chiefly zoology ) digits From French -dactyle , from Latin -dactylus , ultimately from Ancient Greek δάκτυλος (dáktulos, “finger”).
/dæn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of pimobendan derivatives used as cardiac stimulants. From pimobendan .

/-daɹ/, /-dɐː/, /-dɑɹ/, /-dɑː/, /-ɖɑː(ɾ)/ Suffix ( usually humorous ) Forming nouns denoting a putative ability to detect a thing. Probably from rebracketing of gaydar , itself a blend of gay +‎ radar . Alternatively radar might have been rebracketed directly as ray +‎ -dar , but this appears less likely because rays are, if anything, that by means of which a radar detects things.
Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of generic anthelmintic or antibiotic drugs.
Suffix skin or covering From Ancient Greek δέρμα (dérma, “skin”).
Suffix skin or skin disease From Ancient Greek δέρμα (dérma, “skin”).
Suffix having a specified type skin From Ancient Greek δέρμα (dérma, “skin”), genitive δέρματος (dérmatos) +‎ -ous .
/-ˈdiːsɪs/, /-ˌdɪs.ɪs/ Suffix ( medicine ) Binding, fusion. Borrowed from Ancient Greek δέσῐς (désĭs, “binding together”), from δέω (déō, “to bind, tie, fasten”) +‎ -σῐς (-sĭs, nominal suffix).
Suffix ( organic chemistry ) Involving the linkage of a sugar chain attached to a sapogenin through a glycosidic bond. From desmo- +‎ -ide +‎ -ic .
Suffix ( organic chemistry ) An unsaturated hydrocarbon with two double bonds; a diene ( organic chemistry ) A polymer of such a diene.
/dɪl/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of vasodilators. From vasodilator .
/dɪl.ɒl/, /dɪl.ɔl/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of vasodilators. From -dil- (“vasodilator”) +‎ -olol (“β-adrenoceptor antagonist”).
/dəˌpiːn/, /dɪp.iːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of generic calcium channel blocker drugs which are nifedipine derivatives. From nifedipine .
Suffix thirst From Ancient Greek δῐ́ψᾰ (dĭ́psă, “thirst”).
/diːvz/, /dɪvz/ Suffix ( no longer productive ) islands, archipelago. From Prakrit 𑀤𑀻𑀯 (dīva), from Sanskrit द्वीप (dvīpa, “island, peninsula”).

Suffix Used in naming of puzzle games similar to sudoku. Indicating a game that uses a rectangular grid of boxes where a set of symbols is used to fill the boxes of the grid, where each symbol is used only once in each row and each column; and the symbols form an ordered set, where only symbols up to the cardinality of the row or column are used. Typically the game is on a square grid, and the symbols are decimal numerals. Clipping of sudoku .

/-dɑm/, /-dɒm/, /-dəm/ Suffix Forms nouns denoting the condition or state of the root word. Forms nouns denoting the domain or jurisdiction of the root word. Forms nouns — usually nonce words — denoting the set of all examples of the suffixed word. ( fandom slang ) Forms nouns denoting the fandom of the suffixed word. ( BDSM ) Forms uncountable nouns denoting a type of domination. ( BDSM ) Forms countable nouns denoting a type of dominator. From Middle English -dom , from Old English -dōm (“-dom...
/ˈdoʊ.pə/, /ˈdəʊ.pə/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of dopamine receptor agonists or dopamine derivatives used as antiparkinsonisms or prolactin inhibitors. From dopamine .
/ˈdɹæl.ə.ziːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of hydrazinophthalazine derivatives used as antihypertensives. From hydralazine .
/dɹiːn/, /dɹɪn/, /ˌdɹin/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of sympathomimetics. From ephedrine .
Suffix ( literary ) Forms placenames related to racetracks (now especially large covered racetracks) or similar structures.
Suffix Running or moving in a specified manner. From Ancient Greek δρόμος (drómos, “race, running”) +‎ -ic .

Suffix ( engineering ) Forms names of certain types of motors and electrical circuits. ( aviation ) Relating to propeller-powered flight. From Ancient Greek δύναμις (dúnamis, “force”), compare -dynamic . Originally prevalent in now often genericized trademarks.

Suffix Used after a title to indicate that the person has been elected to hold the title, but has not yet officially taken it. From elect .
Suffix Used to form adjectives from chemical names, particularly acids and their derivatives. From -el +‎ -ic .
Suffix Used to form feminine proper names From French -elle .

/-iːm/ Suffix Indicating a fundamental unit in some kind of structure, chiefly linguistic structure. Extracted from phoneme , from Ancient Greek φώνημα (phṓnēma, “sound”), from φωνέω (phōnéō, “to sound”), from φωνή (phōnḗ, “sound”).

Suffix Forming nouns denoting patients or recipients of actions. From Latin -endus , a gerundive ending, related to -andus .

Suffix ( informal ) Added to certain nouns to form familiar diminutives, normally with jocular or light-hearted intent. Suffix Embellished form of -eroo .
Suffix Used to form slang or colloquial equivalents of words. Used to form mostly adjectives used informally. From -er .

Suffix beginning to be; becoming resembling From Latin -ēscēns , present participle of -ēscō (“I become”). By surface analysis, -esce +‎ -ent .

Suffix Used to form nouns denoting condition, action, or process. Suffix Noun suffix that denotes a condition, action, or process. French form of -esis, from Late Latin -esia , from -ēsis, from Ancient Greek -ησις (-ēsis), from -σις (-sis).

Suffix The act of creating something, or ( more broadly ) any process involving the specified thing. Borrowed from French -faction , from Latin -factiō . This is the ending that arises when forming action nouns using -tiō (equivalent to English -tion ) from compound verbs ending in -faciō (“to make, to do”), which carry a causative meaning. For example, liqueō (“to be liquid”) +‎ faciō > liquefaciō (“to make (something) liquid”) > liquefactiō > English liquefaction . The suffix was later app...

Suffix Used to form nouns denoting the process of producing or making something. Clipping of manufacture , ultimately from Latin factus (“done, made”), perfect passive participle of faciō (“to do, make”).

Suffix (Internet slang, originally 4chan slang, offensive, derogatory) Suffix that forms pejorative nouns, generally referring to specific types of people and sometimes used ironically or even affectionately. Usage Note This highly-productive suffix is controversial and not likely to be understood properly outside of 4chan-linked and similar imageboard culture. The nonderogatory senses especially may elude many people and may be interpreted for a homophobic offense (e.g. compare ...
/fə.næk/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of ibufenac derivatives used as generic nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs. From ibufenac .
/ˈfɛn.tə.nɪl/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of fentanyl derivatives acting as opioid receptor agonists and used as analgesics. From fentanyl .

/-fəɹəs/ Suffix Used to form adjectives from nouns, with the sense “bearing an entity or entities as specified by the noun” . Used to form adjectives from nouns, with the sense “producing a material as specified by the noun” . Used to form adjectives from nouns, with the sense “containing a material as specified by the noun” . /ˈfaɪ.bæn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of fibrinogen receptor antagonists. From fib(rinogen) +‎ an(tagonist) .
/faɪ.bɹeɪt/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of clofibrate derivatives used as peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor alpha agonists. From clofibrate.
Suffix Split, cleft From Latin -fidus , a combining form of findō (“to split”).

Suffix One who, or that which, does what is indicated by the stem (usually a verb). From -fy + -er . Etymology treeEnglish -fy Proto-West Germanic *-ārī Old English -ere Middle English -ere English -er English -fier From -fy + -er .
Suffix ( medicine ) Hydrophilic soft contact lens material. Phonetic blend of hydro- phil- ic +‎ con -tact lens .
Suffix ( neologism ) Forming terms denoting disingenuous ways by which people seek to attract attention, especially on a dating app. Back-formation from catfish .

/fɪks/ Suffix ( grammar ) Forming nouns denoting a morpheme used in word formation joined to a word in the specified way ( chiefly grammar ) Forming verbs denoting fastening or attaching (something) in the specified way Usage Note Used to form new words only in language-related fields. The common element of pre fix , suf fix , and af fix — all ultimately from Latin figere (“to fasten”).

/ˈflɑk.səs.ɪn/, /ˈflɒk.səs.ɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of generic fluoroquinolone antibiotics From norfloxacin .
Suffix Denoting an influencer who represents a specific group or specializes in a particular topic. Extracted from influencer .
/ˈflʊə.ɹeɪn/, /ˈflʊɹˌeɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of halogenated compounds used as general inhalation anesthetics. From flu(o)r- +‎ -ane .
Suffix (LGBTQ, rare) Having or pertaining to a gender identity or sexual orientation which fluctuates in intensity or between multiple options. From flux (“flowing; inconstant; variable”).
Suffix ( medicine ) Hydrophobic rigid contact lens material. Phonetic blend of hydro- pho- bic +‎ con -tact lens .

Suffix Used to make adjectives meaning "times". Used to make adverbs meaning "times". Usage Note Some writers and speakers use constructions like "an increase by twofold" to mean "a twofold increase". This can lead to confusion, as the logical rendering of "an increase by twofold" would imply "a 200% increase of the original amount" and the latter "an increase to 200% of the original amount". Caution should be taken to avoid such confusion. Typically, -fold takes no preposition. In scientific co...

Suffix Having the shape or form of; looking like. Related to formic acid in some way. From Latin -fōrmis (“having the form of”), from fōrma (“a form, contour, figure, shape, appearance, looks”). From formic , from Latin formīca (“ant”).
/ˈfɔɹ.mɪn/, /ˈfɔː.mɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of phenformin derivatives used as antihyperglycemics. From phenformin .
/fɒs/, /ˌfɑs/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of phosphorous derivatives used as insecticides, anthelminthics, pesticides, etc.
/ˈfɑs.fəˌmaɪd/, /ˈfɒs.fə.mʌɪd/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of alkylating agents of the cyclophosphamide group. From cyclophosphamide .
/fə.ziːn/, /fəˌsin/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of phosphorous derivatives used as cytostatics. From -fos- (“phosphorous derivative”) +‎ -ine .
/fəˌvɪə/, /fəˌvɪɹ/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of phosphonic acid derivatives used as antivirals. From pho(sphonic acid) +‎ -vir (“antiviral”).

/fɹiː/ Suffix Free from; devoid of; without. Usage Note In words where the suffix -less implies a lack of something that should be there, -free is sometimes used to reframe that absence as an intentional or beneficial state. For example, childless and childfree, saltless and saltfree, or carless and carfree. From Middle English -fre , -freo (as in scotfre , gauelfre , gafolfreo , etc.), from Old English -frēo ("free; free from"; as in scotfrēo , gafolfrēo ), from Proto-West Germanic &#42...

Infix ( vulgar ) An intensifier. Synonyms: -bloody-, -goddamn-, -motherfucking- Usage Note It is generally inserted before the stressed syllable, but may also be inserted between the prefix and the base. Words containing this infix may be spelled with or without hyphens, up to authorial preference. When placed between words in a phrase or name, this infix is usually written with spaces. Euphemisms such as -flipping- and -freaking- tend to be used when intentionally avoiding vulgar words. Tmetic ...
Suffix travelling out from Formed from the stem of Latin fugiō (“flee”), with the suffix -al , from Latin -alis .
Suffix Used to signify a noun that dispels or removes. From Latin -fugus .

/fəl/, /fʊl/ Suffix Used to form adjectives from nouns, with the sense of being full of, tending to, or thoroughly possessing the quality expressed by the noun. Used to form nouns from nouns meaning “as much as can be held by what is denoted by the noun”. Inherited from Middle English -ful , -full , from Old English -ful , -full (“full of; -ful”), from Proto-Germanic *-fullaz (“-ful”), from Proto-Germanic *fullaz (“full”); see full. Cognate with Scots -fu , Saterland Frisian -ful (“-ful”...

Usage Note In current usage, only standard for forming nouns (e.g., cheerfull is now a misspelling). For nouns, both forms have historically been considered acceptable; e.g., Weld’s Progressive English Grammar provides both spoonful and bowl-full as examples. Now usually hyphenated (e.g., bowl-full instead of bowlfull ), though both the spelling -ful and spaced compounds with full are more common (e.g., bowl full ); however, the plural is rarely formed by suffixing -s instead of pluralizing the ...
Infix ( pharmacology ) a monoclonal antibody that targets a fungus From fungus .
/ˈfʌn.d͡ʒɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of antifungal antibiotics. From fungal +‎ -in .
Suffix ( chiefly US ) German-style food, especially wursts or sausages. From the final element of frankfurter . Compare -burger .

/-ɡəməs/ Suffix Having the specified number or form of marriage. Having the specified form of reproduction, or reproductive organs. From Ancient Greek γάμος (gámos, “union, marriage”), equivalent to -gamy +‎ -ous .

/-ɡəmi/ Suffix Used to form nouns describing forms of marriage. ( biology ) Used to form nouns describing forms of fertilization, pollination or reproduction. From Ancient Greek -γαμία (-gamía), from γάμος (gámos, “marriage”).

/-ɡa(ː)z.əm/, /-ɡa(ː)z.ɪm/, /-ɡaz.əm/, /-ɡæz.əm/, /-ɡɛz.əm/ Suffix Forms nouns for a method of orgasm. ( figurative ) Forms nouns for an intensely “explosive” or pleasurable experience. Back-formation from orgasm (“climax or peak of sexual pleasure”).
Suffix Of or relating to the stomach. From Ancient Greek γαστήρ (gastḗr, “stomach”). Compare the prefix gastro- .

/-ɡeɪt/, /-ɡət/ Suffix Combined with a relevant place, person, activity, etc. to form the names of scandals. Used to form place names. Noun ( informal ) A scandal. Back-formation from Watergate , an American political scandal from 1972–1974 which led to resignation of president Richard Nixon. According to the Oxford English Dictionary , the suffix first appeared in a 1973 article in the National Lampoon magazine which referenced a rumoured "Volgagate". The suffix was promoted by New York Times c...
/ɡə.tɹæn/, /ˈɡæ.tɹæn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of thrombin inhibitors used as antithrombotic agents. From contraction of argatroban .
Suffix ( music ) Denotes a genre of music which makes heavy use of distortion effects and wall of sound production. Back-formation from shoegaze .

/ˈɡɛdən/ Suffix Indicating a disastrous or cataclysmic event or situation involving the referenced person or thing. Usage Note The form -mageddon is used with monosyllabic stems in order to match the syllabicity of Armageddon . Back-formation from Armageddon .
Suffix (Internet slang, humorous) Combined with a noun or adjective to denote a superlative example of the referent. Back-formation from gem (“highly valued thing”).
Suffix A producer of something, or an agent in the production of something. Borrowed from French -gène , from the Ancient Greek -γενής (-genḗs).
Suffix describes something which is formed in a certain way or produces something From French -gène and German -gen , from Latin -genus and Ancient Greek -γενής (-genḗs).

Suffix origin Synonym: -gony production Via Latin, from Ancient Greek γένεσις (génesis). The simplex genesis is in use as a common noun (as opposed to Genesis , the name of the biblical book) since the late 16th century, as a suffix since the mid 17th century (attested in epigenesis and palingenesis ). Influenced by French -génésie and German -genese and productive in scientific vocabulary from the 19th century on.

/-ˈdʒɛ.nɪk/ Suffix Forms adjectives relating to things producing or generating something. Forms adjectives relating to things produced or generated by something. Forms adjectives relating to things suitable to be produced or reproduced. Borrowed either from French -gène or directly from its etymological source, Ancient Greek -γενής (-genḗs, “offspring; kind”), with -ic . Compare -gen .
Suffix ( chemistry ) Used to form words for the aglycone (non-saccharide) residues of steroidal glycosides. From -gen +‎ -in , i.e. a compound ( -in ) formed from another compound ( -gen ).
Suffix Producing or yielding.
Suffix production generation origin Borrowed from Ancient Greek -γένεια (-géneia).
Suffix Bearing or producing. From Latin gerō (“to carry”) + English -ous .
/d͡ʒɛst/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of steroids, progestogens. From progestogen .
/ˈd͡ʒɛ.stə.ɹəʊn/, /ˈd͡ʒɛ.stəˌɹoʊn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of progestational steroids. From progesterone .
Suffix Forms names of medical conditions relating to the sense of taste

Suffix Combined with a relevant place, person, activity, etc. to form the names of scandals. Noun ( informal ) A scandal. Back-formation from Benghazi , the site of the 2012 Benghazi attack. The attack led to several investigations by the United States, though no criminal charges were levied.
/d͡ʒɪ.liːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of MAO inhibitors type B. From pargyline .
/ɡlɪˈfloʊ.zɪn/, /ɡlɪˈfləʊ.zɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of phlorizin derivatives used as sodium glucose cotransporter inhibitors. From gli- (“antihyperglycemic”) +‎ phlo(ri)zin .
/ˈɡlɪp.tɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form generic names of dipeptidyl peptidase-4 inhibitor antidiabetic drugs. From gli- (“antihyperglycemic”) +‎ (pe)pt(idase) +‎ in(hibitor) .
/ˈɡlɪt.ə.zɑː/, /ˈɡlɪt.əˌzɑɹ/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of dual peroxisome proliferator activated receptors-α and -γ agonists. Blend of -glitazone +‎ PPAR .

/ˈɡlɪt.ə.zəʊn/, /ˈɡlɪt.əˌzoʊn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of thiazolidinedione derivatives used as peroxisome proliferator activating receptor-γ agonists. From glitazone , from gli- +‎ th(i)az(olidinedi)one .

Suffix ( anatomy ) Used to form nouns relating to the tongue. ( linguistics ) Used to form nouns relating to language or speech. From the Ancient Greek γλῶσσα (glôssa, “tongue”). Compare gloss- , glott- .
Suffix language ( anatomy ) tongue From Ancient Greek γλῶσσᾰ (glôssă, “tongue; language”).
/ˈɡluː.taɪd/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of glucagon-like peptide analogs. From glu(cagon) +‎ -tide (“peptide, glycopeptide”).

Suffix ( zoology ) Forming nouns relating to the jaws or mouthparts. Ultimately from Ancient Greek γνάθος (gnáthos, “jaw”), frequently after formations in New Latin -gnatha , -gnathi , or French -gnathe .
Suffix ( anatomy ) Synonym of -gnathous . From Ancient Greek γνάθος (gnáthos, “jaw”) +‎ -ism . By surface analysis, -gnath +‎ -ic .
Suffix ( anatomy ) Denoting a condition of the jaws or mouthparts. From Ancient Greek γνάθος (gnáthos, “jaw”) +‎ -ism . By surface analysis, -gnath +‎ -ism .
Suffix ( biology ) Having a specified type of jaw. Synonym: -gnathic From Ancient Greek γνάθος (gnáthos, “jaw”) +‎ -ous . By surface analysis, -gnath +‎ -ic .
Suffix Synonym of -gnosis .
Suffix ( obsolete ) Scientific knowledge of a subject. From Ancient Greek -γνωσία (-gnōsía), from γνῶσις (gnôsis). Doublet of gnosis .
Infix ( pharmacology ) a shortened allomorph of the monoclonal antibody affixes -got- and -gov-, used before the affixes -xi- and -zu- for ease of pronunciation
/ɡə.laɪd/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of ergoline derivatives used as dopamine receptor agonists. From (er)goline +‎ -ide .

Suffix ( geometry ) Forms the names of plane figures containing a given number of internal angles, and thus bounded by that number of line segments ( polygons ). Synonym: -angle From Ancient Greek γωνία (gōnía, “corner, angle”), from Proto-Indo-European *ǵónu (“knee”).
Suffix Productive, fertile, fruitful From Ancient Greek γόνιμος (gónimos, “productive, fertile, fruitful”).

/ˈɡoʊnɪəm/, /ˈɡəʊnɪəm/ Suffix (biology, mycology) A reproductive structure of some organisms Relating to such a structure ( biology ) An immature cell that will develop into a reproductive one From Ancient Greek γονή (gonḗ, “engendering”) +‎ -ium (“structure”).
Suffix Forms nouns that describe the genesis of a class of thing. Synonym: -genesis From Ancient Greek γονή (gonḗ, “generation”).

Verb to strive towards some action; similar to -maxx , but also implies misguidedness or foolishness From goon (verb), possibly referring to the masturbation sense or the “being a goon” sense, or both.
Infix ( pharmacology ) a monoclonal antibody that targets testicular tumors From a blend of gonad and testes .
Infix ( pharmacology ) a monoclonal antibody that targets ovarian tumors Blend of gonad +‎ ovary .

Suffix ( zoology ) Describes a specified way of walking or locomotion, especially according to the limbs or organs utilized. Describes a specified direction. ( rare ) Describes a variation along a scale or gradient. From Latin gradus (“step, degree”) and gradior (“walk”).

/ˈɡrɛə̯m/, /ˈɡɹeə̯m/, /ˈɡɹæm/ Suffix Something written, drawn or otherwise recorded. A star polygon, with the number of points indicated by the numeral prefix. From γράμμα (grámma, “written character, letter, that which is drawn”), from γράφω (gráphō, “to scratch, to scrape, to graze”).

Suffix that writes ( metonymic ) that is written ( by analogy ) that draws or shows ( metonymic ) that is drawn or shown A group of letters of a specified number. From Ancient Greek -γράφος (-gráphos, “that writes, describes”), from γράφω (gráphō, “to scratch, to scrape, to graze”), whence also -graphy .
Suffix someone who writes about a specified subject, or in a specified manner a machine which notates a specified subject From Ancient Greek γράφω (gráphō, “I draw, write or describe”) +‎ -er .
Suffix Forms names of writing impairments. Learned borrowing from Ancient Greek -γρᾰφῐ́ᾱ (-grăphĭ́ā, “writing, drawing”).
Suffix Forming adjectives pertaining to writing (of a type specified by the first element). From Ancient Greek -γραφικός (-graphikós), or formed in English from -graphy +‎ -ic .
Suffix Used to form adjectives meaning of or relating to corresponding nouns ending in -graphy or -graph .

/-ɡɹəfi/, /-ˈɡrafi/ Suffix Something written or otherwise represented in the specified manner, or about a specified subject. Field of study. From French -graphie , inherited from Latin -graphia , borrowed from Ancient Greek -γραφία (-graphía), from γραφή (graphḗ, “writing, drawing, description”). By surface analysis, -graph +‎ -y .

Suffix ( historical ) Used to signify a local ruler or administrator. From Old Saxon grēve , typically used in translations of feudal German titles ending in German Graf (“count”), from Proto-West Germanic *garāfijō .
/ɡɹɛl/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of platelet aggregation inhibitors. Probably related to platelet aggregation.
Suffix Female, related to women. Equivalent to gyn- (“female”) +‎ -ous

Suffix the state of having a specific number of wives ( botany ) a situation with respect to female plant organs ( botany ) having a specified number or type of female plant organs From Ancient Greek γυνή (gunḗ, “woman, female”).
Suffix Rotatory.

Suffix (Internet slang, also originally African-American Vernacular) Used to censor words. Used to form new words. An orthographic device that forces the preceding vowel to be read ‘short’: ⟨ dih ⟩ /dɪ/, ⟨ bruh ⟩ /bɹʌ/, etc. (not /da͡ɪ, bɹu/). Although the letter h has long been used ad-hoc for this purpose in English — see uh and meh — the practice of clipping content words to humorous effect took off as an Internet meme in 2025 and created a productive association between -h and su...
/həm/, /əm/, /ˌhæm/ Suffix Place-name suffix, variously indicating a settlement, farm or enclosure. From the convergence of two elements:

Suffix Used to create nouns indicating a state; -hood. ( sometimes derogatory ) Used to form words for people who regularly have their mind focused upon a particular subject, activity, or a specified drug or other substance, or who are addicted in some way. Used to form words to describe people who are dedicated fans of something, especially music. ( derogatory ) Used with other words to form generic insults or epithets to indicate stupidity.
