/-ˈhiːdɹəl/, /-ˈhɛdɹəl/ Suffix Forms adjectives referring to solid figures bounded by a certain number of planes (polyhedra). Borrowed from Ancient Greek ἕδρα (hédra, “face of a geometrical solid”) + -al .
Suffix ( geometry ) Forms the names of solid figures bounded by a certain number of planes (polyhedra). From Ancient Greek ἕδρα (hédra, “face of a geometrical solid”).

Suffix Used to refer to derivatives of Stonehenge. The alignment of the sun at sunrise or sunset with a street, building, or other manmade structure on a particular day of the year. Back-formation from Stonehenge .

Suffix Denotes one who has an addiction to or indulgence in the substance or activity of the stem word. Denotes being addicted to or indulging in the substance or activity of the stem word. Rebracketing of alcoholic .

Suffix A form of addiction, either a physical or obsessive dependency. Usage Note The term -holism is not an accepted medical term, but is a fairly prominent neologism. Rebracketing of alcoholism , the name of one of the first addictions to be widely identified both medically and socially. It should be noted that the word alcoholism is broken up into the root, alcohol , and the suffix -ism , not alco and -holism . The suffix -holism is thus a result of false splitting.
/-hɑn/, /-hɒn/, /-hɔn/ Noun (4chan /lgbt/ slang, derogatory, offensive) Forms compounds referring to trans women who do not pass. Derived from hon (“trans woman who does not pass”).

/hʊd/ Suffix A substantive suffix denoting a condition or state of being. A substantive suffix denoting a group sharing a specified condition or state. From Middle English -hode , from Old English -hād , from Proto-West Germanic *-haidu , from Proto-Germanic *haiduz (compare -head ). Cognate with German -heit , -keit , Dutch -heid , Afrikaans -heid , Swedish -het , Elfdalian -iet , Norwegian Bokmål -het , Norwegian Nynorsk -heit , Danish -hed . The Swedish, Elfdalian, Norwegian and Danis...
Suffix Suffix used to indicate the age of something or someone, in terms of hours.

/hʌb/ Suffix (Internet slang, humorous) Denotes a notional website or webpage that contains pornographic, suggestive or desirable content of the root word. From a clipping of Pornhub , ultimately from hub (“a central facility providing a range of related services”).
Suffix Relating to the hyoid bone. From the Ancient Greek ὖ (û, “name of the letter 'Y'”), whence also hyoid , due to the shape of the bone.

/aɪ/, /iː/ Suffix Used to form adjectives, nouns, demonyms (describing people of a particular city, region or country) or genonyms (describing people of a particular tribe, clan or family) and the language spoken by these people. Forms an alternative plural ending for various words borrowed from Latin that end in ⟨us⟩ in the singular. (nonstandard, sometimes proscribed) Forms an alternative plural ending for various words that end in ⟨us⟩ in the singular, despite not being borrowed from ...

Suffix A spelling variant of a word-final -y vowel when followed by a suffix that does not begin with an i , for example -ied , -ier , -iest . In compound words, a spelling variant of a final or suffixed -y vowel in the first word. Usage Note This convention applies to any final y vowel, not just to the suffix -y . It does not apply to digraph vowels ending in y and followed by a suffix -s , for example lay → lays (cf. laid , lain ), nor to a single-letter y vowel before a suffix such as -ing th...

Suffix Used in forming placenames, especially of countries. Used in forming names of diseases. Used in forming names of flowers. ( rare ) Used in forming names of collections of things. Used in forming plurals of nouns in -ium and -ion . Used in forming female given names. From Latin -ia and Ancient Greek -ία (-ía), -εια (-eia), which form abstract nouns of feminine gender. From the endings of corresponding Latin and Ancient Greek plural nouns. From Latin -ia , feminine of -ius , a suffix formin...

Suffix Forming the name of an epic about the indicated topic. ( rare ) A period of time from one occurrence of an (indicated, regularly recurrent) event to the next. Based on Iliad . Based on Olympiad , and perhaps also influenced by the common ending iad on units of time formed by suffixing -ad to words ending in -ium , e.g. decenniad .

/-i.əl/, /-iː.al/, /-iː.əl/ Suffix Forms a noun from a verb ending in -y. Inherited from Middle English -ial , derived from Old French -ial , derived from Latin -ālis after radical in -ius , -ia , -ium . Equivalent to -i- +‎ -al .

/i.ən/, /jən/, /ɪjən/ Suffix ( as an adjective ) From, related to, or like. ( as a noun ) One from, belonging to, relating to, or like. ( as a noun ) Having a certain profession. A romanization of the Armenian surname suffix -յան (-yan). Usage Note When males with a profession are distinguished from females, males are -ian , females -ienne . The plural is -ians ( one magician , two magicians ). When added to a word ending in a vowel, the infix -v- is inserted ( Peruvian , Whovian ), though this ...

Suffix Used in forming female given names. From Latin -āna (neuter plural of -ānus ) by analogy with formations ending in -ian ( Canadian → Canadiana , Edwardian → Edwardiana ). By surface analysis, -ia +‎ -ana . From -ia + -ana .

Suffix ( pathology ) A pathological condition or process. A Latin word-forming element meaning "process" or "morbid condition". Originally from Ancient Greek ἴασις (íasis). By surface analysis, -i- +‎ -asis .
Suffix Of or relating to a physician. Pertaining to a particular branch of medicine. From Ancient Greek ἰᾱτρικός (iātrikós, “medical”), from ἰατρός (iatrós, “doctor”) +‎ -ικός (-ikós, “-ic”).

Suffix A medical practitioner. Usage Note Used when alternative ways constructing the name for a medical field are not euphonious or are confusing, e.g. podiatrist vs. ** pediatrist > pediatrician . Etymology treeEnglish -iatric Proto-Italic *-nos Latin -nus Latin -ānus Latin -iānus bor.English -ian English -iatrician From -iatric + -ian .
Suffix Forms a noun meaning a specialty medical field of practice. From Ancient Greek ἰατρός (iatrós, “doctor”) +‎ -ics .
Suffix Forms names of specialized fields of medical practice. From Ancient Greek ἰατρεία (iatreía, “healing, medical treatment”), from ἰατρός (iatrós, “doctor”).

/əbəl/, /ɪbəl/ Suffix An adjective suffix, now usually in a passive sense; forms adjectives meaning "able to be", "relevant or suitable to, in accordance with", or expressing capacity or worthiness in a passive sense. An instrumental suffix; forms nouns representing: a tool or instrument a place or location Usage Note The form -able is usually used in the same sense and pronounced the same, though sometimes equivalent terms have diverged in meaning: compare suggestable (“capable of b...

Suffix Used to form adverbs corresponding to adjectives that end in -ible. Euphonic blend of -ible +‎ -ly , due to difficulty of pronouncing * -iblely . Compare same sound change in unrelated feebly , nimbly , and nobly .

/ək/, /ɪk/ Suffix Used to form adjectives from nouns with the meaning “of or pertaining to”. ( chemistry ) Used to denote certain chemical compounds in which a specified chemical element has a higher oxidation number than in the equivalent compound whose name ends in the suffix -ous . For example sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄) has more oxygen atoms per molecule than sulphurous acid (H₂SO₃). From Middle English -ik , from Old French -ique , from Latin -icus , from Proto-Indo-European *-kos, *-ḱos...

Suffix a collection of things that relate to a specific place, person, theme, etc. Synonyms: -ana, -iana, -ia From Latin -ica , neuter plural of -icus . Note that etymologically this is not -ic + -a (“(plural ending)”), as -ic is the English form, having dropped the -um , though adding an -a to an English word ending in -ic does produce a superficial etymologically.

/ɪkəl/ Suffix Used to form adjectives from nouns with the meaning "of or pertaining to"; adjectival suffix appended to various words, often nouns, to make an adjective form. Often added to words of Greek or Latin origin, but used with other words also. From Middle English -ical , a combination of -ic from Old French -ique , from Latin -icus , related to Ancient Greek suffix Ancient Greek -ικός (-ikós), plus -al from Latin adjective suffix -alis , or Old French -el . By surface analysis, -ic ...
/ɪ.kæm/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of isoxicam derivatives used as anti-inflammatory agents. From isoxicam .
/ɪ.keɪs/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of uricases. From uricase .

Suffix ( no longer productive ) Forms abstract nouns. From Anglo-Norman -ice and Old French -ice , from either (1) Latin -īcius (“adjective suffix”), from -īcus +‎ -ius or (2) Latin -itia (“noun suffix denoting condition”).
Suffix ( paleontology ) Forms names of types of trace fossils. From Ancient Greek ἴχνιον (íkhnion, “track, footprint”).

/-ˈɪʃən/ Suffix forming nouns denoting occupations: A person who studies or is an expert in the related -ics. Usage Note Relatively few terms are directly derived using this suffix. Terms ending in -ician were often derived from Middle French words ending in -icien , which themselves were derived from words ending in -ique f sg + -ien , e.g.: From French -icien , from -ique f sg +‎ -ien . By surface analysis, -ic +‎ -ian .

/ɪsɪzem/ Suffix Forms abstract nouns from Greek or Latin bases. From -ic +‎ -ism , either based directly on the adjective in -ic (as in asceticism , mysticism ), or on the verb in -ise (as in anglicism ). Some productivity within English, as in witticism (1670s, by analogy with criticism ).
/ɪs.ɪ.ti/ Suffix Used to form nouns, denoting a quality or condition, from adjectives, especially ones ending in -ic (in which case "ic" is not duplicated (see -ity)).

Suffix Obsolete spelling of -ic . Usage Note Generally, the k fell out of use by the end of the 19th century, with a few exceptions, such as magick and mystick . However, the ending k exists in derived terms ending in -ed , -er and -ing to clarify that the pronunciation does not sound as s (e.g. "trafficking", "panicked").

Suffix (uncommon, diminutive) Used to form diminutive nouns. Synonyms: ( suffixes ) -cule, -ee, -el, -et, -ette, -ie, -kin, -le, -let, -ling, -ole, -ule, -y, ( prefixes ) mini-, micro-, nano- From a back-formation of icicle , the second element of which ultimately from Old English ġicel (“icicle, ice”). More at icicle, ickle. From Latin -culus (diminutive suffix).

Suffix Forms nouns referring to fields of knowledge or practice. Synonyms: -istics, -logy, -s Forms nouns referring to activities. From -ic (“related or pertaining to”), with usage in the plural in imitation of the Latin and Greek usage.

/ɪd/ Suffix ( history ) Forming the names of dynasts, being suffixed to the name of their progenitors and meaning “descendant of”. ( taxonomy ) Forming the common names of members of a taxon which has a name ending in -idae . (dentistry, paleontology) Forming the names of cusps of lower (mandibular) teeth. ( botany ) Forming nouns from Latin or Greek roots, including certain plant names modelled on Latin sources. ( mythology ) Forming names of feminine equivalents or of feminine descendants ...

/aɪd/ Suffix Archaic form of -id . Any of a group of related compounds - azide, polysaccharide, glycoside. A binary compound - bromide, arsenide, palladide. Any of a group of several elements - lanthanide. Borrowed from Middle French -ide m, from Latin -idēs . Borrowed from Middle French -ide f, from Latin -is . Generalized in the early 19th c. from oxide (which has its ending from French acide , itself from Latin -idus in acidus ), and gradually displaced earlier -uret , both introduced in the ...
/ɪ.diːn/ Suffix ( chemistry ) Radical having two valence bonds at the point of attachment. From -ide +‎ -ene .
Suffix (sciences, rare) Forming nouns or adjectives. From the ending -idian , on the model of annelidian , arachnidian , ascidian , etc. By surface analysis, -id +‎ -ian .

Suffix ( organic chemistry ) Denotes a chemical compound containing a ring containing nitrogen ( pharmacology ) alpha-2 adrenergic receptor agonists. Derived from the ending of pyridine, which comes from German Pyridin which in turn comes from a combination of pyro- -id and -in (-ide and -ine)
Suffix Used to form diminutive nouns. From Ancient Greek -ίδιον (-ídion).

Suffix Forms names of body parts, mainly of invertebrate animals, fungi and plants. ( most common ) reproductive organs, gametes, and early life stages sensory organs, mainly of arthropods various segments (arthropods) and functional processes respiratory organs ( marginal ) cellular compartments From Ancient Greek -ίδιον (-ídion, “diminutive”).

Suffix ( conlanging ) Attached to the name of a language (typically a constructed language) to denote a conlang derived from or based on that language. Back-formation from Esperantido , from Esperanto -ido (“offspring”).

/-i/, /-iː/ Suffix Forming diminutive or affectionate forms of nouns or names. Synonyms: ( suffixes ) -cule, -ee, -el, -et, -ette, -icle, -kin, -le, -let, -ling, -ole, -ule, -y, ( prefixes ) mini-, micro-, nano- Forming feminine or gender‐neutral forms of given names. (chiefly Australia, New Zealand, UK) Forms colloquial clippings (often functioning as familiarizers), truncating the root to a single stressed syllable. ( occasionally derogatory ) Forming colloquial nouns signifyin...
Suffix past participle form of verbs ending in y, and participial adjectives therefrom; equivalent to y + -ed .
Suffix indicates a female variant of a noun ending in -ian Borrowed from French -ienne , feminine of -ien (“-ian”).

Suffix spelling of the suffix sequence -y followed by -er , or of any word ending in -(e)y suffixed with -er . Forming nouns denoting occupations, particularly with words from French. From -y + -er . From Middle English -ier , -iere from verbs ending in -ien + -ere , later extended to form other nouns (e.g. Middle English clothyer , glasiere , lovier , tiliere ). Later reinforced by Middle French and modern French -ier , largely in words more recently borrowed from or influenced by French (e.g. ...

Suffix Variant of -es for the plural forms of nouns ending in a consonant + y ; equivalent to (dropped y ) + ( -i- + -es ). Used to form the third person singular in the present indicative of verbs ending in a consonant + y .
Suffix spelling of the suffix sequence -y followed by -est , or of certain words ending in -y suffixed with -est . From -y +‎ -est .
Suffix Forming nouns denoting the quality or condition of being what is indicated by the first element of the word. From French -ieté or its source, Latin -ietas .
/ɪ.fiːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of clomifene or tamoxifen derivatives used as antiestrogens or estrogen receptor modulators. From clomifene and tamoxifen .
Suffix Creating or causing something. ( informal ) Very; extremely; forming adjectives from nouns. From Latin -ficus , from facere (“to make”).

/ɪfɪˈkeɪʃən/ Suffix Forms nouns denoting the act or process whereby a subject becomes something else. Usage Note This suffix occurs in words of French or Latin origin, but is also productive in English. When applying the suffix -ation to a verb ending in -ify , -ification is used instead of the expected * -ifiation . Compare -ability . From Middle English -ificacioun (ending on words generally borrowed whole from Old French), from Old French -ification , in turn from Latin -ficātiō , a noun endi...

/ɪfaɪ/ Suffix Forms transitive verbs meaning "to make" from adjectives and nouns. Forms intransitive verbs meaning "to become" from adjectives and nouns. From Middle English -ifien , from Old French -ifier , from Latin -ificāre , from -ficus , from faciō (“to make, do”). For example, purify from purificare , from purificus .

Suffix (Internet slang, humorous) Deliberate misspelling of -ing . Usage Note This spelling may also be applied to words that end with ing that do not visibly have the -ing suffix, such as during . Originating as a mistyping of -ing .

Suffix Misspelling of -i . Used to form nonstandard, uncommon, often humorous alternative plural forms of nouns that end in -us, -is or a similar sound. Usage Note All such forms are nonstandard, and therefore may be proscribed. For certain forms, such as cactii , a standard spelling with -i ( cacti ) exists; in this case, the -ii spelling may be simply a misspelling of the -i form. Other innovative plurals in -ii, such as stewardii , bear no resemblance to any standard plural form of the word; ...

Suffix ( no longer productive ) Tending to, or capable of. ( statistics ) Any of the values in a sorted data set that splits it into a specified number of equally sized groups. Any of the groups formed from the division. Learned borrowing from Latin -īlis .
/əˌlaɪd/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) A sematilide derivative used as a Class III antiarrhythmic. From sematilide .
Suffix Combined with Latin prefixes for names of integers in order to form names of large numbers as 10 6n+3 . From French -illiard .

Suffix Combined with Latin prefixes for names of integers in order to form names of powers of a million. Added to various nonsense syllables to indicate an arbitrarily very large number. Usage Note The words formed with this suffix may mean either or in different nations and languages. Hence billion can mean “one million million” or “one thousand million”. See Long and short scales on Wikipedia. Back-formation from million , then used in billion , trillion , quadrillion , each from French, ultim...
Suffix Forms adverbs from nouns and verbs whose derived adjectives are suffixed with -y . Combination of -y (“adjectival suffix”) + -ly (“adverbial suffix”), with -y changed to -i- .
Suffix Used to form plurals in words derived from Hebrew. Borrowed from Hebrew ־ים (-ím).
Suffix Used to form names of numerical bases (radixes). Rebracketing of decimal , as if it were from dec- +‎ -imal .
Suffix Suffix denoting ordinal number. From French -ime , from decime , ultimately from Latin -ima .
/ɪ.maɪb/, /ɪ.mɪb/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of acyl CoA:cholesterol acyltransferase inhibitors used as antihyperlipidemics.
Suffix ( slang ) nonsense suffix added to adjectives to give emphasis. Derived from nonsense Italian- and Spanish-sounding words.
/ɪm.əs/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of immunosuppressants other than antineoplastics. Contraction of immunosuppressant .

Suffix ( biochemistry ) Used, as a modification of -ine, to form the names of a variety of types of compound; examples include proteins (globulin), carbohydrates (dextrin), dyes (alizarin) and others (vanillin). A neutral chemical compound. An enzyme. An antibiotic. A pharmaceutical product. Attached to a word (usually a verb) to denote a protest, demonstration or other type of gathering characterized by the activity denoted by the base word. Etymology treeProto-Italic *-īnos Latin -īnus...

Suffix ( colloquial ) Pronunciation spelling of -ing . Usage Note In speech, the difference between the pronunciations /ɪŋ/ and /ɪn/ is sometimes one of formality. Some speakers tend to use /ɪŋ/ in more formal situations and /ɪn/ in less formal situations. However, other speakers can either use mainly /ɪŋ/ or mainly /ɪn/ regardless of the situation. In writing, both the /ɪŋ/ and the /ɪn/ pronunciations are usually spelled -ing . When writers use -in', as in makin', they are emphasizing the infor...
/ɪnˌlɔ/ Suffix Related through marriage. Back-formation from mother-in-law , father-in-law , brother-in-law , etc., equivalent to in +‎ law .
Suffix Depending on the expected arrival of a result, confirmation, etc. About to happen; bound to happen. Attendant to a royal figure.

Suffix Used to form feminine nouns. Synonym: -ine Used to form names of musical instruments. From Italian -ina and its etymon Classical Latin -īna . In some instances perhaps also partly via Spanish -ina . Doublet of -ine .

Suffix A person or thing that does (something). A popular term used notably by Dr. Heinz Doofenshmirtz, a character from the 2007 American animated television series Phineas and Ferb ; however, usage dates to at least the early 2000s, e.g., kupkake-inator in the 2003 film The Cat in the Hat . Modelled after Latinate agent nouns such as terminator . See -ator .

Suffix A component of some female given names. Latinized form of Germanic name suffix -lind , -linde (“tender, soft”), such as Sieglinde and Dietlinde , from Proto-Germanic *linþiz (“gentle, mild”) (English lithe ). Found in names such as Old High German Irmilinda and Old Dutch Frithelind . See lind , Linda .

/-aɪ̯n/, /-in/, /-ɪn/ Suffix ( chiefly no longer productive ) Of or pertaining to. Used to form demonyms. ( chemistry ) Used to form names of chemical substances, especially basic (alkaline) substances, alkaloidal substances, or halogen elements. ( anthropology ) used to form vernacular nouns and adjectives relating to hominoid genera Commercial materials. ( no longer productive ) Used to form feminine nouns. Synonyms: -a, -ess, -ette, -ina, -ress, she-, -trix ( no longer productive ) Used t...

/in/, /iŋ/, /ən/, /ɪŋ/, /ɪŋk/, /ɪŋɡ/ Suffix Used to form nouns or noun-like words (or elements of noun phrases) from verbs, denoting the act of doing something, an action, or the embodiment of an action. As true nouns. As gerunds. Used to form nouns denoting materials or systems of objects which are used or employed in an action, or considered collectively. Used to form present participles of verbs. ( no longer productive ) Forming derivative nouns (originally masculine), with the sense ‘son of,...

/ˈinoʊ/, /ˈiːnəʊ/ Suffix ( physics ) The fermionic supersymmetric partner of a boson (a bosino), symbolized by a tilde over the nonsupersymmetric particle symbol. ( chiefly DoggoLingo ) A diminutive or endearing suffix. (Internet slang, 4chan slang, derogatory) Used to mock progressives through association with DoggoLingo. Usage Note The diminutive is chiefly used after -er . From Italian -ino (“-ine, -o: forming diminutives ”), from the dative form of Latin -īnus . In its use in phy...
Suffix Used to form an adjective meaning "resembling" from nouns ending in -ium or -ia . By surface analysis, -i(um) +‎ -o- +‎ -id and -i(a) +‎ -o- +‎ -id

/aɪən/, /ən/, /ɪən/ Suffix ( no longer productive ) An action or process, or the result of an action or process. ( no longer productive ) A state or condition. An anthropometric reference point. Especially craniometric points Together with apo- and peri-, forms names of specific apoapsides and periapsides. Reanalysed from -ation , -tion , from Middle French -tion , -cion , from Latin -tiō . By surface analysis, this suffix is added to verbs derived from Latin perfect participle stems. Possibly r...
Suffix Suffix occurring in comparative adjectives borrowed from Latin. From Latin -ior , the nominative masculine and feminine singular of the comparative suffix -iōr . Variant of -iour .
Suffix Of or relating to an island or peninsula in Greece or the Hellenic sphere. Back-formation of Cypr iot , from Ancient Greek Κυπριώτης (Kupriṓtēs, “Cypriot”).

Suffix ( no longer productive ) Suffix added to verbs to form nouns. An alteration of -our , arising in the 15th and 16th centuries through analogy with words such as saviour (Middle English saveour , savour ) and paviour (Middle English paver , pavier ) which varied between forms with an intervening -e- or -i- and those without due to differing adaptions of their Old French sources ( sauveor and paveor , *pavier respectively).
/ɪˈɹuː.dɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of hirudin derivatives. From hirudin .

Suffix UK standard spelling of -ization . Etymology treeEnglish -ise Proto-Italic *-tiō Latin -tiō Latin -ātiō lbor.Old French -ation bor.Middle English -acioun English -ation English -isation From -ise + -ation .

/-ɑjz/ Suffix Suffix used in loanwords from French to form abstract nouns of quality or function. From Middle English -isen , from Middle French -iser , from Late Latin -izāre , from Ancient Greek -ίζω (-ízō), from Proto-Indo-European *-idyé- (verbal suffix). Cognate with Old English -ettan (verbal suffix). From Middle English -ise , from Old French -ise , borrowed from Latin -itia .
Usage Note See notes at -ize . From -ise + -er . Etymology treeEnglish -ise Proto-West Germanic *-ārī Old English -ere Middle English -ere English -er English -iser From -ise + -er .

/iʃ/, /əʃ/, /ɪʃ/ Suffix ( of adjectives from common nouns ) Typical of, similar to, being like. ( of adjectives from adjectives, with a diminutive force ) Somewhat, rather. ( of adjectives from numbers, especially of times and ages ) About, approximately. ( of adjectives from roots of proper nouns denoting names of nations or regions ) Of, belonging, or relating to (a nationality, place, language or similar association with something). ( no longer productive ) An ending found on some verbs; ...

/-ɪz(ə)m/ Suffix Used to form nouns of action, process, or result based on the accompanying verb ending in -ise or -ize. Used to form the name of a school of thought, system, or theory based on the name of its subject or object or alternatively on the name of its founder ( When de-capitalized, these overlap with the generic "doctrines" sense below, e.g. Liberalism vs. liberalism. ) Used to form names of a tendency of action, behaviour, condition, opinion, or state belonging to a class or group o...

/-əst/, /-ɪst/ Suffix Added to words to form nouns denoting: a person who studies or practices a particular discipline; a person who uses a device of some kind; one who engages in a particular type of activity; one who suffers from a specific condition or syndrome one who subscribes to a particular theological doctrine or religious denomination; one who has a certain ideology or set of beliefs; one who owns or manages something; a person who holds bigoted, partial views with respect to a particu...
/-ˈiːstə/, /-ˈɪstə/ Suffix Forms nouns denoting one who follows a principle; an adept. From Spanish -ista in Sandinista (1930s). Doublet of -ist .
Suffix Used to form adjectives from nouns or from other adjectives, with the meaning "of or pertaining to" the preceding component. From -ist +‎ -ic .

Suffix Used to form adjectives meaning of or relating to corresponding nouns ending in -istic or -ist ; most often redundant with respect to the latter which can also become adjectives through the addition of -ic alone, or with no change to the root noun. Hence, theist , theistic and theistical can all be used as the adjective form of theism . Combination of the adjective suffixes -istic ( -ist + -ic ) and -al .

Suffix Used to form adverbs describing action in the manner of the root adjective; use is identical irrespective of whether the root adjective ends with -istic or -istical . Alternately -istical (“adjective suffix”) + -ly (“adverb suffix”) or -istic (“adjective suffix”) + -ally (“adverb suffix”). In either case, ultimately -ist + -ic + -al + -ly .
Suffix Synonym of -logy (“branch of learning; a study of a particular subject”). Synonyms: -ics, -logy, -s
Suffix Used to form nouns denoting condition, action, or process. Ultimately from Ancient Greek -σις (-sis).
Suffix Forming adjectives, typically from a place ending in -polis . Borrowed from Latin -ītānus .

Suffix ( very rare ) Forming verbs, typically implying intense or repetitive activity. From Latin -itāt- , past participial stem of -itō . On the model of e.g. Classical Latin nōbilitō and found in borrowings e.g. premeditate , debilitate , agitate .

/-aɪt/, /-ɪt/ Suffix ( sometimes derogatory ) Used to form nouns denoting followers or adherents of a specified person, idea, doctrine, movement, etc. Used to form nouns denoting descendants of a specified historical person, especially a biblical figure. (chiefly US, India) Used to form demonyms. Used to form nouns denoting rocks or minerals. Used to form nouns denoting fossil organisms. ( biology ) Used to form nouns denoting segments or components of the body or an organ of the bod...
Suffix Relating to, resembling, marked by.
Suffix Forms adjectives denoting "pertaining to, characterized by, having the nature of, resembling, tending to." From Latin -ītius or -īcius .

/-ˈaɪtɪs/ Suffix ( pathology ) Denoting diseases characterized by inflammation, itself often caused by an infection. ( humorous ) Used to form the names of various fictitious afflictions or diseases. Usage Note While most of the derived terms theoretically have plurals in -itides (from the Ancient Greek -ῑ́τῐδες (-ī́tĭdes), plural of -ῖτῐς (-îtĭs)), -itises (the regularized English plural), or both, these forms are rarely used, as the derived terms are mass nouns, so their plurals are called for...

Suffix ( organic chemistry ) Forming the names of polyhydric alcohols (sugar alcohols). From reanalysis of the ending of mannitol , sorbitol and so on, from -ite (an old, non-specific use of this suffix) + -ol .
/ɪˈtjuːd/, /ɪˈtud/, /ɪˈt͡ʃuːd/ Suffix Used to forms nouns of state, condition or quality. Borrowed from Middle French -itude , from Latin -(i)tūdō . By surface analysis, -i- +‎ -tude .

/əti/, /ɪti/, /ɪʈi/ Suffix Used to form an uncountable noun from an adjective; especially, to form the noun referring to the state, property, or quality of conforming to the adjective's description. Used to form a countable noun from an adjective, referring to someone or something that conforms to the adjective's description. Used to form other nouns, especially abstract nouns. Usage Note Many nouns formed with -ity are uncountable; those that are countable form their plurals in -ities . Fin...

Suffix ( chemistry ) Used to form the names of metal elements, after the style of early-named elements, as well as the isotopes of hydrogen. ( chemistry ) Used to form the temporary systematic element name of a metallic or nonmetallic element which is postulated to exist, or which has been newly synthesized and has not yet been assigned a permanent name. ( chemistry ) Used to form the name of polyatomic cations. Common examples are quaternary ammonium compounds used as neuromuscular blocking age...

/ɪv/ Suffix An adjective suffix signifying relating or belonging to, of the nature of, tending to, or serving to; as: affirmative, active, conclusive, corrective, diminutive. From Middle English -yf , from Anglo-Norman -if from Latin -īvus . Until the fourteenth century, all Middle English loanwords from Anglo-Norman ended in -if (compare actif , natif , sensitif , pensif etc.). Under the influence of literary Neo-Latin, both languages introduced the form -ive . Those forms that have not been re...
Infix (slang, African-American Vernacular) An intensifier. From a style of cant used by African Americans in the 1970s and popularized by rapper Snoop Dogg. See -izzle for more.

/-əˈzeɪ.ʃən/, /-ˌaɪˈzeɪ.ʃən/ Suffix Forms nouns denoting the act, process, or result of doing/making what is denoted by the noun/adjective. Usage Note See the usage notes on -ize . Etymology treeProto-Indo-European *-id- Proto-Indo-European *-yéti Proto-Indo-European *-idyéti Proto-Hellenic *-íďďō Ancient Greek -ῐ́ζω (-ĭ́zō)bor.Late Latin -izō der.Middle French -iser bor.Middle English -isen English -ize Proto-Italic *-tiō Latin -tiō Latin -ātiō lbor.Old French -ation...

/-aɪz/, /-ɑjz/ Suffix Used to form verbs from nouns or adjectives, especially similative verbs to make what is denoted by the noun/adjective to do what is denoted by the noun/adjective From Middle English -isen , from Middle French -iser , from Late Latin -izāre , from Ancient Greek -ίζω (-ízō), from Proto-Indo-European *-idyé- (verbal suffix). Cognate with Old English -ettan (verbal suffix). See -ise (noun-forming suffix).
Suffix used to form nouns which are formed from verbs from nouns or adjectives, the final nouns having the sense of "the agent which makes what is denoted by the noun/adjective". From -ize +‎ -er .
/ɪ.ziːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of diphenylmethylpiperazine derivatives. Probably from hydroxyzine .

/-ˈɪzəl/ Suffix (slang, African-American Vernacular) Forms hip-hop-sounding words, which replaces the word with the first sound of the word followed by -izzle . Popularized by rap artist Snoop Dogg, but first put to record by Frankie Smith's 1981 "Double Dutch Bus", from a style of cant (esoteric slang) used by African American pimps and jive hustlers of the 1970s. The “ -iz , -izzle , -izzo , -ilz ” speak (which also uses an infix -iz- ), similar in some ways to Pig Latin, was developed by ...
Suffix (colloquial, used only after a /d/ sound) You, ya.
Suffix ( India ) An honorific conveying respect. From a suffix found in various languages of the Indian subcontinent, e.g. Hindi -जी (-jī), Punjabi -ਜੀ (-jī), etc.

Suffix (rare, no longer productive) A suffix found in verbs of Middle English, Old English, usually with an intensive or frequentative effect. (rare, no longer productive) A suffix found in nouns, usually with a diminutive effect. From Middle English -ken , -kien , from Old English -cian , from Proto-West Germanic *-ukōn , *-ikōn , from Proto-Germanic *-ukōną , *-ikōną . Cognate with West Frisian -kje , German -chen , Danish -ke , Swedish -ka , Faroese -ka . Perhaps relat...
/ˈkeɪ.sɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of kanamycin and bekanamycin derivatives (obtained from Streptomyces kanamyceticus ) used as antibiotics. Contraction of kanamycin .
Infix ( pharmacology ) a shortened allomorph of the monoclonal antibody affix -kin-, used before the affixes -xi- and -zu- for ease of pronunciation

Suffix ( now chiefly dialectal ) Used to form adjectives expressing resemblance or likeness to, similar to -like . (plural -kin ) Used to form nouns having qualities of or belonging to a particular kind, class, or sort. ( now archaic ) Forming diminutives of nouns. Synonyms: ( suffixes ) -cule, -ee, -el, -et, -ette, -icle, -ie, -le, -let, -ling, -ole, -ule, -y, ( prefixes ) mini-, micro-, nano- From Middle English -kin , -kinne , -kunne , from Old English cynna , the genitive plural ...

Suffix Used to form nouns denoting groups or classes taken collectively from animate nouns From Middle English -kinde , -kunde , -kuinde , alteration (due to the noun kind (“type, class”)) of -kin , -kun , -cun , from Old English cynn (“family, race”), see kin. Most uses appear to have been formed by analogy with mankind .
Suffix Forms names of specific cytokines. From -kinesis or directly from Ancient Greek κινέω (kinéō, “to move”).
Suffix Forms words related to body movement, often pathological, sometimes synonymous to words ending in -kinesis . From Ancient Greek κίνησις (kínēsis, “motion”). Compare kinesia (sense 2).
/kɪˈniːsɪs/ Suffix movement, motion Borrowed from Ancient Greek κίνησις (kínēsis, “motion, movement”), from κινέω (kinéō, “I move, put in motion”).
Suffix indicates a type of swimwear By extraction from bikini, in interpreting the "bi" as a prefix bi-, creating a new suffix (a libfix).
/ˈkɪn.ɹə/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of interleukin receptor antagonists. From (interleu)kin r(eceptor) +‎ a(ntagonist) .
Suffix Forms (often intentionally childish or twee) diminutives of nouns. From -kin +‎ -s . See -kin .
/kəɹ.ən/, /ˈkaɪ.ɹən/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of renin inhibitors. From aliskiren .
Suffix A flagellate of a given type. From Ancient Greek κοντός (kontós).
Suffix ( anime and manga fandom ) Appended to a young man's name or nickname to indicate familiarity. Borrowed from Japanese くん ( kun ).
Suffix sexual predilection; paraphilia Learned borrowing from Ancient Greek λαγνεία (lagneía, “sexual intercourse; lasciviousness”).
Suffix sexual predilection; paraphilia Learned borrowing from Ancient Greek λαγνεία (lagneía, “sexual intercourse; lasciviousness”).
Suffix Forming nouns denoting abnormal or disordered forms of speech. From Ancient Greek λαλιά (laliá, “talk, chat”).

/lænd/, /lənd/, /lɪnd/ Suffix Used to form the name of a territory, country, or region. Used to form the name of a sphere of activity or interaction. Etymology treeProto-West Germanic *land Old English land Middle English lond English land English -land From land .
Suffix ( humorous ) A fictional or metaphorical place relating to the person or thing being suffixed. From -land +‎ -ia .

Suffix (programming, informal) Combining with the names of programming languages, especially those which may be confused with common English words. (conlanging, linguistics, informal) Combining form of language. From lang , itself a shortened form of language .

/-lə.tə(r)/ Suffix Worshipper of. A back-formation from idolater . Formally from Latin -latres (seen in īdōlolatres ), from Ancient Greek -λάτρης (-látrēs), from λατρεύω (latreúō, “to serve, serve gods”).
Suffix Forming adjectives : worshipping. From -latry , -later +‎ -ous .
/-lətɹi/ Suffix worship of From Late Latin latria , from Ancient Greek λατρεία (latreía, “service; worship”).

/l̩/, /əl/ Suffix A frequentative suffix of verbs, indicating repetition or continuousness: Forming adjectives from verbs with the meaning of "prone to", "tending to", "apt to", "capable of"; compare -ative : Forming diminutives from other nouns. Synonyms: ( suffixes ) -cule, -ee, -el, -et, -ette, -icle, -ie, -kin, -let, -ling, -ole, -ule, -y, ( prefixes ) mini-, micro-, nano- Forming agent nouns from verbs: Etymology treeProto-Germanic *-lōną Old English -lian Middle English...

/lɛkt/ Suffix ( linguistics ) A (language) variety. From the terminal element of dialect , thus representing the Ancient Greek element -λεκτος (-lektos), ultimately from λέγω (légō, “I say or speak”).

Suffix ( no longer productive ) A suffix meaning “meadow” present in various given names and toponyms of Old English origin From Old English lēah (“meadow, pasture, field, clearing”) (modern English lea , leigh ), used in various compound names, especially place-names.
Suffix a specified form of possession (noun sense 5 or noun sense 6), fit or seizure From Ancient Greek λῆψις (lêpsis, “seizure”) and epilepsy , which is from Ancient Greek ἐπιληψίᾱ (epilēpsíā).
Suffix ( palynology ) Having pollen grains with one or more leptomata, thin edges near the pole

Suffix Of or relating to a condition of seizing, as in nympholeptic . A person who is afflicted with a condition prone to cause violent seizures, as in epileptic . ( chemistry ) Describing a coordination compound having some type(s) of ligand. From Ancient Greek λῆψις (lêpsis, “taking hold, seizing”) + -ic , as in Ancient Greek ληπτικός (lēptikós).
Infix ( pharmacology ) a monoclonal antibody that targets an infectious lesion From lesion.

/ləs/, /lɪs/ Suffix Lacking (something); without (something). Added usually to a noun to form an adjective signifying a lack of that noun. Usage Note Adjectives formed using -less often form nouns by the addition of -ness (e.g. helplessness ), but generally do not form nouns by the addition of other noun-forming endings. A notable exception to the usual usage of this suffix is that doubtless is usually an adverb, rather than an adjective. From Middle English -les , -leas , from Old English -lēas...

/-lət/ Suffix A diminutive suffix. Synonyms: ( suffixes ) -cule, -ee, -el, -et, -ette, -icle, -ie, -kin, -le, -ling, -ole, -ule, -y, ( prefixes ) mini-, micro-, nano- Piece; as in a suit of armor. From Middle English -let , -elet , from Old French -elet , a double diminutive from Old French -el + -et .
/ˈlɪ/ Infix ( pharmacology ) a shortened allomorph of the monoclonal antibody affix -lim-, used before the affixes -xi- and -zu- for ease of pronunciation
Suffix Used to form intensified adjectives indicating deliciousness, from nouns and adjectives. Synonyms: -riffic, -tacular, -tastic Rebracketing of delicious .
Suffix Obsolete spelling of -ly .
Suffix comparative form of -ly : more -ly
Suffix superlative form of -ly : most -ly

Suffix Resembling, having some of the characteristics of ( used to form adjectives from nouns ). ( dialectal ) Used to form adverbs from adjectives or nouns; alternative of -ly. Usage Note Words formed with like are often spelled with a hyphen. This is particularly the case with British spelling more so than American spelling, where it is somewhat more common to form the word without a hyphen. Though -like shares many meanings with -ly, -ly is most often paired with animate nouns while -like...
/ˈlɪm/ Infix ( pharmacology ) a monoclonal antibody that is an immunomodulator
Suffix ( limnology ) A part of a stratified lake. From Ancient Greek λιμνίον (limníon, “small pond”), from λίμνη (límnē, “lake, marsh”).

From Middle English -ling , from Old English -ling , from Proto-West Germanic *-ling , from Proto-Germanic *-lingaz , a nominal suffix, probably composed of Proto-Germanic *-ilaz (agent/instrumental/diminut...

Suffix ( now UK dialectal ) forming adverbs, generally of condition or situation From Middle English -linges , variant (with genitive -es ) of Middle English -ling (adverbial suffix), equivalent to -ling +‎ -s . Compare Dutch -lings (adverbial suffix), German -lings .

/-ˌlaɪt/ Suffix Used to form the names of rocks and minerals. Representing Ancient Greek λίθος (líthos, “stone”). This etymology is incomplete. You can help Wiktionary by elaborating on the origins of this term. Particularly: “via French?”
Suffix Related to stone. -lith +‎ -ic

Suffix ( no longer productive ) Action or proceeding, practice, ritual From Middle English -lok , -lak , -lac , from Old English -lāc (suffix denoting activity or action), from Proto-Germanic *laiką (“play, sport, fight, activity”), *laikaz (“game, dance, hymn, sport”), from Proto-Indo-European *leyg- (“to bounce, shake, tremble”). The etymology of the suffix is the same as that of the noun lāc "play, sport", also "sacrifice, offering", corresponding to obsolete Modern English lake (...
/ˌlɑɡ/, /ˌlɔɡ/ Suffix US standard spelling of -logue .
Suffix Synonym of -logist .
Suffix Forms names of speech disorders. Features in Latin names of rhetoric figures. From Ancient Greek -λογία (-logía, “speech”).
Suffix -logical

/ˈlɒ.d͡ʒə.kəl/, /ˈlɒ.d͡ʒɪ.kəl/ Suffix Used to form adjectival forms of nouns ending in -logy . Usage Note Relatively few terms are directly derived using this suffix. Terms ending in logical are often derived from words formed in other languages or by suffixing -ical to a word ending in logy , as biological = biology + -ical and genealogical = genealogy + -ical .

/-lədʒɪst/ Suffix A person who studies or is an expert in the related -logy. Synonym: -loger Usage Note Relatively few terms are directly derived using this suffix. Terms ending in -logist are often derived from words formed in other languages or by suffixing -ist to a word ending in -logy , as dermatologist = dermatology + -ist and genealogist = genealogy + -ist .

/ˌlɑɡ/, /ˌlɒɡ/, /ˌlɔɡ/ Suffix Speech, or a way of speaking, a narrative, logical discourse. Synonym: -logy ( sciences ) A correspondent (homologue or analogue). (obsolete, archaic, dated, rare or historical) Synonym of -logist . ( rare ) Synonym of -logy . Back-formation from dialogue . Doublet of -alogue . Back-formation from analogue or homologue . From French -logue , from Ancient Greek -λόγος (-lógos).

/-lə.d͡ʒi/ Suffix A branch of learning; a study of a particular subject. Speech, or a way of speaking, a narrative, logical discourse. The English -logy suffix originates with loanwords from the Greek, usually via Latin and French, where the suffix -λογία (-logía) is an integral part of the word loaned. E.g. astrology from astrologia , since the 16th century. The French -logie is a continuation of Latin -logia , ultimately from Ancient Greek -λογία (-logía). Within Greek, the suffix is an -ία (-...
Suffix Forms adjectives indicating length or duration when suffixed to nouns. From long .

/ləkwi/ Suffix ( forming nouns ) speaking, speech Usage Note Terms formed with this suffix are invariably stressed on the antepenult. Extracted from Latin compounds such as soliloquium , vaniloquium .

/lɔɹd/, /lɔːd/ Suffix Someone who exercises power through a particular means. (slang, usually derogatory) forms intensifiers of an epithet or insult (slang, usually derogatory) Someone who engages in a disapproved activity. From lord . Possibly a generalization from gaylord .
Suffix Transparent in radiometry or sonometry. From translucent . Confer also lucent (“translucent”).
/luː.kæst/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of leukotriene receptor antagonists. From leuk(otriene) +‎ a(ntagoni)st .
/ˈluː.tə.maɪd/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of nonsteroid antiandrogens.

/li/ Suffix Used to form adjectives from nouns, the adjectives having the sense of "behaving like, or having a nature typical of what is denoted by the noun" Similar in meaning to -like but most often paired with animate nouns. Used to form adjectives from nouns, the adjectives having the sense of "appearing like, resembling, or having the likeness of what is denoted by the noun". Used to form adjectives from nouns specifying time intervals, the adjectives having the sense of "occurring at such ...
Suffix Suffix forming female given names. From the given name Lynn.
/-lɪ.sɪs/ Suffix decomposition or breakdown dissolving disintegration From Ancient Greek λύσις (lúsis, “a loosening”). Compare lysis .

Suffix Word-forming element used in making nouns corresponding to nouns in -lysis and adjectives in -lytic. Borrowed from Ancient Greek λυτός (lutós, “loosed”), verbal adjective of λύειν (lúein, “to unfasten, loose, loosen, untie”).
Suffix Used in adjectives relating to nouns with the suffix -lysis . From -lysis +‎ -tic .

Infix ( humorous ) Adds ironic pseudo-sophistication to an existing word. Probably modeled on existing playful terms where -ma- is the third syllable like thingamabob , thingamajig , rigamarole , razzamatazz , whatchamacallit .

/mæb/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) monoclonal antibody Usage Note an arbitrary prefix to create a unique name (officially monosyllabic) a suffix for the disease state a suffix for the animal source -mab to identify it as a monoclonal antibody (MAb) From MAb , acronym of m(onoclonal) a(nti)b(ody) .
/məki/ Suffix Forming nouns with the sense of ‘battle, fight’. Representing Ancient Greek -μαχία (-makhía), from μάχη (mákhē, “battle, fight; dispute”).

/mæn/, /mən/ Suffix Someone (possibly implied male) who is an expert in an area or who takes part in an activity. Someone (possibly implied male) who is employed or holds a position in an area. Someone (possibly implied male) who has special characteristics relating to a topic or area. Someone (possibly implied male) who has a particular nationality. A man whose name begins with the preceding letter. A ship which has special characteristics relating to a trade or area. Usage Note To more explici...

Suffix to carry out a specified form of divination (fandom slang, video games) A suffix attached to a noun or character's name to create a word for an in-game romance subplot or mechanic. From a rebracketing of romance .

Suffix A practitioner of a specific type of divination. A user of a specified type of magic. ( fandom slang ) A suffix attached to a video game character's name to create a word for a player who romances that character in the game. From necromancer ; equivalent to -mancy +‎ -er . From a rebracketing of romancer .

Suffix Divination. ( fantasy ) Variety of magic, especially that controlling or related to a specific element, substance, or theme. From Latin -mantīa , ultimately from Ancient Greek μᾰντείᾱ (mănteíā, “divination”).
Verb Combining form of gerrymander .
/meɪn/ Suffix Forming compound nouns with the sense of person who has a mania for. Synonym: -maniac Borrowed from French -mane , from Ancient Greek -μανής (-manḗs).
Suffix compulsion or obsession. A place where something can be found in great amounts. See mania .
/-ˈmeɪ.niˌæk/ Suffix Forms nouns indicating a person who experiences a mania (a type of compulsion or obsession). Synonym: -mane See maniac .

Suffix (obsolete, UK, thieves' cant) Appended to adjectives to form nouns meaning the state of being (the adjective) , the quality of being (the adjective) , or the measure of being (the adjective) . (obsolete, UK, thieves' cant) Substitute for part of another word to disguise meaning. Cognate with English -ment , French -ment and Italian -mente .

Suffix Expertise, involvement, or special status in an area. Etymology treeEnglish -man Proto-West Germanic *-skapi Old English -sċiepe Middle English -schipe English -ship English -manship From -man + -ship . /ˈmæn.tə.diːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of adamantane derivatives. From amantadine .
Suffix Forms adjectives corresponding to nouns ending in -mancy . From Latin ‑manticus , from -mantīa + -icus . Compare Ancient Greek ‑μάντικος (‑mántikos).
Infix ( pharmacology ) a monoclonal antibody that targets mammary tumors From mammary .

Suffix Indicates holidays or sacred days. From Middle English -masse , -messe , from Old English -mæsse , the second element in several ritual and holiday names (e.g. Candelmæsse , Cristes mæsse , hlāfmæsse , etc.), from Old English mæsse (“the mass, church festival”). Compare Dutch mis , German Messe , Danish messe , Swedish mässa , Icelandic messa .
Suffix An abnormality of the breast. From Ancient Greek μαστός (mastós, “breast”).

/ˈmæs.tɪks/ Suffix (archaic, literary, uncommon) Suffix denoting a strong hatred and opposition of whatever thing the suffix is attached to. Borrowed from Ancient Greek μάστῐξ (mástĭx, “whip, scourge, plague”).
Suffix Used to form nouns having the sense of "a comrade or fellow with whom what is denoted by the noun is shared". From the noun mate (“fellow, comrade, partner”).
Suffix Denotes maximum dimensions of a vessel that can travel through the given canal. Clipping of maximum . Clipping of maximize .

/-mæks/ Suffix ( incel slang ) Forms verbs denoting an enhancement or improvement, with the goal of "maximizing" one's attractiveness, appeal, or sexual market value. (Internet slang, loosely, ironic) Forms verbs denoting the maximization of a certain aspect of one's lifestyle. Clipping of maximize , with a superfluous x presumably to mimic Internet slang. Compare doxx . Etymology treeProto-Italic *magisVmos Latin maximus Proto-Indo-European *-id- Proto-Indo-European *-yéti P...
Infix ( pharmacology ) a shortened allomorph of the monoclonal antibody affix -mel-, used before the affixes -xi- and -zu- for ease of pronunciation

/miːl/ Suffix ( rare or no longer productive ) Used to denote a fixed number, measure, or amount at a time. Denotes a powder made of ground cereal. From Middle English -mele , from Old English -mǣlum (“at times”), from Proto-Germanic *mēlamiz , instrumental case of Proto-Germanic *mēlą (“measure; time”). Cognate with Saterland Frisian -moal , Dutch -maal , German Low German -maal , German -mal . More at meal. From Middle English mele , from Old English melu (“meal, flour”), from ...
Suffix enlargement From Ancient Greek μεγάλος (megálos), further μέγας (mégas).

/ˈmaɪstə/, /ˈmaɪstɚ/ Suffix An expert on the specified subject. A person in charge of a specified thing. Attached to a person's name in humorous (or ironic) approbation. Usage Note Sometimes used to point up a German association. From meister , from German Meister (“master”, “champion”).
Infix ( pharmacology ) a monoclonal antibody that targets melanomas From melanoma .
Suffix A condition of the limb(s).

Suffix Attached to a verb to form a noun meaning the action denoted by the verb or its result. Usage Note Generally attached to stem without changes, except when the stem ends in -dge, where the -e is sometimes dropped, as in abridgment , acknowledgment , judgment , and lodgment , with the forms without -e being preferred in American English. Of these, judgment is the most significant, and usage varies globally; see Judgment: Spelling for discussion. From Middle English -ment , from Old French -...

Suffix ( chemistry ) Used to form words relating to chemical structure, denoting parts of a molecule, for example, monomer (one part), dimer (two parts), polymer (many parts). Etymology treeProto-Indo-European *(s)mer- der.Proto-Hellenic *(h)méros Ancient Greek μέρος (méros)lbor.English -mer Learned borrowing from Ancient Greek μέρος (méros, “part”).
Suffix Forming nouns with the sense of part, segment A lakeside settlement; combining form of mere. Identical with moor .

/-məɹəs/ Suffix (biology, forming adjectives) Having parts of the specified quality. (chiefly botany and zoology, forming adjectives) Comprising the specified number of parts. Usage Note In biological descriptions, it is common to use numbers instead the Greek prefixes. From French -mère and its etymon Ancient Greek -μερής (-merḗs, “having (the specified number of) parts”, “sharing”): the combining form of μέρος (méros, “part”)) with the English suffix -ous ; compare the Engl...
/ˈmɛ.steɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of aromatase inhibitors. From [Term?] +‎ (andro)stane .
/ˈmɛt.əs.ɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of indometacin derivatives used as anti-inflammatory agents. From indometacin .
/ˈmɛt.ə.səʊn/, /ˈmɛt.ə.zəʊn/, /ˈmɛt.əˌsoʊn/, /ˈmɛt.əˌzoʊn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of prednisone and prednisolone derivatives. From meth- +‎ -a- +‎ (predni)s(ol)one .

/-mə.tə/, /-mɪ.tə/, /-mɪ.təɹ/, /-mɪ.təɾ/, /-mɪ.ʈəɾ/, /-mɪ.ʈɜː(ɾ)/ Suffix Used to form the names of measuring devices. Usage Note Whereas the spelling of the unit of length varies between British ( metre ) and American ( meter ) English, the spelling of this suffix is -meter in both, while -metre is a nonstandard alternative form found in British English. Words with the suffix - o meter (compare -o- ) always have a primary stress on the antepenultimate syllable: ba rom eter , speed om eter , ta c...
/ˈmɛθ.ə.səʊn/, /ˈmɛθ.ə.zəʊn/, /ˈmɛθ.əˌsoʊn/, /ˈmɛθ.əˌzoʊn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of prednisone and prednisolone derivatives. From meth- +‎ -a- +‎ (predni)s(ol)one .
/ˈmɛt.ɹɪk/ Suffix Forming adjectives corresponding to nouns in -meter . Of or relating to measurement.
Suffix Forms nouns relating to measurement or the study of measurement. From the noun metric.

Suffix Forming nouns relating to measures and measurement. From Old French -métrie , from Latin -metria , from Ancient Greek -μετρία (-metría), from μέτρον (métron, “measurement”) + -ίᾱ (-íā, “-y: forming abstract nouns ”). Equivalent to -meter +‎ -y .
/ˈmaɪ.sɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of aminoglycosides, antibiotics obtained from various Micromonospora . Blend of translingual Micromonospora +‎ -mycin .
Suffix ( limnology ) Refers to the manner in which a body of water stratifies. From Ancient Greek μίξις (míxis, “mingling”). First used in holomictic .
/mɪ.fiːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of clomifene derivatives used as antiestrogens or estrogen receptor modulators. From clomifene .
Suffix Used in the names of several twisty puzzles. Uncertain, perhaps a back-formation of Pyraminx , coined by Uwe Mèffert.

Suffix ( rare ) Hatred; dislike; aversion. From Ancient Greek μῖσος (mîsos, “hatred”) +‎ -ia . First attested in the 1950s, in iatromisia (“hatred of doctors”). Devised as an alternative to the suffix -phobia , which etymologically (and clinically) denotes fear, though it is also widely used in English to denote hatred. Compare -misic (“hating something”), -misiac (“one who hates something”).

Suffix ( rare ) Hating (something); averse to (something). From -misia (or its root, Ancient Greek μῖσος (mîsos, “hatred”)) +‎ -ic . Devised as an alternative to the suffix -phobic ; see -misia for more.
Suffix (paper, printing) Used to form page and book sizes. From Latin ending and apparent suffix of cardinal numbers.
/məbiːl/ Suffix Used to form nouns meaning a particular type of vehicle, particularly one to transport the person or thing described by the word to which the suffix is attached.

/-moʊ.də/, /-moʊ.dəɹ/ Suffix ( transgender slang ) Forms nouns relating to a person who is presenting in a given manner regardless of their gender identity. From mode +‎ -er , modelled after boymoder and girlmoder .
Suffix (Internet slang, incel slang) Forms verbs denoting a situation where someone dominates someone else in the specified characteristic, making that person feel unattractive and inferior.

Suffix Forming a placename or a surname derived therefrom and denoting or connoting a hill or mountain; sometimes (as in estate names) used more for euphony and connotation than for geomorphic denotation. Word formation in English using the suffix to name towns and family estates was most productive in the mid-19th through mid-20th centuries. From French -mont , from French mont (“hill, mountain”).

Suffix Deadjectival suffix used to form an abstract noun usually from personal adjectives. Denominal and deverbal suffix used to form a noun designating a legal status, estate, property. Learned borrowing from Latin -mōnium , -mōnia .

Suffix ( archaic ) Used to form a comparative of certain adjectives and adverbs, usually ending in -er. Used for placenames; variant of moor. Usage Note No longer productive in contemporary English except archaically. From Middle English -more , -mor , from mor (“more”, adverb). Analogically formed to -most . Identical with moor .
/moʊˈɹɛl.ɪn/, /məʊˈɹɛl.ɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of growth hormone release–stimulating peptides. From [Term?] +‎ -relin (“pituitary hormone release–stimulating peptide”).
Suffix shape, form, structure morpheme
Suffix having a specific shape or form From Ancient Greek μορφή (morphḗ, “form, shape”) + -ic , equivalent to -morph +‎ -ic .
Suffix The state of having a specified shape or form. From Ancient Greek μορφή (morphḗ, “form, shape”) +‎ -ism , equivalent to -morph +‎ -ism .

/ˈmɔːfi/ Suffix Shaped or formed. From Ancient Greek μορφή (morphḗ, “shape”), partly after -μορφία (-morphía).

/ˌməʊst/ Suffix Furthest; -est; used to form superlatives of certain adjectives, especially directional and inherently-comparative ones . Usage Note Productivity of the suffix is limited in contemporary English. From Middle English -most(e) , from earlier -mest , from Old English -(e)mest , from Proto-Germanic *-umistaz , from the comparative suffix *-umô (from Proto-Indo-European *-mHo- ) + the regular superlative suffix *-istaz (whence English -est ). The Middle English form is...
Infix ( vulgar ) An intensifier.
/mə.taɪd/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of immunological agents for active immunization. From [Term?] +‎ -tide (“peptide, glycopeptide”).
Suffix Relating to motion or motor functions.
Infix ( pharmacology ) a shortened allomorph of the monoclonal antibody affix -mul-, used before the affixes -xi- and -zu- for ease of pronunciation
Infix ( pharmacology ) a monoclonal antibody that targets the musculoskeletal system From musculoskeletal .

Suffix (biology, mycology) Used to form words used for categorising fungi based on their evolutionary origin, life history, growth form, or ecological preferences. From Ancient Greek μύκης (múkēs, “mushroom, fungus, mushroom-shaped objects”).

/ˈmaɪ.sɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of antibiotics produced by Streptomyces strains. ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of antibiotics that pertain to fungi From streptomycin . From Ancient Greek μύκης (múkēs, “fungus”).

/(ə)nt/ Suffix (no longer productive, unless humorous) Negates the meaning of the clause in which it occurs. Usage Note This contraction does not receive stress, even for contrast: either you DID or you DIDn't. The suffix -n’t can only be added to auxiliary verbs, including dare and need in certain uses (see daredn't ), as well as main verbs be (in almost all uses) and have (in some uses). Indeed, in some dialects, not all auxiliary verbs accept -n’t ; for example, mayn’t is present in some ...
Suffix ( Mid-Ulster ) -n't (slang, on a verb) -ing to (with infinitive); indicates the grammatical present continuous active mood Inherited from Middle English ne . Contraction of -ing to.
/næb/, /nəb/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of cannabinoid receptor agonists. From cannabinol .
Suffix Denoting a hurricane or a whirl containing or made of the stem word. From the latter part of tornado .

/nəˈkɪn.ɹə/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of interleukin-1 receptor antagonists. Probably from Ancient Greek (μο)νά(ς) ((mo)ná(s), “solitary, mono-”) +‎ -kinra (“interleukin receptor antagonist”).

Suffix Forming verbs with the sense of "abduct", or sometimes "abscond with". Usage Note Verbs formed with this suffix are typically found in their gerund-participle forms (in -napping ), and in derived agent nouns (in -napper ). Apparently a back-formation from kidnap (see libfix ); ultimately from nap .
Suffix Pressed, closed. From Ancient Greek ναστός (nastós, “pressed”), from the verb νάσσω (nássō).
Suffix ( botany ) relating to a nastic change From Ancient Greek ναστός (nastós, “pressed”), from the verb νάσσω (nássō). By surface analysis, -nast +‎ -y .
Suffix Forms nouns meaning voyager or traveller From Ancient Greek ναύτης (naútēs, “sailor”).
/nəˌvɪə/, /nəˌvɪɹ/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of HIV protease inhibitors used as antivirals. From saquinavir .
Suffix Marks ordinals written in digits when the final term of the spelled number is "second"
/ni/ Suffix ( informal ) Designating acne located on a particular part of the body. Clipping of acne .
Infix ( pharmacology ) a shortened allomorph of the monoclonal antibody affix -ner-, used before the affixes -xi- and -zu- for ease of pronunciation
Suffix ( chiefly biology ) Characteristic of, pertaining to, or possessing a filiform structure. From Ancient Greek νῆμᾰ (nêmă, “thread”). Compare nemat- .
Suffix (medicine, uncommon) Pertaining to or connected with a kidney. From Ancient Greek νεφρός (nephrós, “kidney”) +‎ -y .
Infix ( pharmacology ) a monoclonal antibody that targets the nervous system From ner vous system .
/nəs.ɛpt/, /nɚˌsɛpt/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of tumor necrosis factor receptor ligands. Blend of ne(c)rosis +‎ recept(or) .

/nəs/, /nɛs/, /nɪs/ Suffix Appended in general, often informally, stylistically, or jocularly, for reification of an attribute. Appended to adjectives to form nouns meaning "the state of being (the adjective)", "the quality of being (the adjective)", or "the measure of being (the adjective)". Appended to words of other parts of speech to form nouns (often nonce words or terms in philosophy) meaning the state/quality/measure of the idea represented by these words. Usage Note If an adjective ends ...
Suffix Obsolete spelling of -ness .
/nət.ənt/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of neurokinin NK 3 receptor antagonists. From ne(uro)- +‎ -tant (“neurokinin receptor antagonist”).
/nək.lɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Nicotinic cholinergic receptor partial agonist/agonist. Probably blend of nicotine +‎ choline .
/ˈnaɪ.dəˌzoʊl/, /ˈnʌɪ.də.zəʊl/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of metronidazole derivatives used as antiprotozoals and radiosensitizers.
/ˈnɪd.əˌpiːn/, /ˈnɪd.ɪp.iːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of nifedipine derivatives used as calcium channel blockers. From contraction of nifedipine .

Suffix ( sometimes derogatory ) Used to form nouns denoting a person who exemplifies, endorses, or is associated with a specified thing or quality, often a particular ideology or preference. Usage Note The plural -niki is usually only used in contexts related to Russia or the former Soviet Union; e.g., beatniki , peaceniki , refuseniki . Terms from Yiddish may also have plurals in -nikim ; e.g., Chabadnikim , Likudnikim , kibbutznikim . From the Slavic suffix represented by Russian -ник (-ni...
Suffix (algebra, mathematics) Denotes an expression with a specified number of terms. Derived from Ancient Greek νομός (nomós, “portion, part”), compare binomial .

Suffix Used to form nouns, referring to a book of knowledge on the specified topic. Most likely from the name of the fictional Necronomicon , or from Ancient Greek νομῐκόν (nomĭkón), the neuter form of νομικός (nomikós, “relating to laws”) (also used in the titles of various real works, like the Nomicon of Photius, or the 18th century Nomicon Procheiron of Michael Photinopoulos).

Suffix The rules of a discipline. ( economics ) Used to form nouns meaning the economics, economic practises, or economic philosophy of a specified person or state From Ancient Greek νόμος (nómos, “law”). The addition of an English prefix thus indicates the "laws" of the discipline or area denoted by the prefix. By surface analysis, -nomy +‎ -ics . Back-formation from economics .
/nə.mi/ Suffix a system of rules, laws, or knowledge about a body of a particular field; distribution, arrangement, management , from Ancient Greek νόμος (nómos, “law, custom”).
Usage Note Most of the words that end in -nym end in -onym , which is the proper suffix from the Greek ὄνυμα (ónyma). See -onym § Etymology .

/oʊ/, /əʉ/, /əʊ/ Suffix ( historical ) A vocative or exclamative particle appended to street cries, nautical calls, or names to project a hail or shout. A colloquializing suffix, typically appended to names, abbreviations of long words, or substantive uses of adjectives. ( humorous ) Converts certain words to faux Italian or Spanish. Can be used with Spanish el for expressions such as el stinko . Added to verb stems to create a noun describing an error relating to the action described by the ver...
Suffix Appended to titles of (often fictional) machines, especially to suggest that the verb to which the suffix is appended is its primary function. Back-formation from automatic .
Suffix ( humorous ) Forms names for real or imaginary dinosaurs; see -saurus.
Suffix Used to convert the name of an atom or ion to an adjective that means containing cations or anions of that atom or ion. Suffixal construction from -o(us) +‎ -an .
Suffix ( organic chemistry ) Used to form the names of salts and esters of carboxylic acids e.g. benzoate, hexanoate From -oic +‎ -ate .
Suffix Indicating a life-form, often microscopic Abstracted from aerobe and microbe , both formed in French using the Ancient Greek word βίος (bíos, “life”).

Suffix Forming nouns from nouns, originally with a diminutive sense. From Middle English -ock , -ok , -uk , -uck , from Old English -oc , -uc (diminutive suffix), from Proto-West Germanic *-uk , from Proto-Germanic *-ukaz (diminutive suffix). Akin to Old Saxon -ik (diminutive suffix), German -ich , Middle Low German -ken (diminutive suffix), Middle Dutch -kin , Old Norse -kr (diminutive suffix). More at -kin.
Suffix Plus some indeterminate fraction not amounting to the next higher round number or significant digit; and change; -some.

Suffix Way, path, travel. Way, means, method, system. Electrode, electrical conductor used to make contact with a non-metallic part of a circuit. Characterized by. Denoting abundance. Resembling, denoting likeness. Etymology treeProto-Indo-European *sed- der.Proto-Indo-European *sodós ?Proto-Hellenic *hodós Ancient Greek ὁδός (hodós)bor.English -ode Borrowed from Ancient Greek ὁδός (hodós, “road, path, way, journey, travel, means, method, system”). From Ancient Greek -ώδης (-ṓdēs, “f...
Suffix tooth; toothed From the oblique stem of Ancient Greek ὀδούς (odoús).

/əʊˈdɒntɪə/, /əˈdɑnt͡ʃi.ə/, /əˈdɑnʃə/, /əˈdɒntɪə/ Suffix ( dentistry and medicine ): ( forms nouns ) branch of dentistry ( forms nouns ) condition of the teeth Formed from the suffixation of ὀδόντ- (odónt-), the stem of the Ancient Greek ὀδούς (odoús, “tooth”), with the English suffix -ia ; compare the Latin -odontia and the French -odontie .
Suffix ( medicine ) pain From international scientific vocabulary, reflecting a New Latin combining form, from Ancient Greek ὀδύνη (odúnē, “sorrow, grief, anguish, unhappiness”).

/ˈiʃəs/, /ˈiːʃəs/ Suffix Forms adjectives describing the arrangement of sexual organs. ( biology ) Forms adjectives describing the host of an organism. From Ancient Greek οἰκία (oikía, “house”) +‎ -ous .

Suffix ( suffixed to a bare infinitive ) A competition [of the activity denoted by the word it combines with], especially a final, tiebreaker, or repechage within a broader tournament. Back-formation from playoff , a deverbal, as the archetype. See -ov .
Suffix ( organic chemistry ) used to form the names of carboxylic groups and acids Clipped from benzoic.

/-oɪ̯d/, /-ɔ(ː)jɖ/, /-ɔɪ̯d/ Suffix Resembling; having the likeness of (usually including the concept of not being the same despite the likeness, but counterexamples exist). ( less commonly ) Of, pertaining to, or related to. Added to nouns to create derogatory terms, typically referring to a particular ideology or group of people. ( category theory ) Added typically to the name of an algebraic structure, to denote the horizontal categorification of that structure. Usage Note “-oid” may be suffix...

/ɑl/, /ɒl/, /ɔl/ Suffix ( organic chemistry ) An alcohol or phenol. Indicating an oily substance Shortened from alcohol . From Latin oleum (“oil, olive oil”), from Ancient Greek ἔλαιον (élaion, “olive oil”).

Suffix Used to form the names of several commercial companies and products. ( entertainment industry ) Used to form pejorative terms associated with commercial bribery. Used to form humorous and pejorative words. Used to form words relating to oil or oiliness From Latin -ola , -ula , feminine form of -ulus , -lus (diminutive suffix). From Latin oleum (“oil, olive oil”).

Suffix ( organic chemistry ) in the Hantzsch–Widman nomenclature for naming heterocycles, denotes an unsaturated five-membered ring system with nitrogen A diminutive suffix referring to small parts of organisms or their cells. Synonyms: ( suffixes ) -cule, -ee, -el, -et, -ette, -icle, -ie, -kin, -le, -let, -ling, -ule, -y, ( prefixes ) mini-, micro-, nano- Partly arbitrary variant of -ol (now used to distinguish between specific cases) and partly from Latin oleum (“oil”). From Latin -olus, from ...

Suffix Indicating a saturated 5-ring heterocyclic compound with nitrogen IUPAC Commission of Nomenclature for Heterocyclic Compounds (1957) This etymology is incomplete. You can help Wiktionary by elaborating on the origins of this term.

Suffix (organic chemistry, obsolete) Indicating a 5-ring heterocyclic compound with nitrogen and one double bond Usage Note Names with the suffix -oline are deprecated by IUPAC. IUPAC Commission of Nomenclature for Heterocyclic Compounds (1957) This etymology is incomplete. You can help Wiktionary by elaborating on the origins of this term.

/ˈɑləd͡ʒi/, /ˈɒləd͡ʒi/ Suffix ( often humorous ) added to an ordinary English word to create a name for a (possibly non-existent) field of study. Etymology treeAncient Greek -ο- (-o-)der.Latin -o- bor.English -o- Ancient Greek -λογῐ́ᾱ (-logĭ́ā)bor.Latin -logia lbor.French -logie bor.English -logy English -ology From -o- (interconsonantal vowel) + -logy . Etymology treeAncient Greek -ο- (-o-)der.Latin -o- bor.English -o- Proto-Indo-European *leǵ- Ancient Greek λόγος (lógos)Proto-Indo-Euro...

/ə.lɒl/, /əˌlɔl/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of compounds with the structure Ar–OCH 2 CH(OH)CH 2 NH–R used as β-adrenoceptor antagonists. From propranolol . By surface analysis, reduplication of -ol .
/ə.ləʊn/, /əˌloʊn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of steroids or steroidlike drugs that are not prednisolone derivatives.

/ˈo.mə/, /ˈoʊ.mə/, /ˈoː.mə/, /ˈɐʉ.mə/, /ˈəʉ.mə/, /ˈəʊ.mə/ Suffix ( pathology ) Forming nouns indicating disease or morbidity. (pathology, specifically) Forming nouns indicating tumors or masses, which may be non-neoplastic or (more often) neoplastic, and (if the latter) either benign or malignant (cancerous); in accord with present-day understanding of histopathology, the suffix is now nonproductive for non-neoplastic senses. Synonyms: -cele, -coele Usage Note For most nouns formed with -oma...
Usage Note See usage notes at -oma.
Suffix Used to form a noun indicating a condition characterized by abnormal growth, tumors, or masses. By surface analysis, -om(a) +‎ -osis .
Suffix Used to form an adjective meaning "possession of or relation to" from nouns ending in -oma or -omata . By surface analysis, -omat(a) +‎ -ous .

/ˌom/, /ˌoʊm/, /ˌoːm/, /ˌɐʉm/, /ˌəʉm/, /ˌəʊm/ Suffix A mass of something. ( biology ) The complete whole of a class of substances for a species or an individual. Alteration of -oma , from Ancient Greek -ωμα (-ōma). Only partially cognate to -some (“body”), from σῶμα (sôma, “body”), in that both share the case ending -μα (-ma), but the ω is unrelated. Back-formation from mitome , reinforced by chromosome . Early examples include biome (1916) and genome , from German Genom (1920). Some association...

Suffix Forming compound nouns describing "the action or process of measuring" something, frequently with corresponding nouns in -ometer . From the ending of words such as geometry ; compare -o- , -metry .
Suffix Obsolete form of -oma . From Latin -omia , from Ancient Greek -ωμία (-ōmía).
Suffix Forms adjectives meaning "related to the study of".

Suffix ( chiefly biology ) Forms nouns meaning “a study of the totality of something”. From -ome (“whole of class”) +‎ -ics , both via international scientific vocabulary and New Latin ultimately from Ancient Greek.
/-ɑməˌnoʊ/, /-ɒmɪnəʊ/ Suffix A tile with a specified number of connected squares. Rebracketing of domino , as if the d were for di- (“two”), a domino being made up of two squares.
Suffix Used to form a verb from nouns ending in -oma . By surface analysis, -oma +‎ -ize .
Suffix Obsolete form of -oma . From Latin -omia , from Ancient Greek -ωμία (-ōmía).

/-ɒn/, /-ən/ Suffix (physics, mathematics) Forming nouns denoting subatomic particles and quanta. ( chemistry ) Forming names of noble gases and certain nonmetal elements. (biology, genetics) Forming names of things considered as basic or fundamental units. Forms names for chemical compounds, often proprietary. Etymology treeNew Latin ēlectricus bor.English electric Proto-Hellenic *éimi Ancient Greek ἰόν (ión)der.English ion English electron bf.Ancient Greek -ον (-on)influ.English -o...
Suffix Used to form names of conditions involving a tumor, swelling, or mass. From Ancient Greek ὄγκος (ónkos, “tumor”).
/-əʊn/ Suffix a ketone any similar compound containing a carbonyl functional group Clipping of ketone , which was analogous to margarone . Further etymology is unclear.
Suffix ( organic chemistry ) Used to form the names of acids derived from aldoses by the oxidation of the aldehyde group (e.g., gluconic acid).
/ˈɑn.ɪˌdin/, /ˈɒn.ɪ.diːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of clonidine derivatives used as antihypertensives. From clonidine .

Suffix ( particle physics ) Designating an onium particle. ( chemistry ) Used to form the name of polyatomic cations. Common examples are quaternary ammonium compounds used as neuromuscular blocking agents, cholinergic agents, anticholinergic agents, antibacterials/antiseptics, or other agents. From onium or -on +‎ -ium .

Suffix Indicating a life-form or life-stage, especially with regards to reproduction. From Ancient Greek -ων (-ōn), the present participle ending ("-ing"). Scientific usage possibly originates from -biont .

Suffix word name From international scientific vocabulary, reflecting a New Latin combining form, from Ancient Greek ὄνυμα (ónuma), Doric and Aeolic dialectal form of ὄνομα (ónoma, “name”); compare also -onymy § Etymology .

Suffix Used to form nouns describing the study, formation, or use of words or names. From international scientific vocabulary, reflecting a New Latin combining form ( -onymia ), from Ancient Greek ὄνομα (ónoma, “name”)/Ancient Greek ὄνυμα (ónuma, “name”) (whence also onoma ) + -ia ; compare also ὄνομα § Derived terms .
/uːmə(ɹ)/ Suffix (4chan slang, Internet slang) Used to form the names of Wojak characters, or, by extension, caricaturized personifications of a given trait/quality. Back-formation from boomer .
/uːn/ Suffix ( no longer productive ) Forming nouns, mainly in borrowed words. From French -on .
/ˈoʊ.pəˌmin/, /ˈəʊ.pə.miːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of dopaminergic agents and dopamine derivatives, used as cardiac stimulants, antihypertensives or diuretics. From dopamine .
Suffix Eye From Ancient Greek ὠπός (ōpós), genitive of ὤψ (ṓps, “eye”).

Suffix (medicine, ophthalmology) vision (used to form names of visual defects) Attested classically as -ωπία in a few words, such as ἀμβλυωπία (ambluōpía). Apparently equivalent to -οψία (-opsía). By surface analysis, -ope +‎ -ia .

/ˈɒpsɪə/ Suffix ( ophthalmology ) Forming compound nouns denoting specific deficiencies of sight. [from 19th c.] From Ancient Greek -οψία (-opsía), related to ὀπτικός (optikós, “optic, pertaining to sight”). Doublet of -opsy .
Suffix ( rare ) Synonym of -opia (“seeing”). From Ancient Greek ὄψις (ópsis, “aspect, appearance”).
Suffix ( medicine ) examination Borrowed from Ancient Greek -οψίᾱ (-opsíā, “sight, view”).

/ə/, /ɚ/ Suffix Creates an agent noun, often from a verb, indicating a person or object (often machines or parts of them) that do the verb or part of speech with which they are formed. Synonyms: -er, ( casual ) -ster ( electrical science ) Appended to the names of members of classes of components, especially those that have an extensive property name of the same root suffixed with -ance Used to form nouns of quality, state, or condition. From Middle English -our , from Old French -eor , from Lat...

Suffix Used to form, from one noun, a second meaning "wide view of" the first, or (with ironic reference to the preceding sense) "surfeit of", "overattention to", or "exaggerated praise of" the first. Usage Note The suffix is usually appended without hyphenation, or occasionally with hyphen intended to catch the eye. Following a noun ending in a vowel or r sound, the altered form -rama is usually substituted. Back-formation from panorama and cyclorama (which are modern coinages, not Greek words)...
Suffix Related to appetite and eating disorders. From Ancient Greek ὄρεξις (órexis, “appetite”). Modeled on anorexia .

/-ˈoɹ.ɪ.əm/, /-ˈoɾ.ɪ.əm/, /-ˈoːɹ.ə.əm/, /-ˈoːɹ.ɪ.əm/, /-ˈoːɾ.ɪ.um/, /-ˈɔːɹ.ɪ.əm/ Suffix Forming nouns denoting a place for a particular function or thing. Synonym: -arium Learned borrowing from Latin -ōrium . Doublet of -ory .
/ˈɔɹˌfæn/, /ˈɔː.fan/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of morphinan derivates used as opioid receptor antagonists/agonists. Contraction of morphinan .

/ˈɔɹˌfin/, /ˈɔː.fiːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of morphinan derivates used as opioid receptor antagonists/agonists. From -orph- (“morphinan derivate”) +‎ -ine , related to morphine .
/ˈɔɹˌfoʊn/, /ˈɔː.fəʊn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of morphinan derivates used as opioid receptor antagonists/agonists. From -orph- (“morphinan derivate”) +‎ -one .

/-(ə)ɹi/ Suffix Added to nouns and verbs (often Latinate) to form adjectives meaning “of”, “pertaining to”, or “serving for”. Synonyms: -al, -ar, -ary, -ese, -ic, -id, -ish, -like, -oid, -ous, -y Added to nouns and verbs (often Latinate) to form nouns meaning “that which pertains to or serves for”. Synonyms: -age, -al, -arium, -drome, -ery, -ium, -orium From Middle English -orie , from the Latin adjective suffix -tōrius . By surface analysis, -or +‎ -y . From the Latin noun suffix -tōrium . ...

Suffix Used to form plurals of some Hebrew and Yiddish loanwords, usually ending in -a or -ah . From the traditional Ashkenazi pronunciation of Hebrew ־וֹת (“-ót”), and its descendant, Yiddish ־ות (-es).
