Infix ( pharmacology ) a monoclonal antibody that targets bone From Latin os (“bone”).

/-oʊs/, /-oʊz/, /-əʊs/, /-əʊz/ Suffix full of ( chemistry ) Used to form the names of sugars. ( biochemistry ) Used to indicate a product of protein breakdown. Borrowed from Latin -ōsus . Doublet of -ous in stressed position. Back-formation from glucose .
Suffix An alpha-1 adrenergic receptor antagonist

/-oʊ.sɪs/, /-ɐʉ.səs/, /-əʉ.sɪs/, /-əʊ.sɪs/ Suffix ( pathology ) functional disease or condition such as hepatosis process, action such as phagocytosis formation, increase such as leukocytosis Usage Note Corresponding adjectives are formed using -otic . The plural suffix -oses is rarely used for diseases, paralleling the rarity of the plural forms of diseases in -itis , but for words such as diagnosis the plural in -oses is well-established. Learned borrowing from New Latin -ōsis , from Ancient G...

/-ɾi/, /ˈɑ.sə.ti/, /ˈɒ.sɪ.ti/ Suffix Forming nouns, usually abstract, and usually from adjectives in -ous or -ose . ( colloquial ) Forming nouns from other adjectives for humorous effect. From Middle English -ouste , from Old French -ouseté , from Latin -ōsitās ; later modified to the current form. It is equivalent to -ose or -ous + -ity .
Suffix ( medicine ) Smell; odour. From Ancient Greek ὀσμή (osmḗ, “smell, scent, odour”).

Suffix ( biology ) Having or equipped with a certain feature or structure. From Ancient Greek -ωτά (-ōtá), neuter plural of -ωτός (-ōtós), verbal adjective from the stem of -όω (-óō, denominal verb suffix). Related to translingual -ota .
Suffix Used to form plurals of some Hebrew loanwords ending in -a or -ah . From Hebrew ־וֹת (“-ót”).

Suffix ( pathology ) Having disease or abnormal condition. Pertaining to a process or action. Borrowed from French -otique , from Latin -ōticus , from Ancient Greek -ωτικός (-ōtikós), from -όω (-óō)-stem verbs + -τικός (-tikós). Equivalent to -όω (-óō) + -σις (-sis) + -ικός (-ikós).

Suffix (British spelling, Commonwealth spelling) Used to form abstract nouns of state, quality, or action from Latin or French roots. From Middle English -our , from Anglo-Norman -our , -ur , and Old French -or (modern -eur ), from the Latin accusative suffix -ōrem , from -or . The spelling with u reflects the Anglo‐Norman raising of the Old French close‐mid vowel /o/ (which developed from the Latin long vowel /oː/) to a high‐back vowel /u/. In the United States, the spelling -or —which had ...

/-əs/ Suffix Used to form adjectives from nouns, to denote: possession of presence of a quality in any degree (typically an abundance) relation or pertinence to ( chemistry ) Used in chemical nomenclature to name chemical compounds in which a specified chemical element has a lower oxidation number than in the equivalent compound whose name ends in the suffix -ic . For example sulphuric acid (H 2 SO 4 ) has more oxygen atoms per molecule than sulphurous acid (H 2 SO 3 ). See Inorganic nomencl...
Suffix Related through a nonmarital romantic relationship, through a platonic relationship or friendship or through a marriage that ended in divorce. By analogy to -in-law .
Suffix A romanization of the Slavic possessive suffix -ов (-ov).
/ˈɑk.səs.ɪn/, /ˈɒk.səs.ɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of nalidixic acid derivatives used as antibacterials. From cinoxacin .
/ˈɑk.səˌnaɪd/, /ˈɒk.sə.nʌɪd/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of salicylanilides and analogs. Blend of (hydr)oxy +‎ benzan(il)ide .
/ˈɑk.səˌpin/, /ˈɒk.sə.piːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of tricyclic compounds. From benzoxepine .
/ˈɑk.səˌtin/, /ˈɒk.sə.tiːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of fluoxetine derivatives used as serotonin and/or norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors. From fluoxetine .
/ˈɑk.sɪˌfin/, /ˈɒk.sɪ.fiːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of tamoxifen derivatives used as antiestrogens or estrogen receptor modulators. From tamoxifen .
Suffix ( organic chemistry ) An ether functional group, consisting of a carbon chain with an intervening oxygen atom at the attachment point.
/əʊɪl/ Suffix ( chemistry ) Forming the names of acyl radicals from the names of corresponding carboxylic acids.
/pəɡəs/ Suffix ( medicine ) conjoined twins From Ancient Greek πάγος (págos, “fixed, set”), from πήγνυμι (pḗgnumi, “fasten, fix, set”).
/pə.mɪl/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of verapamil derivatives used as calcium channel blockers. From verapamil .

Suffix (informal, usually derogatory) Used with adjectives ending in -y to form nicknames based on a negative quality of a person. Usage Note Usually the adjective must be two syllables long and end in -y , potentially with an interfix if the original adjective ends in a different sound. As with fancypants , usage has not yet settled as to whether this should be separate, linked with a hyphen as a suffixoid, or fused like a normal affix. Extracted from fancypants .
/pə.ɹɪn/, /pɛ.ɹɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of heparin derivatives including low molecular mass heparins. From heparin .
/ˈpɛɹ.ɪ.nʌks/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of synthetic heparinoids. From -parin (“heparin derivative”).

Suffix parous; relating to parity (the number of times a woman has given birth). Causing or producing (the thing indicated by the stem to which the suffix is attached). From Latin -parus , from Latin pariō (“produce, bring forth, give birth”).
/-ˈpɑɹtaɪt/, /-ˈpɑːtaɪt/ Suffix ( forming adjectives ) Having the specified number of parts. Usage Note This suffix is combined with Latinate number prefixes when coining new words. See partite.

Suffix Used to form nouns indicating someone with a particular disorder Used to form nouns indicating someone with a particular capability, as a type of remedial treatment From Ancient Greek πάθος (páthos, “suffering”).
Suffix Forms the names of disorders and diseases affecting a particular part of the body.
/ˈpæθɪk/, /ˈpɛθək/ Suffix Adjectival form of suffix -pathy .
/-pəθi/ Suffix suffering, feeling, emotion damage to, disease of, disorder of, or abnormality therapy, treatment, method, cure, curative treatment
Suffix Indicative of a pause or discontinuance. From Middle French pause , from Latin pausa , from Ancient Greek παῦσις (paûsis, “discontinuance”).
Suffix Referring to the recurring or repeating element in a word describing multiple iterations of an event.
Suffix Foot; having or relating to feet. Partly from French ‑pède and partly from Latin pedis (genitive of pēs (“foot”)).
Suffix A specialized encyclopedia about the prefixed subject. A general encyclopedia in the structure described by the prefix. Back-formation from encyclopedia .
Suffix Sea. From πέλαγος (pélagos, “sea”).
Suffix deficiency
/ˈpɛ.ɹɪ.dɒl/, /ˈpɛɹ.ɪˌdɔl/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of haloperidol derivatives used as antipsychotics. From haloperidol .
/ˈpɛ.ɹɪ.dəʊn/, /ˈpɛɹ.ɪˌdoʊn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of risperidone derivatives used as antipsychotics. From risperidone .
/pə.ɹəʊn/, /pəˌɹoʊn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of 4’-fluoro-4-piperidinobutyrophenone derivatives used as tranquilizers and neuroleptics. From (pi)per(idine) +‎ (butyrophen)one .

Suffix Someone who is an expert in an area. Someone who is employed or holds a position in an area. Someone who has special characteristics relating to a topic or area. ( nonstandard ) Someone of a particular nationality. ( More often, the separate noun "person" is used, e.g. "Norse person". ) person used in compounds.
Suffix travelling towards Formed from the stem of Latin petō, petere (“seek, aim”), with the suffix -al , from Latin -alis .
Suffix Fixing (in place), fastening.

/feɪd͡ʒ/ Suffix eater; something that eats, or consumes. From Latin -phagus , from Ancient Greek φάγος (phágos, “glutton”), from φαγεῖν (phageîn, “to eat”), aorist active infinitive of ἔφαγον (éphagon, “I ate, devoured”).

Suffix eating, biting or swallowing From Ancient Greek -φαγία (-phagía) (compare -φάγος (-phágos, “eater”)), suffix corresponding to φαγεῖν (phageîn, “to eat”), infinitive of ἔφαγον (éphagon, “I eat”), which serves as aorist for the defective verb ἐσθίω (esthíō, “I eat”).
Suffix Used to form adjectives from prefixes, to denote diet types From Ancient Greek -φαγία (-phagía), combining form of φαγεῖν (phageîn, “to eat”), infinitive aorist of ἐσθίω (esthíō, “I eat”).
Suffix One who eats something. Equivalent to -phagy +‎ -ist or -phage +‎ -ist .
/-fəɡəs/ Suffix Used to form adjectives meaning "eating" or "feeding on". Synonym: -vorous From Latin -phagus , from Ancient Greek -φάγος (-phágos, “-eating”), from φαγεῖν (phageîn, “to eat”).

Suffix ( forming nouns ) Feeding on; consumption of. ( forming nouns ) Eating in a specified manner, normal or abnormal. From Ancient Greek -φαγία (-phagía), combining form of φαγεῖν (phageîn, “to eat”), infinitive aorist of ἐσθίω (esthíō, “I eat”).
Suffix ( medicine ) Denoting a condition of the phalange bones in the fingers and toes. From Latin phalanx , with combining form phalang-.
Suffix Appearing; visible; seeming. Learned borrowing from Ancient Greek -φανής (-phanḗs, “having a certain appearance”).

Suffix Forms adjectives describing a material's appearance or the conditions under which this appearance changes. From Ancient Greek -φανής (-phanḗs, “appearing”), from φαίνω (phaínō, “I shine, appear”), compare diaphanous and apophanous .
Suffix Concerning a manifestation or (first) appearance. From Ancient Greek φαίνω (phaínō, “I shine, appear”), modelled after terms such as ἐπιφάνεια (epipháneia, “divine manifestation, epiphany”).
Suffix Used to form the names of disorders and phenomena relating to words and speech. From Ancient Greek φάσις (phásis, “utterance”), from φημί (phēmí, “I say”).
Suffix Synonym of -phasia : forms names of speech disorders. Borrowed from New Latin -phēmia , from Ancient Greek φήμη (phḗmē, “voice”).
/faɪ̯l/ Suffix Forming nouns and adjectives meaning "loving", "friendly", or "friend". Learned borrowing from Latin -phila , from Ancient Greek φίλος (phílos, “dear, beloved”).

/-ˈfiː.li.ə/, /-ˈfɪ.li.ə/ Suffix Used to form nouns meaning liking, love (for something). ( pathology ) Used to form nouns meaning abnormal liking or tendency (for something), e.g. paraphilia. From Ancient Greek φιλία (philía, “(fraternal) love”).

Suffix Forming nouns : Someone with a tendency towards something, or an abnormal attraction to something. Forming adjectives : Relating to a tendency towards something, or an abnormal attraction to something.
Suffix Synonym of -philous . From Ancient Greek φίλος (phílos, “love”) + -ic . By surface analysis, -phile +‎ -ic .

Suffix Synonym of -philia . Etymology treeProto-Indo-European *bʰil- Proto-Indo-European *-os Proto-Indo-European *bʰil-o-s Ancient Greek φίλος (phílos)bor.Latin -phila lbor.English -phile Proto-Indo-European *-id- Proto-Indo-European *-yéti Proto-Indo-European *-idyéti Proto-Hellenic *-íďďō Ancient Greek -ῐ́ζω (-ĭ́zō)Proto-Indo-European *-mos Proto-Indo-European *-mós Ancient Greek -μός (-mós)Ancient Greek -ισμός (-ismós)der.English -ism English -philism From...
Suffix Synonym of -phile . From Ancient Greek φῐ́λος (phĭ́los) +‎ -ist .
Suffix Synonym of -phile . ( mineralogy ) Forming names of minerals associated with a substance. From Ancient Greek φῐ́λος (phĭ́los) +‎ -ite .
Suffix having an affinity, attraction, preference or love of something Synonyms: -phile, -philic From Ancient Greek φίλος (phílos, “love”) + -ous .
Suffix liking for tendency towards (botany) pollination by From Ancient Greek φιλία (philía, “(fraternal) love”).

/-foʊb/ Suffix Used to form nouns denoting a person having a fear of a specific thing. Used to form nouns denoting a person who hates or dislikes a type of person, thing, etc. From French -phobe , from Latin -phobus , from Attic Greek -φόβος (-phóbos), combining form of φόβος (phóbos), ablaut variant of φέβεσθαι (phébesthai), middle infinitive of φέβομαι (phébomai), from Proto-Indo-European *bʰegʷ . Cognates include Russian бегать (begatʹ, “run, flee”), Slovak bežať (“run”), Polish biec (“ru...

/-ˈfoʊ.bi.ə/, /-ˈfəʊ.bi.ə/ Suffix Used to form nouns meaning fear of a specific thing. Used to form nouns meaning hate, dislike, or repression of a specific thing. From New Latin, from Latin, from Ancient Greek -φοβία (-phobía), from φόβος (phóbos, “fear”).

/-ˈfoʊbɪæk/, /-ˈfəʊbɪæk/ Suffix Used to form adjectives indicating a fear of a specific thing. Used to form adjectives indicating a dislike or aversion. Used as a synonym of -phobic to form nouns. From -phobia + -ic . Modelled on -maniac .

/-ˈfoʊbɪk/, /-ˈfəʊbɪk/ Suffix Used to form adjectives indicating a fear of a specific thing. Used to form adjectives indicating a dislike or aversion. Used in biology and chemistry to mean a natural repulsion or avoidance. Used as a synonym of -phobe to form nouns. From -phobia (from Late Latin -phobia , from Koine Greek -φοβία (-phobía), from φόβος (phóbos, “fear”)) +‎ -ic .

/ˈfoʊ̯n/, /ˈfɐ̞ʉ̯n/, /ˈfɔʊ̯n/, /ˈfəʉ̯n/, /ˈfəʊ̯n/ Suffix A type of sound. A device that makes a sound. Forming adjectives: speaking a certain language. Forming nouns: a speaker of a certain language. From Ancient Greek φωνή (phōnḗ, “sound, voice, speech, language”), from Proto-Indo-European *bʰoh₂neh₂ , from *bʰeh₂- (“to speak”); related to fame and fable . Some terms ending in phone , such as textphone , are actually compounds made with phone .

Suffix ( medicine ) Forms nouns describing a condition related to production of voice or reaction to sound. Learned borrowing from Classical Latin -phōnia , from Ancient Greek -φωνία (-phōnía). By surface analysis, -phone +‎ -ia . Doublet of -phony .

Suffix Forms adjectives relating to sound. Synonym: -phonous Partly from -phone +‎ -ic , and partly from -phony +‎ -ic , ultimately from Ancient Greek φωνή (phōnḗ, “sound”). Attested from the 19th century.
Suffix Names electronic systems related to acoustics and recording. Potentially first used in this sense in stereophonics (1958)
Suffix Of the sound, voice, or speech sound of the specified type. Synonym: -phonic Adapted borrowing of Ancient Greek -φωνος (-phōnos) +‎ -ous . By surface analysis, -phone +‎ -ous .

/-fəni/ Suffix Forms nouns related to: sound or its production musical structure language sounds and languages medical auscultation voice (Alternative form of -phonia ) From French -phonie , from Old French -phonie , and its etymon Classical Latin -phōnia , from Ancient Greek -φωνία (-phōnía). By surface analysis, -phone +‎ -y . Doublet of -phonia .
Suffix Bearer, carrier or conveyor. From New Latin -phorus , from Ancient Greek -φόρος (-phóros, “bearing”), a derivative of φέρω (phérō, “to bear, to carry”). See phoresy.

/fəˈɹiː.sɪs/ Suffix transmission or movement of particles Borrowed from Ancient Greek φόρησῐς (phórēsĭs, “an act of bearing, wearing”), from φορέω (phoréō, “to bear repeatedly or habitually; to be carried along”) +‎ -σῐς (-sĭs, action noun suffix).
Suffix Related to phoresis. From the adjective phoretic.
Suffix Carrying, bearing.
Suffix Forms names of speech disorders. From Ancient Greek φράσις (phrásis, “speech”).
Suffix mental disorder. From φρήν (phrḗn, “mind”) +‎ -ia .
Suffix leaf From Ancient Greek φύλλον (phúllon, “leaf”).

Suffix (botany, of plants) Of having a specified number or kind of leaves. (botany, of plants) Having a specified relationship to the leaves. From Ancient Greek φύλλον (phúllon, “leaf”) and Latin Latin -ōsus .
Suffix Forms nouns relating to having a certain type or configuration of leaves. -phyll (“leaf”) +‎ -y
Suffix Tribehood. Learned borrowing from Ancient Greek φυλή (phulḗ).
Suffix Growth or growing. Form or structure. From Ancient Greek φύσις (phúsis, “growth, I bring forth”).

/faɪt/, /fʌɪt/ Suffix A member of a taxonomic group of plants or algae, chiefly one whose taxonomic name ends (or ended) in -phyta . A plant that grows in a specified habitat. A plant that grows in a specified form or habit. A pathological growth. From Ancient Greek φυτόν (phutón, “plant”).
/pɪ.dɛm/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of zolpidem derivatives used as hypnotics/sedatives. From zolpidem .

Suffix ( originally 4chan slang ) Forming adjectives denoting dedication to or a heightened awareness of something. Influenced by redpilled , blackpilled , etc. whose stems refer to the colour of the notional pill rather than their actual effects. Popularized from a scene in the 1999 film The Matrix .
/piːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of tricyclic compounds.
/ˈpɪp.ɹə.zəʊl/, /ˈpɪp.ɹəˌzoʊl/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of phenylpiperazine derivatives used as psychotropics. Blend of (phenyl)pip(e)razine +‎ azole .
/pɪt.ənt/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of neurokinin NK 1 (substance P) receptor antagonists. From pi- (“letter P”) +‎ -tant (“neurokinin receptor antagonist”).
Suffix ( medicine ) Growth or formation From New Latin -plasia , from Ancient Greek πλάσις (plásis, “molding, formation”).
/plæz.əm/ Suffix ( biology ) The contents of a cell. From Ancient Greek πλάσμα (plásma, “something molded”); a doublet of plasma and plasm used mostly in biology.
/plæst/ Suffix A small body, structure, particle, or granule, especially of living matter. A plastid.

/-ˈplæs.ti/ Suffix ( surgery ) Repair or restoration of a part or function. Molding or shaping through a surgical procedure. Derived from Ancient Greek πλᾰστός (plăstós, “molded, formed”). Etymology treeProto-Hellenic *plátʰyō Proto-Hellenic *pláťťō ?Ancient Greek πλᾰ́σσω (plắssō)Proto-Indo-European *-tós Proto-Hellenic *-tós Ancient Greek -τός (-tós)Ancient Greek πλᾰστός (plăstós)der.English -plasty Derived from Ancient Greek πλᾰστός (plăstós, “molded, formed”).
/ˈplæt.ɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of platinum derivatives used as antineoplastic agents. From platinum .

/pəl/ Suffix ( as a noun ) A tuple containing the specified number of terms. ( as an adjective ) Containing the specified number of terms. From Latin -plus (“more, in sum”). From Ancient Greek πόλις (pólis).
Suffix paralysis From paraplegia , from Ancient Greek παραπληγία (paraplēgía), ultimately from πλήσσω (plḗssō, “to strike”). By surface analysis, para- +‎ -plegia .
Suffix Adjectives derived from the related -plegia
/-plɛks/ Suffix Comprising a number of parts, as in duplex . ( mathematics ) ten to the power of the number, as in googolplex .
Suffix (botany, of leaves) Indicating that the main nerves are lateral and arise from a point distinctly above the base of the leaf. Combined with a numerical prefix: 3-plinerved , 5-plinerved , etc.

Suffix (biology, genetics) Indicating the number of homologous sets of chromosomes in a cell. Back-formation from haploid and diploid . Equivalent to Ancient Greek -πλ(όος) (-pl(óos), “-fold”) + -oid .
Suffix The condition or state of having a certain number or type of chromosomes. From -ploid +‎ -y .

/plɒn/, /ˌplɑn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of imidazopyrimidine or pyrazolopyrimidine derivatives used as anxiolytics, sedatives, or hypnotics. Probably contraction of pyrazolopyrimidine .

/-ˈpniːə/ Suffix ( physiology ) breathing, respiration Representing -πνοια (-pnoia), the common terminal element of the Ancient Greek ἄπνοια (ápnoia, “apnea”), δύσπνοια (dúspnoia, “dyspnea”), εὔπνοια (eúpnoia, “eupnea”), ὀρθόπνοια (orthópnoia, “orthopnea”), etc, from πνέω (pnéō, “I blow”) and πνοή (pnoḗ, “breath, breathing”).
Suffix Denoting a catastrophic event caused by or related to the stem word. From the latter part of apocalypse .

Suffix Related to or resembling a foot or similar limb. Having or being a certain kind of foot. Etymology treeProto-Indo-European *pṓds der.Ancient Greek πούς (poús)Proto-Indo-European *-os Proto-Hellenic *-os Ancient Greek -ος (-os)Ancient Greek ποδός (podós)bor.English -pod From Ancient Greek ποδός (podós), genitive singular of πούς (poús, “foot, leg”).
Suffix Having feet; footed. From -pod (“foot”) +‎ -al .
Suffix ( biology ) Relating to or being a leg-like structure. From Ancient Greek πούς (poús, “foot”) +‎ -ium (“biological structure”).
Suffix footed; having a certain number or type of feet or legs From Ancient Greek πούς (poús, “foot”), from Proto-Indo-European *pṓds .
Suffix ( poetry ) A unit or line consisting of a specified number of metrical feet From Ancient Greek -ποδία (-podía) from πούς (poús, “foot”).
/ˈpiːə/ Suffix Used to form nouns that denote the making or creating of something. From Latin -poeia , from Ancient Greek ποιέω (poiéō, “I make”).
Suffix production, creation or formation Suffixed form of poiesis , from Ancient Greek ποίησις (poíēsis), from ποιέω (poiéō, “to make”).
Suffix Relating to production or formation. See -poiesis
Suffix ( Internet slang ) Used with a country code or abbreviation to form a noun denoting the politics of that country. Clipping of politics .
Suffix Forms names of cities or kinds of cities. Learned borrowing from Latin -polis , from Ancient Greek πόλις (pólis, “city”).

Suffix ( economics ) Pertaining to the number of sellers in a market. ( games ) Pertaining to a derivative of the board game Monopoly . Derived from the root of Ancient Greek πωλέω (pōléō, “I sell”), by analogy with monopoly . Unrelated to poly- .
Suffix Forms names of systems of agriculture. From geoponics (“agriculture”), which is ultimately from Ancient Greek γεω- (geō-, “earth”) +‎ πόνος (pónos, “work, toil”) +‎ -ικός (-ikós).

/pu/, /puː/ Suffix Added to nouns, especially personal names, ending in -y or -ie , or with this sound inserted, to form affectionate, playful diminutives. Used for constructing a breed name of designer dogs one of whose parents is a poodle. Unknown, probably arbitrary or from poo . Clipping of poodle .
/poɹ/, /pɔː/ Suffix Used to form the names of cities and towns, especially in South Asia and Southeast Asia. From Sanskrit पुर (púra, “city, town”).
/pə.ɹaɪd/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of sodium–hydrogen antiporter inhibitors. From cariporide .
Suffix ( Internet ) Forming nouns that denote a type of Internet post. ( Internet ) Forming verbs that denote making a type of Internet post. From post .

Suffix ( vulgar ) Creates insults usually implying idiocy or contemptibility. Back-formation from tosspot (“( originally ) one who drinks excessively; ( now ) idiot”), likely reinterpreting it as toss (“masturbation; nonsense”) + -pot .
Infix ( pharmacology ) a shortened allomorph of the monoclonal antibody affix -pro-, used for ease of pronunciation before all source affixes but -xi- and -zu-
/pɹə.miːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of substances of the imipramine group. From imipramine .
Suffix Forms names of impairments of motor control. From Ancient Greek πρᾶξις (prâxis, “action”), whence also praxis .
/pɹə.zəʊl/, /pɹəˌzoʊl/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of benzimidazole derivatives used as antiulcers.
/ˈpɹɛn.ə.liːn/, /ˈpɹɛn.əl.ɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Synonym of -terol .
Suffix ( business ) entrepreneur Back-formation from entrepreneur .
/pɹəˌvɪə/, /pɹəˌvɪɹ/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of hepatitis C virus protease inhibitors used as antivirals. From [Term?] +‎ -vir (“antiviral”).
/pɹaɪd/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of sulpiride derivatives. Clipping of sulpiride .
/pɹɪl/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of angiotensin converting enzyme inhibitors. From captopril .
/pɹɪl.ət/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of diacid analogs of angiotensin converting enzyme inhibitors. From -pril (“angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor”) +‎ -ate (“salt”).
/pɹɪm/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of trimethoprim derivatives acting as dihydrofolate reductase inhibitors and used as antibacterials. From trimethoprim .
/ˈpɹɪ.stɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of streptogramin antibacterials, pristinamycin derivatives acting as protein synthesis inhibitors. From pristinamycin .
Infix ( pharmacology ) a monoclonal antibody that targets prostate tumors From prostate.
/pɹəf.ən/, /ˈpɹoʊ.fən/, /ˈpɹəʊ.fən/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of ibuprofen derivatives used as anti-inflammatory agents. From ibuprofen .

Suffix Added to a noun to form an adjective denoting imperviousness to that noun. Added to a noun to form a verb denoting a process to make something impervious to that noun. Usage Note Often used as the second part of a combined form (such as bullet-proof) rather than as a true suffix (as in waterproof) From the adjective proof (“fully resistant”), from the same Old French root as prove .
/pɹɑst/, /pɹɒst/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of prostaglandins. From prostaglandin .
/ˈpɹɑˌstɪl/, /ˈpɹɒ.stɪl/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of prostaglandins used as antiulcers. Probably from -prost- (“prostaglandin”) +‎ -yl .
Suffix wing From Ancient Greek πτερόν (pterón, “wing”).
Suffix Having wings ( of a specified size, type or number ) From Ancient Greek πτερ(όν) (pter(ón), “wing”) +‎ -ous .

/ptaɪl/, /ptɪl/, /ptʌɪl/ Suffix (botany, forming adjectives and nouns) type of leaf (ornithology, forming nouns) type of plumage Usage Note Words formed with this suffix are traditionally stressed on the penult; however, authorities also cite pronunciations, featuring antepenultimate stress, which have developed more recently. Representing the Ancient Greek πτῐ́λον (ptĭ́lon, “feather”, “down”; “leaf”); in the botanical sense denoting leaves, probably after the French -ptile . Compare pti...

Suffix Denotes an aesthetically-oriented genre of speculative fiction based on the noun to which it is suffixed, usually involving ahistorical or anachronistic technology and its effects on society. First used when coining cyberpunk or as a back-formation to coin steampunk . Probably from the influence of the punk subculture on these genres.
Suffix Obsolete spelling of -poeia .
Suffix ( narratology ) Indicating some form of sequel Clipping of sequel
/kwiːn/, /kwɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of quinoline derivatives. Contraction of quinoline .
/ˈkwɪ.nɪl/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of quinoline derivatives used as benzodiazepine receptor full, partial, or inverse agonists. From quin(oline) +‎ -il ( of unknown origin ).
/ɹə.sɪl/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of uracil type antineoplastics. From uracil .

Suffix Alteration of -orama , used in suffixing a word ending with an r or vowel sound. Usage Note The suffix is usually appended without hyphenation, or occasionally with hyphen intended to catch the eye. A libfix, a modification of -orama , influenced by diorama and panorama , and thus indirectly derived from Ancient Greek ὅρᾱμᾰ (hórāmă, “sight, spectacle”).
Suffix Marks ordinals written in digits when the final term of the spelled number is "third"
Suffix Marks ordinals written in digits when the final term of the spelled number is "thirds"

Suffix ( no longer productive ) A suffix forming nouns of condition or state. From Middle English -rede , -red , -redde , -reden , from Old English -rǣden (which see). Cognate with German -rat (as in Heirat (“wedding”)).
/ɹəl/ Suffix pejorative suffix diminutive suffix From Middle English -rel , -erel , from Old French -erel , -erelle .
/ˈɹɛl.ɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of pituitary hormone-release stimulating peptides, especially of LHRH release–stimulating peptides. From rel(easing) +‎ -in .

Suffix (chiefly nonstandard, humorous) Used to form the plural of nouns Usage Note In Standard English, this suffix occurs only in children . Some non-standard uses can be found in dialects, such as calveren (“calves”), lambren (“lambs”), etc. In brethren and sistren , the suffix is -en , with the -r- coming from the stem of the word ( brothe r , siste r ). In standard language, a back-formation from children and brethren . In dialectal usage, from Middle English -ren , -eren (compounded...
/ɹən.əʊn/, /ɹəˌnoʊn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of spironolactone derivatives used as aldosterone antagonists. From canrenone .
/ˈɹiː.ə.taɪd/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of somatostatin receptor agonists/antagonists. From [Term?] +‎ -tide (“peptide, glycopeptide”).
/ɹəs/, /ɹɪs/ Suffix Forms feminine agent nouns. From -er +‎ -ess .
Suffix Obsolete spelling of -ress .
/ˈɹɛt.n̩/, /ˈɹɛt.ɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of retinol derivatives. From retinol .
/ɹɪ.biːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of ribofuranosyl derivatives of the pirazofurin type. From rib(osyl) +‎ -ine .

Suffix ( no longer productive ) A termination denoting jurisdiction, or a district over which government is exercised. From Northern Middle English -rike (Southern -riche ), from Old English -rīċe (“-ric”), from rīċe (“realm, domain, jurisdiction”), from Proto-Germanic *rīkiją (“realm, power”). Cognate with Dutch -rijk , German -reich .
/ˈɹɪdən/ Suffix Figuratively construed as “repeatedly/perennially affected by” or “unable to escape from”. Back-formation from bedridden .
Suffix Used to form intensified adjectives from nouns and adjectives. Probably a rebracketing of terrific . Used in the 1950s to form trademarks and words used in advertising.
Suffix Used to form intensified adjectives from nouns and adjectives. Synonym: -riffic Back-formation from terrific .
/ɹɪn.əʊn/, /ɹɪˌnoʊn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of amrinone derivatives used as cardiac stimulants. From amrinone .
/ɹaɪz/ Suffix ( astronomy ) Used to form noun from a noun corresponding to astronomical body, to indicate the rise of the astronomical body appears in the horizon.
/ɹɪ.taɪd/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of natriuretic peptides. From (nat)ri(uretic) +‎ -tide (“peptide, glycopeptide”).
/ˈɹoʊ.lɪm.əs/, /ˈɹəʊ.lɪm.əs/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of rapamycin derivatives used as immunosuppressants. From sirolimus .
/ɹə.zəʊl/, /ɹəˌzoʊl/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of imidazole-triazole derivatives used as aromatase inhibitors. From [Term?] +‎ (a)zole .

Suffix ( medicine ) Forms nouns indicating excessive discharge or haemorrhage from an organ. From Latin -rrhagia , from Ancient Greek -ραγία (-ragía), from the stem of ῥήγνυμι (rhḗgnumi, “to break, burst”).
/ɹəfi/, /ɹəfɪ/ Suffix ( surgery ) suture From Ancient Greek -ρραφία (-rrhaphía), from ῥάπτω (rháptō, “I sew”).
/ɹiə/, /ɹɪə/ Suffix ( American spelling ) flowing. From Ancient Greek ῥοία (rhoía, “flow, flux”).
Suffix rupture splitting From New Latin, from Ancient Greek ῥῆξις (rhêxis), from ῥήγνυμι (rhḗgnumi).
Suffix ( British spelling ) Obsolete spelling of -rrhoea .
/ˈɹuː.bɪs.ɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of daunorubicin derivatives used as antineoplastics. From daunorubicin .

/s/, /z/, /əz/, /ɪz/ Suffix Used to form regular plurals of nouns. Used to form many pluralia tantum (nouns that are almost or entirely without singular forms). Forms names of sciences and fields of studies from adjectives (See Usage notes) When appended to a number ending in at least one 0, expresses a range of numbers which share the digits before some or (usually) all of the 0s; frequently used for decades, centuries and temperatures. Used to form the third-person singular indicative pres...
Suffix Free from danger or problems.
/sæl/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of salicylic acid derivatives. From salicylic acid .
/sə.læn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of brominated salicylamide derivatives used as disinfectants. From -sal- (“salicylic acid derivative”) +‎ -an ( of unknown origin ).
/ˈsæl.ə.zaɪd/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of phenylazosalicylic acid derivatives. From salazo- (“phenylazosalicylic acid derivative”) +‎ -ide .
/ˈsæl.ə.ziːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of phenylazosalicylic acid derivatives. From salazo- (“phenylazosalicylic acid derivative”) +‎ -ine .
Suffix (anime and manga, fandom slang) Appended to a person's name or nickname to convey honour and respect. Borrowed from Japanese さま (-sama).

Suffix (informal, chiefly Japanophilic slang) Honorific ending used to indicate that a person is Japanese, talking with a Japanese, or treated like a Japanese. Borrowed from Japanese さん (san, “Mr, Ms”).
/ˈsɑɹ.tən/, /ˈsɑɹˌtæn/, /ˈsɑː.tæn/, /ˈsɑː.tən/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of nonpeptidic angiotensin II receptor antagonists used as antihypertensives. From sartan .

/-sɔːs/ Suffix ( slang ) Used to add emphasis to adjectives, especially those that relate to cool- or uncoolness. Usage Note Sometimes written with a space or a dash. Probably a back-formation from awesome sauce .
Suffix Misspelling of -saurus .
Suffix Misspelling of -saurus .
/ˈsɔːɹəs/ Suffix Forms names for real or imaginary dinosaurs. From translingual and New Latin forms of Ancient Greek σαῦρος (saûros, “lizard, reptile”), used in taxonomic names of various dinosaurs.

Suffix form, formation, shape scene, picture, view A specific type of space Back-formation from landscape , the suffix representing Middle Dutch -schap (“the English suffix -ship, e.g. of friendship, kinship, ownership”), from Old Dutch -skap (“-ship”), from Proto-Germanic *-skapiz (“-ship”), from *skapaz (“shape, form”). Cognate with Modern Dutch -schap (“-ship”), German -schaft (“-ship”), Swedish -skap (“-ship”), -scipe (“-ship”). More at -ship, shape.
/-skoʊp/, /-skəʊp/ Suffix Used to make terms denoting an instrument used for viewing or examination. From Ancient Greek σκοπέω (skopéō, “examine, inspect, look to or into, consider”).
Suffix Forming adjectives relating to observation or viewing. From -scope +‎ -ic .
/-skəpi/ Suffix observation, viewing From Ancient Greek σκοπέω (skopéō, “to see”).
Suffix Denoting a type of tall building. Back-formation from skyscraper .

/-d-ʒ/, /-n-s/ Suffix Forms verbs from adjectives or nouns. When attached to certain adjectives, it forms a transitive verb whose meaning is, to make (adjective) . The same construction could also be done to certain (fewer) nouns, as, bless , in which case the verb means roughly, to make bloody/sanctify . Usage Note No longer productive. From Middle English -sen (verbal ending), from Old English -sian (verbal ending), from Proto-Germanic *-isōną .
Suffix Combining with a number to indicate an object's seating capacity, such as of a vehicle, a piece of furniture, or a venue.

/ˈsɛl/ Suffix Used in forming intensive and reflexive forms of the singular personal pronouns. Usage Note In the third person, -self or -selves is attached to the pronoun's objective form ( him , them ); in the first person and second person, to its prenominal possessive form ( my , your ). The suffixes -self and -selves may be separated by an intervening word or phrase (especially own ) from the rest of the pronoun. When this occurs, self or selves stands alone as its own word, and the pronoun'...
/ˈsɛlvz/, /ˈsɛlz/ Suffix Used in forming intensive and reflexive forms of the plural personal pronouns. Usage Note See usage notes at -self.
/səˌmaɪd/, /sɪm.ʌɪd/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of furosemide derivatives used as diuretics. From furosemide .
Suffix ( forming nouns ) follow Extracted from Latin compounds such as obsequium , exsequia .
/sə.ɹɒd/, /səˌɹɑd/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of serotonin receptor antagonists and partial agonists. From sero(tonin) .
/ˈsɜː.piːn/, /ˈsɝːˌpiːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of derivatives of Rauwolfia alkaloids. From reserpine .
/sɛt/ Suffix ( astronomy ) Used to form noun from a noun corresponding to astronomical body, to indicate the astronomical body descending below the horizon.

/sə.tɹɒn/, /səˌtɹɑn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of serotonin receptor 5-HT 3 antagonists not fitting into other established groups of serotonin receptor antagonists. Blend of se(ro)t(onin) receptor +‎ antagon(ist) .

Suffix Forms a noun or adjective describing a state or style of sexuality. From sexual . Etymology treeProto-Indo-European *sek- der.Proto-Indo-European *séks-u-s der.Proto-Italic *seksus Proto-Italic *sekaō Latin secō Latin secāre ?Latin sexus Proto-Italic *-ālis Latin -ālis Latin sexuālis bor.English sexual English -sexual From sexual , borrowed from Latin sexuālis .

/ˈʃɪp/ Suffix Appended to a noun to form a new noun denoting a property or state of being, time spent in a role, or a specialised union. In particular, the way a set of social duties associated with a particular role shapes or develop's one's character. From Middle English -schipe , -shippe , from Old English -sċiepe , from Proto-West Germanic *-skapi , from Proto-Germanic *-skapiz . Compare Scots -schip , West Frisian -skip , Dutch -schap , German -schaft , Swedish -skap , Icelandic -sk...
/-ʃaɪə/, /-ʃaɪɚ/, /-ʃə/, /-ʃɚ/ Suffix A combining form used in the names of British counties. See shire .
Suffix ( informal ) Combined with a noun to indicate something cold or frozen. From icicle or popsicle .

/saɪd/ Suffix Forms adjectives describing position next to or alongside an object. Forms adjectives and adverbs describing position in relation to a dividing line or other separation. Forms nouns describing the area alongside or around an object. ( chiefly UK ) Forms proper nouns naming the conurbation around a river.
/siz/, /ziz/ Suffix A diminutive suffix used to add an element of childishness, informality, affectionateness, or levity to a word.
/ʒən/ Suffix Variant of -tion, most common in words inherited directly from Latin with an accusative singular ending in -sionem. From Latin, from words suffixed with -iō with a preceding -s- .

Suffix forming nouns of action or process ( medicine ) forming nouns of condition forming additional nouns Ultimately from Ancient Greek -σις (-sis, “forms noun of action”), often via Latin or French. Identical in meaning with Latin -entia, English -ing.

Suffix (informal, humorous) Added to a word, name, or phrase to invoke Russianness, Polishness, or a more general Slavicness. From Russian -ский (-skij, adjectival suffix), perhaps influenced by Russki , Polski , or other -ski -terminated terms such as Russian and Polish surnames. Doublet of -ish and -esque .

/-skaɪ/, /-ski/, /-skiː/ Suffix ( Internet slang ) Combined with a noun or adjective for a specific community of users or a genre of posts on the social media platform Bluesky. Clipping of Bluesky . Compare -blr , -Tok , and -Tube .
Suffix (informal, Australia, politics) An overwhelming or surprising victory by the group or individual specified. Back-formation from landslide .

Suffix (Internet slang, derogatory) Denotes something of low-quality or low-effort. (Internet slang, derogatory, sometimes proscribed) Denotes an attribute of, or association to, something which is undesirable, or is seen as desirable but should not be. Back-formation from slop (“content produced and consumed mindlessly; low-quality food; [from 2024] junk output from generative AI”).

Suffix ( uncommon ) Denoting a particular society or social circle. Usage Note Used in the names of university student societies (e.g. RockSoc for rock music), or in reference to George Orwell's Newspeak (e.g. Ingsoc ).
Suffix Used in the names of software houses. From software .
Suffix ( soil science ) Forming the names of different kinds of soil. From Latin solum (“floor, ground”).

/soʊm/, /səm/ Suffix ( forms adjectives from nouns or adjectives ) Characterized by some specific condition or quality, usually to a considerable degree. ( forms nouns from numerals ) Denoting a group with a certain number of members. ( noun combining form ) Body. ( noun combining form ) Chromosome. Plus some indeterminate fraction not amounting to the next higher round number or significant digit; and change; -odd. From Middle English -som , -sum , from Old English -sum (“same as; -some”), ...
Suffix Indicating approximation, as in a range of numbers. Synonyms: -odd, -some From something .

/sən/ Suffix Added to a stem (usually a given name or surname) to form a patronymic or matronymic surname. ( Hong Kong ) Added to a stem (usually a given name or surname) to form a given name. From Middle English -son, -eson, -esson, -essone, -essonne , from Middle English -es (“'s”) + sone (“son”).

Suffix ( fandom slang ) Added to a noun to describe a character of the noun's form, typically one used to represent oneself in fandom spaces. ( by extension ) Added to a noun to indicate a separate presentation of the self used in a certain real life context. Likely originated in the furry community with fursona . See also sona .
Suffix wisdom, knowledge, learning From Ancient Greek σοφία (sophía, “knowledge, wisdom”).

/-ˌspik/, /-ˌspiːk/ Suffix Indicates a manner of speech or writing typical of or characterized by the root term. Synonym: -ese Probably originally from Newspeak , coined by George Orwell in his book Nineteen Eighty-Four .
Suffix To look at, perceive, or observe. From Latin specio .
Suffix Forms nouns related to sperm or semen. Internationalism, from sperm +‎ -ia .

Suffix ( mathematics ) Sphere of a certain dimensionality. Spherical object. ( cytology ) A spherical colony of cells or organoid. A spherical field or sphere of influence. Ecosystem around a particular object. Global system or layer of the Earth or another planet. ( figuratively ) Particular realm or interest of a group of people, object, etc.; its spatial or virtual sphere of influence.
/spɪ.ɹəʊn/, /spɪˌɹoʊn/, /ˈspaɪˌɹoʊn/, /ˈspʌɪ.ɹəʊn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of buspirone derivatives used as anxiolytics. From buspirone .

Suffix ( slang ) A suffix combined with a descriptive adjective or noun to create a verb meaning someone who fits that description condescendingly explaining something to someone who does not fit that description (especially, something the listener has more experience of). From splain , itself from explain , following the pattern of mansplain .
Suffix exploitation of a specific demographic, person, or thing, particularly in media. From exploitation , in the sense exploitation film .

/-spoʊ/, /-spəʊ/ Suffix ( informal ) Forms words to describe content (usually on social media) intended as inspiration to achieve a certain goal. From inspo , a clipping of inspiration . Popularised by thinspo .
Suffix Related to spores; characterized by spores of a certain type; sporous.

Suffix Marks ordinals written in digits when the final term of the spelled number is "first" Excrescent suffix (adding sound but largely not changing the meaning). From Middle English -st ; see -est . From the written form of first ; see further etymology there. From -s +‎ -t of excrescent suffixes, with -s sometimes genitive.

/stæn/ Suffix Used in the names of regions in the Middle East and Central Asia which were historically influenced by Persian and Turkic cultures. ( often humorous ) Used to form fictitious or metaphoric country names, often with a connotation of it being an exotic or hostile country. (derogatory, ethnic slur) Used after European and American place names to emphasize that many Middle Easterners or Muslims reside there. Usage Note The corresponding demonym is usually formed by suffixing -i...
Suffix ( physiology ) slowing down, stopping. From stasis .

/stat/, /stæt/ Suffix Used to form nouns naming scientific instruments that act to render the prefixed element stationary or static in some respect. ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of enzyme inhibitors. The terminal element in certain names of scientific instruments, employed on the analogy of early coinages from other languages, namely heliostat (from the New Latin hēliostata , itself apparently representing an assumed Ancient Greek etymon of the form * ἡλιοστάτης (hēliostátēs), from ἥλιος ...
Suffix used in adjectives relating to nouns with the suffix -stasis

Suffix Used to form the names of statins (lipid-lowering drugs, e.g., atorvastatin, rosuvastatin). [1970s] Used to form the names of statins (inhibiting hormones, e.g., follistatin, somatostatin). [c. 1970s] Circa 1970s (appearing first in the sense for hormone names), from international scientific vocabulary reflecting New Latin roots, from Ancient Greek στάσις (stásis) (whence also stasis ) + -in .
/-staɪn/, /-stiːn/ Suffix Added to a stem to form a patronymic or matronymic surname. Usage Note Along with -berg and -witz, -stein is a common Ashkenazi suffix. Borrowed from German -stein .
/steɪn/, /stəˌin/, /stɪ.iːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of mucolytics other than bromhexine derivatives. From acetylcysteine .

Suffix ( music ) Denoting a genre of electronic dance music, especially one influenced by drum and bass. Back-formation from hardstep (“a subgenre of drum and bass music characterised by a gritty production style and urban feel”), which emerged around 1994.

Suffix Someone who is, or who is associated with, or who does something specified. (humorous, sometimes offensive) A diminutive appended to a person's name. Usage Note Relatively uncommon for agent nouns, compared to more usual -er and -or ; primarily used for single-syllable words. Also informal, particularly in contemporary productive use – compare hipster , scenester , bankster ; older terms such as barrister do not have this casual connotation, however. Sometimes used in proper names...

Suffix ( biochemistry ) Forms terms relating to binding behaviors towards enzymes and receptors. See Allostery. From Ancient Greek στερεός (stereós, “solid, three-dimensional”) + -ic . The principal derivations are allosteric and orthosteric.
/stə.ɹaɪd/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of testosterone reductase inhibitors.
/stə.ɹəʊn/, /stəˌɹoʊn/ Suffix ( biochemistry, steroids ) Steroid hormone. ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of androgens/anabolic steroids and progestational steroids. From sterol +‎ -one .
/ˈstɪɡ.miːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of acetylcholinesterase inhibitors.
Suffix Forming the names of theme-based festivals or concerts. From Woodstock, a famous American festival.

/-stə.dɑn/, /-stə.dɒn/ Suffix ( Internet ) Combined with a noun or adjective to create a word for a specific community of users or a genre of posts on the social networking service Mastodon. Synonym: Masto- Clipping of Mastodon .
Suffix Having stoma(ta). Having mouth(s). From the adjective stomatous
Suffix mouth or mouth-like opening See Ancient Greek στόμα (stóma).
Suffix A surgical procedure forming a hole for access, nutrition, or waste elimination. See stoma .
Suffix Female equivalent of -ster. From Middle English -estere .
Suffix Used to form nouns indicating a turn or twist of the indicated kind From Ancient Greek στροφή (strophḗ, “a turn, bend, twist”).
Suffix column(s); forming adjectives From Ancient Greek στῦλος (stûlos, “pillar, column”), or -στῦλος (-stûlos, “columned”).
Suffix Pertaining to styles. From -style +‎ -y (“noun suffix”), from Ancient Greek στῦλος (stûlos, “pillar”).

Suffix Suffix forming nouns or adjectives, sometimes diminutive and usually informal or jocular. Perhaps originally two distinct suffixes, one diminutive and the other adjectival; sources of both are uncertain. If so, then equivalent to -s +‎ -y .

Suffix Used to form the past tense and/or past participle of some verbs. An excrescent ending appended to words suffixed with -s . Used to form nouns from verbs of action; equivalent to -th . ( no longer productive ) Used to form verbs from nouns or adjectives (compare -ate , -ize ), frequently having a causative force, or modified from an existing verb into a frequentative verb. (African-American Vernacular, slang) Added to the end of words ending in ⟨d⟩, representing an AAVE pronun...
Suffix Related to taxis.
Suffix (slang, often ironic) Used to form adjectives denoting some type of exceptionality. Rebracketing of spectacular .
/tə.diːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of tricyclic compounds used as histamine H 1 receptor antagonists. From loratadine .
Suffix Denoting material created in part to entertain, while also serving another purpose. Back-formation from entertainment .
/tənt/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of neurokinin (tachykinin) receptor antagonists. Probably from t(achykinin) +‎ ant(agonist) .
/ˈtæf/ Suffix grave; burial From Ancient Greek τάφος (táphos, “grave”) as a final member of compounds. Doublet of tapho- .

Suffix (slang, vulgar, derogatory or offensive) Used to form words conveying an attitude of contempt or doubt over the subject's intelligence. Usage Note Usually clips the noun to which it is suffixed when it is polysyllabic, effectively making a blend of that word with retard (formerly or less often bastard ). See retard for notes about offensiveness.

Suffix (slang, derogatory, offensive, vulgar) Used to form adjectives conveying an attitude of contempt or doubt over the subject's intelligence. Usage Note When the word suffixed to is polysyllabic, the end is usually clipped as if creating a blend with retarded . See retarded and retard for notes on offensiveness. From retarded . Compare -tard . By surface analysis, -tard +‎ -ed .
Suffix (slang, sometimes sarcastic) Fantastic; used to form adjectives conveying praise or celebration. Rebracketing of fantastic .
Suffix ( furry fandom ) Added to a noun to describe a taur with the lower body of the noun's form. Clipping of centaur or from compounding of taur .
/ˈtak.sɛl/, /ˈtæk.sl̩/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of taxane derivatives used as antineoplastics. From paclitaxel .

/tiːn/ Suffix Used to form cardinal numbers from thirteen to nineteen. From Middle English -tene , from Anglian Old English -tēne (compare West Saxon -tīene ), from an inflected form of Proto-West Germanic *tehun , from Proto-Germanic *tehun (“ten”).
/ˈtɛɡ.ɹəˌvɪə/, /ˈtɛɡ.ɹəˌvɪɹ/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of HIV integrase inhibitors used as antivirals. From (in)tegra(se) +‎ -vir (“antiviral”).
Suffix Denoting an end or conclusion.
Suffix ( biology ) Ribbon-like structure; Used to form words relating the structure of chromosomes in meiosis From Ancient Greek ταινία (tainía, “ribbon, band, fillet”).
/tə.piːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of tricyclic compounds. Contraction of thiepine .
/təp.leɪz/, /təˌpleɪs/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of tissue-type plasminogen activators. From Latin tē (“the letter T (for tissue )”) +‎ pl(asminogen) +‎ -ase (“enzyme”).
/tə.ɹɒl/, /təˌɹɔl/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of phenethylamine derivatives used as bronchodilators. Probably contraction of arterenol .
/tə.ɹəʊn/, /təˌɹoʊn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of antiandrogens. From cyproterone .

/-θ/ Suffix ( no longer productive ) Used to form nouns from verbs of action. ( no longer productive except in informal coinages ) Used to form nouns of quality from adjectives. Used to form the ordinal numeral when the final term of the spelled number is not “first”, “second”, or “third”. ( mathematics ) Used to form a term denoting the ordinal numeral corresponding to the value, being a natural number, of a mathematical expression. Used to form the denominator of a fraction. ( archaic ...
Suffix tissue lining certain organs and vessels From epithelium , see there. By surface analysis, thel- +‎ -ium .
Suffix ( biology ) used to name types of animal having a specified form of body temperature mechanism heat From Ancient Greek θερμός (thermós, “warm”).
Suffix Relating to heat or temperatures. Synonym: -thermic
Suffix Relating to heat or temperatures. Synonym: -thermal Mid 19th century, from Ancient Greek θερμός (thermós, “heat”) +‎ -ic .
Suffix heat

Suffix Placed, positioned, or located in a manner or place (indicated by the first element). Synonym: -thetical Ultimately from Ancient Greek ‑θετικός (‑thetikós) (in, e.g., νομοθετικός (nomothetikós)), from θετικός (thetikós, “that can be placed”).
/ˈθaɪ.əˌzaɪd/, /ˈθʌɪ.əz.ʌɪd/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) A thiazide derivative used as a diuretic.
Suffix ( organic chemistry ) Primarily forms names of organothiophosphates used as insecticides. Extracted from shortened forms of IUPAC names containing thio- and -one .
/ˌθaɪ.oʊˈjʊɹ.əˌsɪl/, /ˌθʌɪ.əʊˈjʊə.ɹə.sɪl/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of uracil derivatives used as thyroid antagonists. From thiouracil .
Suffix ( organic chemistry ) Used to form names of pyrethroid insecticides. From pyrethrin.
Suffix Forms nouns referring to states of mind. From Ancient Greek θυμός (thumós, “spiritedness”)
/ˈtaɪ.ə.zɛm/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of diltiazem derivatives used as a calcium channel blockers. From diltiazem .
Suffix Forms adjectives from nouns ending with -sis, -o, or -a Forms adjectives from nouns ending with ‘-ism’, replacing this ending with ‘-istic’ From Ancient Greek -τικός (-tikós).

/taɪd/ Suffix Time, period (especially around an event or festival), season; often added to a festival name to indicate the period around that festival or a season beginning or ending with it. ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of peptides and glycopeptides. From Middle English -tide , -tyde , from Old English -tīd (in compounds), from tīd (“point or portion of time, due time, period, season; feast-day, canonical hour”); see tide . From peptide , glycopeptide .
/tɪ.diːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of cimetidine derivatives used as histamine H 2 receptor antagonists. From cimetidine .
Suffix Suffix used to indicate how many times something has occurred.
Suffix ( pharmacology ) Abbreviation of t(yrosine) (k)in(ase) (inh)ib(itor) .

/-ʃən/ Suffix ( no longer productive ) Used to form nouns meaning "the action of (a verb)" or "the result of (a verb)". Words ending in this suffix are almost always derived from a similar Latin word; a few ( e.g . gumption) are not derived from Latin and are unrelated to any verb. More often, -ation is used. From rebracketed Middle English -cioun , rebracketed borrowing of Old French -tion , -cion , borrowed from the stem of Latin -tiō . The Middle English -cioun became -tion in Modern Engl...

Suffix Adjective suffix denoting "full of, prone to." Rebracketed from words like fractious and presumptious , with the t from the base +‎ -ious , ultimately from Latin ‑tiōsus , which is rebracketed from -t(us) +‎ -i- +‎ -ōsus .
/ˈtaɪ.ɹəl.ɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of thyrotropin-releasing hormone analogs. From thy(rotropin) +‎ -relin (“pituitary hormone release–stimulating peptide”).
/təˌzaɪd/, /tɪz.ʌɪd/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) A chlorothiazide derivative used as a diuretic. Contraction of -thiazide .
/ˈtaɪ.zəˌlæm/, /ˈtɪz.əˌlæm/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of diazepam derivatives. Blend of thieno +‎ -azolam .
Suffix Future, later, expectant. Usage Note Attached to a wide range of nouns, including nearly any noun that denotes an acquired relationship.
/toʊ.ɪn/, /təʊ.ɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of hydantoin derivatives used as antiepileptics. From hydantoin .
/-ˌtɑk/, /-ˌtɒk/ Suffix ( Internet slang ) Combined with a noun or adjective for a specific community of users or a genre of videos on the social media platform TikTok. Clipping of TikTok .

Suffix An instrument for cutting. A section or segment. From Ancient Greek -τομον (-tomon, “that cuts”), from τέμνω (témnō, “I cut”). From Ancient Greek τομή (tomḗ), τόμος (tómos, “a cutting”), from τέμνω (témnō, “I cut”).

Suffix (mineralogy, crystallography) Describes the cleavage of minerals. From Latin -tomus , introduced by James Dwight Dana, from Ancient Greek τόμος (tómos, “slice, something cut”). Perhaps modelled on Ancient Greek ἀκρότομος (akrótomos, “cut off sharply”, adjective).
/-təmi/ Suffix Cutting, incision, section ( anatomy ) A division. ( surgery ) A surgical incision. From Ancient Greek τέμνω (témnō, “I cut”).

/tən/ Suffix Place-name suffix, originally denoting a town or enclosure of buildings. (mathematics, bridge) A set of specific objects or persons. From Middle English -ton , -tone , -tune , from Old English -tūn , derived from Old English tūn (“town”). Doublet of town .
Suffix ( medicine ) Tone, condition. From New Latin -tonia , from Ancient Greek -τονία (-tonía) (in ἀτονία (atonía)), from τόνος (tónos) + -ία (-ía). Compare French -tonie , German -tonie .
Suffix place, area or region From Ancient Greek τόπος (tópos, “place”).
Suffix Place; position; location; geographic region. Paradise. Back-formation from utopia and other words ultimately deriving from Ancient Greek τόπος (tópos, “place”).

Suffix place From Latin topica , from Ancient Greek τοπικός (topikós, “pertaining to a place, local, pertaining to a common place, or topic, topical”), from τόπος (tópos, “a place”); equivalent to -topia +‎ -ic .
Suffix A suffix meaning place, position, or localized.
Suffix To make something change its shape. From Old French tort , from Latin tortum , substantive use of the past participle of torqueō (“twist, turn”). Compare with torsion , torture .
Infix ( pharmacology ) a shortened allomorph of the monoclonal antibody affix -toxa-, used for ease of pronunciation before all source affixes but -xi- and -zu-
Infix ( pharmacology ) a monoclonal antibody that targets a toxin Irregular toxo-
/tɹəˈkɪn.ɹə/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of interleukin-4 receptor antagonists. From (te)tra- +‎ -kinra (“interleukin receptor antagonist”).
/tɹə.liːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of serotonin reuptake inhibitors. From tetralin .

Suffix Having a trema (hole or aperture) or tremata of the type, position or number specified by the prefix. From New Latin trēma (“aperture”), from Ancient Greek τρῆμα (trêma), from Proto-Hellenic *trḗmə (“hole”), from Proto-Indo-European *tréh₁-mn̥ (“to drill”), from Proto-Indo-European *terh₁- (“to drill”).
/ˈtɹɛk.seɪt/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of folic acid analogs. From methotrexate .
/tɹɪ.d͡ʒiːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of sodium channel blockers used as signal transduction modulators. Probably from tr(ansduction +‎ (s)ig(nal) +‎ -ine .

Suffix Used to form nouns referring to a surgical procedure involving the crushing, breaking, rubbing, or pulverization of a substance (especially a calculus or stone). From Ancient Greek τρῖψῐς (trîpsĭs, “rubbing, friction”) + -y , from τρίβειν (tríbein, “to rub, thresh, pound, wear out”).

/-tɹəks/, /-tɹɪks/, /-ʈɹɪks/ Suffix (dated, plural usually -trices ) Found on the end of words, chiefly female agent nouns. (BDSM, plural usually -trixes ) Forms nouns defining women who engage in specific forms of sexual dominance (by analogy with dominatrix ) Usage Note For masculine agent nouns ending in -tor , the feminine equivalent ending in -trix is the etymologically consistent form. However, -trix is relatively dated and not particularly productive in modern English, where eithe...
Suffix Ciliated band.

Suffix Used to name various electronic and neutronic devices. Used to name a number of subatomic particles. Used to name a number of particle accelerators. Used to name a number of machine learning algorithms. Borrowed from Ancient Greek -τρον (-tron), a suffix denoting an instrument, as in Ancient Greek ἄροτρον (árotron, “plow”). The word cyclotron in particular has served as a motivation for use in high-technology terms. Compare also electron (and electronic ), which is unrelated to the -τρον ...

Suffix Forms the names of types of electronic devices and systems. Also used to form the name of a number of electronics and technology companies. Formed from a rebracketing of electronics as elec + -tronics , rather than the original electron + -ic + -s ; first attested in molectronics (1959)/ moletronics (1962). Equivalent to -tron +‎ -ic +‎ -s .

Suffix ( sciences ) something that turns, affects, changes, responds, moves. From Latin tropus , from Ancient Greek τρόπος (trópos, “a turn, way, manner, style, a trope or figure of speech, a mode in music, a mode or mood in logic”), from τροπή (tropḗ, “turn; solstice; trope”). See trope .
Suffix Forming nouns denoting members of classes of organisms distinguished by trophic modes. From Ancient Greek τροφή (trophḗ, “nourishment”).
Suffix Used to form adjectives corresponding to nouns ending in -trophy From Ancient Greek τροφικός (trophikós, “pertaining to food or nourishment”), from τροφή (trophḗ, “food”).
Suffix ( sciences ) nutrition ( biology ) development From tropho- +‎ -ism .
/tɹə.fi/ Suffix nutrition growth, development From Ancient Greek τροφή (trophḗ, “food, nourishment”), from τρέφω (tréphō, “I congeal, thicken”).

Suffix (sciences, medicine) turning, movement; identical in origin to -tropy but more commonly associated with medicine, especially eye conditions. From Ancient Greek -τροπίᾱ (-tropíā), from τρόπος (trópos, “a turn”).

Suffix ( sciences ) Turning or changing. ( sciences ) Affecting or attracted to the thing specified. Borrowed from Ancient Greek τροπικός (tropikós, “of or pertaining to a turn or change; or the solstice; or a trope or figure; tropic; tropical; etc.”), from τροπή (tropḗ, “turn; solstice; trope”). Compare trope and tropic .
/ˈtɹoʊ.pɪn/, /ˈtɹəʊ.pɪn/ Suffix ( biochemistry ) Hormone. From -tropic (“attracted to or acting upon”) +‎ -in .

/-ˈtɹoʊˌpɪzəm/, /-ˈtɹəʊpɪz(ə)m/ Suffix ( sciences ) movement, turning. ( biology ) growth towards. From Latin tropus (English trope ) +‎ -ism , from Ancient Greek τρόπος (trópos, “a turn, way, manner, style, a trope or figure of speech, a mode in music, a mode or mood in logic”), from τροπή (tropḗ, “turn; solstice; trope”). Early use in botany as geotropism and heliotropism .

Suffix ( sciences ) exhibiting a behavior. ( sciences ) turning, affecting, change, response, movement. Borrowed from French -tropie , from Ancient Greek -τροπίᾱ (-tropíā), from τρόπος (trópos, “a turn”).
Infix ( pharmacology ) a shortened allomorph of the monoclonal antibody affix -tum-, used before the affixes -xi- and -zu- for ease of pronunciation
Suffix ( Internet slang ) Combined with a noun or adjective for a specific community of users or a genre of videos on the social media platform YouTube. Clipping of YouTube .
/ˈtjuː.bə/, /ˈtu.bəɹ/, /ˈtʃuː.bə/ Suffix Forms agent nouns for YouTubers or similar occupations who have the qualities of the root word. , possibly modelled after VTuber .

Infix ( pharmacology ) a monoclonal antibody that targets a tumor not covered by one of the suffixes -col-, -mel-, -mar-, -got-, -gov-, -pr-/-pro-, or that targets a combination of such tumors From tumor.
Suffix ( mathematics ) A tuple containing the specified number of terms.

Suffix ( Internet slang ) Combined with a noun or adjective to create a word for a specific community of users or a genre of posts on the social media platform Twitter. From a textspeak-style shortening of Twitter . See also twt .

/ti/ Suffix A nonproductive suffix indicating single-digit integer multiples of ten. From Middle English -ty , from Old English -tiġ (“-ty”), from Proto-Germanic *tigiwiz , plural of *teguz (“group of ten”), from Proto-Indo-European *deḱ- , *déḱm̥ (“ten”). Cognate with Scots -ty (“-ty”), West Frisian -tich (“-ty”), Dutch -tig (“-ty”), German -zig (“-ty”), Gothic 𐍄𐌹𐌲𐌿𐍃 (tigus, “group of ten, decade”). Related to ten. From Middle English -te , from Old French -te , from Latin ...

Suffix impressed form; stamp; print typical form ( biology ) Used to form words referring to groups of organisms characterized by the presence of a specific feature -like From Ancient Greek τύπος (túpos, “mark, type”), see type .
Suffix Of a certain type. See -type
Infix ( pharmacology ) a monoclonal antibody derived from a human source. From Latin -u- . From the initial vowel of human .
Suffix An anion formally derived by adding a hydride ion to a parent hydride.
Suffix Forming diminutive nouns. Usage Note The suffix is not very productive in English and most common in words directly borrowed from Latin. Borrowed from Latin -ula . Doublet of -ule .

Suffix (rare, sciences) Diminutive suffix. Synonyms: ( suffixes ) -cule, -ee, -el, -et, -ette, -icle, -ie, -kin, -le, -let, -ling, -ole, -y, ( prefixes ) mini-, micro-, nano- Derived from Latin -ula and French -ule . Etymology treeProto-Indo-European *-tlom, *-dʰlom Latin -ula der.Proto-Italic *-elos Latin -ulus der.▲Latin -ula der.Proto-Indo-European *-tlom der.Latin -ulum der.French -ule der.English -ule Derived from Latin -ula and French -ule .
Suffix Used to form the names of ketose sugars. Back-formation from levulose .

/əm/ Suffix Denotes singular grammatical number. ( chemistry ) Forms the ends of the names of certain elements (such as molybdenum and platinum). Denotes transitive verbs in the trade pidgins used between English-speakers and indigenous populations; used derogatorily by extension in English by addition to any verb, transitive or not. From the homographic case endings of the nominative, accusative, and vocative forms of numerous neuter Latin second declension nouns. Possibly from 'em .
/jəˌmæb/, /jʊm.æb/, /əm.æb/, /əˌmæb/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of monoclonal antibodies derived from a human source. From hu(man) +‎ -mab (“monoclonal antibody”).
Suffix Used in forming affectionate terms of address. Perhaps after diddums or snookums .

/-oː/, /-uːɾ/, /-ɔː/, /-ʉ(ː)ə̯/, /-ʉɹ/, /-ʊwɜ̯(ː)(ɾ)/, /-ʊə̯/, /-ʊɹ/ Suffix A process; a condition; a result of an action. An official entity or function. A tail. Inherited from Middle English -ure . Inherited from Middle English -ure , borrowed from Old French -ure , from Latin -(ā)tūram . Derived from Ancient Greek οὐρᾱ́ (ourā́, “tail”)..

Suffix (chemistry, obsolete) -ide. Coined in 1788 by translator of de Morveau, Lavoisier et al.'s 1787 book Méthode de nomenclature chimique James St. John, from Modern Latin -uretum and French -ure .
Suffix urine
Suffix A technique for working with something. Etymology treeProto-Hellenic *wérgon Ancient Greek ἔργον (érgon)bor.English -urgy Borrowed from Ancient Greek ἔργον (érgon, “work”).

/-ˈjʊəɹɪə/ Suffix ( pathology ) urine; urination; presence in urine (of a substance denoted by a prefixed combining form); state or condition of the urine. From international scientific vocabulary, reflecting a New Latin combining form, Latin -ūria , from Ancient Greek -ουρία (-ouría), from οὖρον (oûron, “urine”).
Noun ( neologism ) The phenomenon by which words are formed using the suffix -ussy . From -ussy (suffix denoting a type of pussy) +‎ -fication .

/-ˈʊsi/, /-ˈʌsi/ Suffix (Internet slang, humorous) Denoting anything thought to resemble the female genitalia (the "pussy") in some way, often referring to the genitalia of non-human creatures, usually implying that the object in question is fuckable. [from 2017] (Internet slang, by extension, humorous) Combining with the phrase put one's whole pussy into , denoting an effort of a specified kind or theme. (Internet slang, by extension, humorous) Added arbitrarily to...
Suffix ( slang ) Used to form slang forms of plural nouns.
Preposition Abbreviation of versus . Perhaps a result of the hardening of /-u(w)iən/ (to /-uviən/); compare English Peruvian to French péruvien and Italian peruviano .

Suffix (obsolete, marketing, science fiction, computing, mid-20th century) Used in the naming of computer systems and computer models, due to the use of vacuum tubes as the functional elements of computers of the time. ( marketing ) Used in the naming of vacuum cleaner models. Clipping of vacuum .
Suffix Having one or more valences. Back-formation from valence .
/ˈvæp.tən/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) A vasopressin receptor antagonist. From vaptan .
Suffix Variant with propyl side chain. Shortened 3-carbon alkyl side chain instead of pentyl (5-carbon). Shortened side chain compared to a related compound. From var(iant) +‎ -in .

/və.stæt.n̩/, /vəˈstat.ɪn/, /ˈvas.tət.ɪn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors used as antihyperlipidemic substances. Probably from vas- (“vascular”) +‎ -statin .
Adjective Abbreviation of negative . Abbreviation, using the minus sign.
/və.ɹiːn/, /və.ɹʌɪn/, /vəɹ.ɪn/, /vəˌɹin/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of spasmolytics with a papaverine-like action.

/vəːs/, /vɚs/ Suffix Forming compound nouns denoting the whole range or totality of what is indicated by the first element. [from 20th c.] Forming compound nouns denoting the fictional world of a given character, television series etc. [from 20th c.] ( fandom slang ) Forming compound nouns denoting an alternative universe. Extracted from universe .

Suffix (anatomy, pathology) A tilted position of an anatomical structure. ( ophthalmology ) A movement of both eyes. ( dentistry ) A slanted position of a tooth. ( psychology ) A personality orientation. ( medicine ) A conversion or transition. From version and related words. Compare especially the medicine and ophthalmology senses.
Suffix Used to refer to a person with a particular personality around other people. From Latin vertere (“to turn”).
Infix ( pharmacology ) a shortened allomorph of the monoclonal antibody affix -vir-, used for ease of pronunciation

/ˈvɪl/ Suffix Used to form a name of an inhabited place, a town or city. ( figurative ) Used with an adjective, noun, or gerund as a mildly intensifying locative, indicating a region or state of that kind. Usage Note Used for many towns in the English- and French-speaking world. Also often used to construct fictional and exemplar placenames, such as Nowheresville and Smallville.
/ˌvɪə/, /ˌvɪɹ/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of generic antivirals. From virus .
/ˈvɪə.ɹiːn/, /ˈvɪɹˌin/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of nonnucleoside reverse-transcriptase inhibitors (NNRTIs) used as antivirals. From -vir- (“antiviral”) +‎ -ine .
/vɪ.ɹɒk/, /vɪˌɹɑk/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of chemokine CC motif receptor 5 (CCR5) antagonists. From -vir- (“antiviral”).
/ˈvɪə.sən/, /ˈvɪɹ.sən/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of antiviral antisense oligonucleotides. From -vir- (“antiviral”) +‎ -rsen (“antisense oligonucleotide”).
Suffix Having a certain number of broods or generations in a year. From French -voltin , from Italian volta (“time, instance, occasion”) + -in .
/-vɔː(ɹ)/ Suffix An organism, usually animal, identified by its kind of diet. From Latin vorare (“to devour”).
/vəɹəs/ Suffix forming adjectives with the sense of ‘habitually eating, feeding on’ From Latin -vorus , equivalent to -vore +‎ -ous .
/vjəˌdin/, /vjʊ.diːn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of antineoplastics and antivirals of the zidovudine type From zidovudine .
Suffix ( slang ) Added to a variety of vulgar terms and insults for an obnoxious person to create new insults. From wad (“a mass; a pile”).

/wɔɹd/, /wɔːd/, /wəd/, /wɚd/ Suffix Forming adverbs denoting course or direction to, or motion or tendency toward, as in "backward", "toward", "forward", etc. Forming adjectives, as in "a backward look", "the northward road", etc; used even by speakers who usually use -wards for adverbs. Usage Note The choice between -ward and -wards is individual or dialectal; both are widely used with adverbs, though -ward is heavily favoured for adjectives. Adverbs ending in -wards (Anglo-Saxon -weardes ) and...

/wɔɹdz/, /wɔːdz/, /wədz/, /wɚdz/ Suffix Forming adjectives and adverbs denoting course or direction to, or motion or tendency toward, as in "backwards", "towards", etc. Usage Note The choice between -ward and -wards is individual or dialectal; both are widely used with adverbs, though -ward is heavily favoured for adjectives. Adverbs ending in -wards (Anglo-Saxon -weardes ) and some other adverbs, such as besides , betimes , since (earlier sithens ), etc., originated as genitive forms used adver...

/-wɛ(ə)/, /-wɛɹ/ Suffix Used to form nouns denoting, collectively, items made from a particular substance. Used to form nouns denoting, collectively, items made in a particular place. Used to form nouns denoting, collectively, items of a particular kind or for a particular use. ( computing ) Used to form mass nouns denoting specific classes of computer software, based on use, function, or method of distribution. From Middle English ware , from Old English waru , from Proto-West Germanic *war...

Suffix To coat with, or a coating of, paint of the colour specified. To focus on, or an act of focussing on, a supposedly positive aspect of an organisation in order to distract from or downplay negative perceptions. ( informal ) An overwhelming victory by a team or entity of the colour specified; often a clean sweep. Back-formation from whitewash . Second sense popularised by greenwash .
Suffix Applied to various (often very specialised) music genres, subcultures and aesthetics. Likely derived from new wave , and popularized by the terms chillwave and vaporwave .
Suffix Towards, in the direction towards. In the orientation of.
Suffix Towards, in the direction towards. In the orientation of. From way +‎ -s (adverbial suffix).

Suffix Used to form nouns denoting clothing: worn by a particular sex (e.g., menswear, womenswear) or age of person (e.g., kidswear). worn on a particular part of the body (e.g., neckwear). for a particular use (e.g., daywear). made in a particular way (e.g., knitwear) or (redundantly) of a particular kind (e.g., shoewear). Usage Note Not to be confused with -ware . From the noun wear (“clothing”).

Suffix ( in placenames ) Village; settlement; hamlet; trading centre. ( in placenames ) Brine spring; well. ( cooking ) Sandwich. From Middle English wic , from Old English wīc (“abode, dwelling-place”), an early borrowing from Latin vīcus (“village”), from Proto-Indo-European *weyḱ- (“village, household”). Latin cognate to Gothic 𐍅𐌴𐌹𐌷𐍃 (weihs), Old High German weihs (“village, settlement”), from Proto-Germanic *wīhsą (“village, settlement”) of the same Proto-Indo-European root. Cog...
Suffix Throughout the specified area or thing. See wide .

/waɪz/ Suffix In the direction or orientation of. In the manner of. In the matter of; with regard to. One at a time, or one thing at a time. Usage Note The suffix -wise is particularly productive in Indian English. See for example classwise , datewise , subjectwise . From Middle English -wis (“-wise”), from Old English -wīs (“-wise”), from Proto-West Germanic *-wīs (“-wise”), from Proto-Germanic *-wīsaz (“-wise”), from Proto-Germanic *wīsaz (“wise, skilled, knowledgeable”), related t...
Suffix Added to a stem to form a patronymic or matronymic surname. Borrowed from German -witz .

Suffix A woman who is an expert in an area. A woman who is employed or holds a position in an area. A woman who has special characteristics relating to a topic or area. A woman of a particular nationality. Usage Note To include people regardless of gender, either the suffix -person is used, or a gender-neutral hypernym, for example police officer instead of policewoman . From Middle English -woman , from the noun woman .

Suffix Denoting a craft involving a particular material, tool, or practitioner Synonym: -craft Denoting a structure or work of art crafted chiefly from a given material Denoting the structure or mechanism of a given object. From Middle English -work , -werk , from Old English -weorc , -ġeweorc , equivalent to English work . Compare also Saterland Frisian -werk , Dutch -werk , German -werk , Norwegian -verk .
Suffix Denotes a quantity corresponding to the time, value or dimension of the suffixed term. From worth .

Suffix Of sufficient worth for; deserving of. Suitable or safe for; capable of enduring or able to bear; able to withstand. Able to be; fit to be; -able. Having the right, power, permission, or freedom to. Partly from worthy (combining form), and partly continuing Middle English -wurthe (“-able”), from Old English -wierþe (“-able”), from Proto-West Germanic -*wirþī , from Proto-West Germanic *wirþī , from Proto-Germanic *wirþijaz (“worthy”), from Proto-Indo-European *wert- (“to t...
Suffix ( sports ) Abbreviation of -cross .

/ks/ Suffix Used to represent a value that may vary: see x . (chiefly US, informal) Used to replace a /ks/ sound, especially in monosyllabic words ending in -cks or -ks . An abbreviation marker. ( neologism ) Used to replace a gendered suffix. The letter x is prototypically pronounced [ks] in English; it therefore serves as a convenient shorthand for the digraphs ( cs , ks , etc.) or trigraphs ( cks etc.) that would otherwise represent that consonant cluster. From the use of ...
Infix ( neologism ) Used to replace a gendered element or reduce perceived genderedness, such as in Mixter , mxn , womxn , etc.
/ksə.bæn/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of blood coagulation factor Xa inhibitors used as antithrombotics. From factor Xa +‎ ban .
/-ksəni/, /-ksɪnɪ/ Suffix guest, stranger, host From Ancient Greek ξενία (xenía), from ξένος (xénos, “foreign, strange; foreigner, stranger, guest”).

/ksɪ.mæb/ Suffix ( pharmacology ) Used to form names of chimeric monoclonal antibodies, derived from both human and murine sources. From -xi- (alteration of chimeric ) +‎ -mab (“monoclonal antibody”), with the x reflecting both the Greek etymology and the idea of crossed/mixed cell lines. This etymology is incomplete. You can help Wiktionary by elaborating on the origins of this term.
Infix ( pharmacology ) A hybrid monoclonal antibody, derived from both chimeric and humanized sources. From -xi- +‎ -zu- .
Suffix ( Internet slang ) used in respelling words ending in -cker or -ker

/-i/ Suffix Added to nouns and adjectives to form adjectives meaning “having the quality of”, either “involving the referent” or “analogous to it”. Added to verbs to form adjectives meaning "inclined to". Forming diminutive nouns. Synonyms: ( suffixes ) -cule, -ee, -el, -et, -ette, -icle, -ie, -kin, -le, -let, -ling, -ole, -ule, ( prefixes ) mini-, micro-, nano- Forming familiar names, pet names, nicknames and terms of endearment. Added for metrical reasons to songs, often in children's music wh...

Suffix (rare, usually no longer productive) Agent suffix, equivalent to -er, appended to some nouns (especially those ending in the letter "w" or "v"). Inherited from Middle English -iere , probably from -ien (infinitival suffix) +‎ -ere (agent noun); i.e. nouns in -ere built on verbs in -ien ; possibly reinforced by Old French -ier once -ie- became a rising diphthong. More at -ier , -er .

Suffix ( organic chemistry ) A univalent radical or functional group formed from a given molecule. Thus propyl from propane, benzyl from benzene, and so forth. Via French méthylène from Ancient Greek ὕλη (húlē, “wood, material”).
Suffix Synonym of -ene ( organic chemistry ) used to form the names of bivalent radicals From ethylene and amylene , where the -yl- is etymological. Sense 2 from -yl +‎ -ene .

/ˈɪl.ɪ.diːn/ Suffix ( organic chemistry ) Bivalent radical derived especially from a saturated hydrocarbon by removal of two hydrogen atoms from the same carbon atom or the removal of the oxygen atom of an aldehyde. From -yl +‎ -idene .
Suffix ( organic chemistry ) An unsaturated hydrocarbon having at least one triple bond; an alkyne. A polymer derived from an alkyne. Contracted from the suffix -ylene of acetylene

Suffix (urban slang, lolspeak, leetspeak) Used as a substitute for -s in marking the plural of nouns. Usually used in words in which the -s suffix is actually pronounced /z/. (UK, Australia, New Zealand) Forms nicknames, especially of personal names. ( UK ) Forms colloquial variants of words. Usage Note Typically applied to words where the stressed vowel is followed by an /ɹ/, with the suffix replacing the rhotic consonant and truncating the remainder of the word. An ...
